0.review_text:
I found the core idea here pretty intuitive, and honestly a bit overdue. If you are going to use early-layer [CLS] attention as the signal for token pruning, then yes, giving the early layers some actual task pressure makes sense. In that sense the paper’s main claim landed for me quickly. The plots in Fig. 1 and later the ablations around early exits support the story reasonably well. I also appreciated that the method is conceptually simple rather than yet another complicated token scoring gadget.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates “multi-exit helps representation learning” from “multi-exit specifically improves token scoring reliability.” The argument on p. 3–4 is plausible, but the evidence feels somewhat indirect. Eq. (6) and the surrounding text describe the auxiliary loss and self-distillation, but I wanted a more direct analysis of whether the [CLS]-attention ranking actually aligns better with leave-one-out token importance or some perturbation-based metric. Right now the paper mostly shows end-task accuracy/FLOPs improvements, which is necessary but not sufficient for the mechanistic claim.

I also found some details underexplained. On p. 5, around the implementation of exits, it seems the exits are used only in training and removed at inference, but the wording is a bit slippery. If so, good, but then the exact training overhead should be reported more explicitly. Table 1 focuses on inference efficiency, which is fair, but in practice training cost matters here because you are adding multiple heads plus distillation. Maybe I missed it.

The empirical results are solid overall. Table 2 and Table 3 look competitive, especially at more aggressive reduction ratios, and that is exactly where these methods often fall apart. I liked that. Still, I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are perfectly apples-to-apples. Some baselines seem adapted from prior codebases with different schedules/augmentations, and the appendix needs to be very explicit there. In my experience, token pruning papers are extremely sensitive to recipe details.

A smaller issue: the writing is occasionally a bit too cute (“simple romance,” “newfound chemistry”). I get the intent, but for a conference paper it sometimes undercuts an otherwise technically serious contribution.

Overall I think this is a meaningful and practically useful paper, though not a huge conceptual leap. My leaning is positive, but I would want the camera-ready (or rebuttal) to clarify the causal evidence for the token-scoring story and the true training-time cost.

2.review_text:
I had to read this twice, partly because the core claim is interesting and partly because the presentation is pretty hard to follow. The idea of using TRUST-TECH to systematically traverse to multiple local optima during one training run is, at least on paper, novel for deep nets. I’ve seen a lot of “find diverse minima / cheap ensembles” papers over the years, and this one is trying to do something more deterministic than the usual cyclic LR / snapshot / mode connectivity story. I appreciate that.

That said, I’m not convinced the paper really delivers on its strongest claims. The biggest red flag is the experimental framing around “20% improvement” on CIFAR-10 and “15%” on CIFAR-100 in the abstract. Looking at Table 2 (and I think also Table 3), this seems to be relative error reduction rather than absolute accuracy gain, which is a very different thing. As written, it reads much more dramatic than it is. I find that a bit slippery.

Technically, the DSP component is also under-explained. The discussion around the dynamic search paths on p. 4–5 is intuitive, but I never felt I got a clean algorithmic understanding of what is actually guaranteed versus heuristic. Eq. (7) and the surrounding text are especially dense, and I’m not entirely sure how the method avoids just becoming a complicated perturb-and-retrain procedure. The claim that the computation graph remains constant with only minor GPU overhead is nice, but where is the actual accounting? I wanted a wall-clock and memory comparison against standard baselines, not just conceptual statements.

I also struggled with the notion of “high-quality local optimal solutions.” Are these truly distinct basins, or just nearby points found along a path induced by the same training trajectory? Figure 3 suggests some diversity, but not enough to support the stronger narrative. In my experience, ensemble gains often come from fairly mundane diversity effects, and the paper does not convincingly isolate what TRUST-TECH specifically contributes beyond generating several checkpoints with some structured perturbation.

The paper is promising, and I do think there is something here. But right now it feels oversold and under-validated. I would have liked stronger comparisons to snapshot ensembles, SWA/SWAG-style basins, and modern cheap ensemble methods, plus clearer ablations on DSP itself. My leaning is weak reject: interesting idea, but the evidence and exposition are not at top-conference level yet.

3.review_text:
I thought this was a strong paper overall, and honestly one of the more interesting “multilingual self-supervised speech” submissions I’ve read in a while. The core idea is quite simple—take wav2vec 2.0 and pretrain it jointly on raw speech from multiple languages—but the empirical results are good enough that I don’t think simplicity is a criticism here. In fact, Table 1 and Table 3 are pretty convincing. The CommonVoice gains are large, and the low-resource transfer story seems real rather than cherry-picked.

What I particularly liked is that the paper does not just report downstream ASR numbers and stop there. The analysis of shared quantized units across languages (I think this is around Figure 4 / the later analysis section) is actually useful. A lot of multilingual papers hand-wave about “shared representations,” but here there is at least some attempt to inspect whether the codebook usage aligns with language relatedness. That said, I wanted more. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t fully sure whether the observed sharing is genuinely linguistic or partly an artifact of acoustic/domain overlap in CommonVoice. I might be wrong, but the paper seems a bit too quick to interpret this as language-structure sharing.

I also found some of the comparisons slightly slippery. The “best known results” claim in the abstract is flashy, maybe too flashy. On page 5/6 (where the CommonVoice results are discussed), I wanted a cleaner accounting of supervision, model size, and data balance across baselines. Some baselines are monolingual, some multilingual, some use different decoding setups; this is all normal, but then the framing should be more careful. Otherwise it reads a little like marketing.

The methodology itself is sound. Equation (1)-style wav2vec objectives are standard by now, so the novelty is mostly in the multilingual setup and shared quantization, but I think that is enough for a conference paper. Figure 1 is clear. The BABEL results also matter because they suggest this is not only a CommonVoice effect.

One thing I was missing: more ablations on language sampling and imbalance. In my experience, multilingual training lives or dies on this. How sensitive is XLSR to high-resource languages dominating the codebook? The paper hints at this but doesn’t really answer it. Also, the single multilingual model being “competitive” with individual models is true, but from Table 4 it is not uniformly better, so I’d tone that down a bit.

Still, I came away thinking this will likely have impact. It’s practical, timely, and the gains are substantial. I would lean accept.

4.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of meta-RL submissions I’ve seen lately. The core idea—use offline reward-labeled data for meta-training, then collect unlabeled online interaction to reduce the adaptation-time distribution shift—is sensible, and in retrospect kind of obvious in a good way. The problem setup on p.2–3 is well articulated, especially the distinction between the adaptation data distribution induced by the learned exploration policy versus the static offline buffer. That is a real issue; I’ve run into related failure modes in offline RL and this paper is one of the few that states it clearly.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the method is as principled as the presentation suggests. The objective around Eq. (4) / Eq. (5) (assuming I read it correctly) seems to splice together offline reward-conditioned meta-training with an online self-supervised representation or dynamics-learning term, but I had to read that section twice. Figure 2 helps, but the algorithmic description is still a bit hand-wavy about what exactly is updated during the unlabeled online phase and what is frozen. If this is all specified in the appendix, it should really be clearer in the main text. Right now it feels like an idea paper with strong experiments.

The empirical results are good. Table 1 and the locomotion plots in Fig. 3 show a pretty striking gap over prior offline meta-RL baselines, and the claim that performance approaches online meta-RL is mostly supported. I do wish the authors were more careful here: in some environments the gap is small, in others not so small, and “matching” feels slightly overstated. Also, I might be wrong, but I did not see enough discussion of sensitivity to the amount of unlabeled online data. This is crucial to the paper’s practical pitch. If you need a huge amount of online interaction, even without rewards, that changes the story quite a bit.

Another concern is fairness of baselines. Were all prior methods tuned comparably for the offline setting? Offline meta-RL papers are notoriously brittle, and small implementation details matter a lot. The manipulation results (Table 2) are promising, but I would have liked at least one stronger ablation on the self-supervised objective itself. Is any online unlabeled data useful, or is the specific auxiliary loss essential?

Overall, I lean positive. The paper addresses a real bottleneck, and the results are stronger than I expected. But the method section needs sharpening, and some of the claims should be toned down. I’d probably accept this if the bar is novelty + practical significance, though not without reservations.

1.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit overdue. There has been a lot of excitement around Mamba-style models, but PEFT for SSMs has felt oddly underexplored compared to the endless LoRA-on-transformers literature. So I appreciate the paper’s attempt to map the space systematically rather than just proposing yet another variant.

The empirical message is fairly clear: prompt/prefix-style methods underperform on SSMs, vanilla LoRA still works, and where you place the adaptation matters a lot. I think the most useful takeaway is the negative result around tuning the SSM modules directly. Table 2 and Table 3 (if I’m reading them right) make that point pretty convincingly. In practice, this is probably what many people wanted to know. The proposed SDLoRA is also sensible rather than flashy, which I mean as a compliment.

That said, I was not fully convinced by the theoretical side. The analysis around why prefix tuning fails (around Eq. 6–10, and the discussion on p.5) felt more like an intuition dressed up as theory than a real explanatory result. I might be wrong, but the assumptions seem strong enough that the conclusion is somewhat baked in. Similarly, the argument for why LoRA should avoid SSM modules was plausible, but I don’t think it rises to the level of a satisfying theory paper contribution. It supports the experiments, yes, but doesn’t really predict much beyond them.

I also had some trouble with the scope of the benchmark. The paper says “systematic,” but the model/task coverage still felt limited. I would have liked more diversity in downstream settings, especially beyond the standard language tasks. SSMs are often motivated partly by long-context behavior, and I kept waiting for a stronger long-sequence adaptation result that really stresses the architectural distinction. Figure 3 was suggestive, but not enough. Also, after reading the experimental section twice, I was still unsure whether the hyperparameter tuning budget was truly comparable across PEFT methods; this matters a lot in papers like this.

The writing is mostly clear, though there are places where the terminology gets slippery. On p.4, the distinction between “SSM modules” and surrounding projections should be introduced more cleanly. I also thought the SDLoRA method description was a bit too compressed at first pass.

Overall, I think this is a solid empirical paper with practical value. Not groundbreaking, and the theory is weaker than the paper seems to think, but the main findings are useful and likely to hold up. I lean positive.

5.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because delayed/noisy reward plus credit assignment is a real problem, and in principle bringing preference-style supervision into the loop is a reasonable idea. The paper’s core claim, as I understand it from p.2–3, is that trajectories are bucketed into three preference levels based on delayed return, and then a causal Transformer predicts timestep-level relevance to those preferences, which is then used for credit assignment. I think the overall direction makes sense. In particular, the sepsis setting is a good choice; a lot of Mujoco-only papers claim “delayed reward” and then evaluate on toy-ish setups.

That said, I found the paper surprisingly hard to parse for what seems like a fairly simple method. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I’m still not entirely sure how the three preference categories are constructed in practice. Is this just quantile binning over returns? The wording around “the entire trajectory space” is vague. Eq. (4) and the surrounding text (I think p.4) seem to imply a probabilistic assignment, but elsewhere it sounds deterministic. This matters, because if the preference labels are derived directly from the same noisy delayed rewards the paper is trying to be robust against, then the robustness story becomes weaker than advertised.

The causal Transformer part also feels under-motivated. Figure 2 gives the architecture, but I did not see a convincing argument for why this is better than much simpler temporal models or even standard return decomposition baselines. In my experience, papers often drop in a Transformer because it looks modern, and I had that feeling here a bit. The ablations in Table 3 are not enough. I really wanted to see comparisons against a recurrent model with similar parameter count, and some sensitivity analysis on trajectory length / noise level. Without that, it’s hard to know whether the gains come from the preference formulation or just model capacity.

The experimental results are decent but not fully convincing. On Mujoco (Table 1), the margins over baselines are there, but some are modest, and I couldn’t tell whether hyperparameters were equally tuned. The sepsis experiment is potentially the most interesting part, but also the least transparent. What exactly is the reward noise process there? The setup on p.6–7 needs more detail.

Overall, I think there is a potentially useful idea here, but the current paper is underspecified and a bit overclaims robustness. I’m leaning borderline reject unless the camera-ready-level clarifications and stronger ablations are possible, because right now too many details are fuzzy.

6.review_text:
I had a somewhat mixed reaction to this paper. The basic premise — that numeric columns in tabular data may benefit from richer encodings than “just scale it and let the network sort it out” — is not crazy at all. In fact, I’m broadly sympathetic to it. People in tabular ML often under-explore representation choices. But as a conference paper, this reads much more like a library overview plus advocacy piece than a sharply formulated research contribution.

The biggest issue for me is novelty. By the time I got through the discussion of normalization, binning, clipping/noise, derivatives, and grouped “family tree” transformations (around pp. 3–5, especially the examples in Fig. 1 / Fig. 2), I still wasn’t clear what exactly is new scientifically versus what is an implementation convenience in Automunge. A package exposing many preprocessing variants is useful, sure, but usefulness is not the same thing as a publishable ML contribution. The “generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning” claimed in the abstract feels overstated. Noise injection for tabular models is not new, and I did not see a convincing theoretical or empirical reason to call this generalized or novel in a strong sense.

I also found the experimental section frustrating. Table 1 and the later results tables (I think Tables 2–4, unless I misread the numbering) report gains, but the setup is underspecified. Which datasets are used, how were hyperparameters selected, were the baselines given equal tuning budget, and how sensitive are the gains to the exact transformation family depth? I went back to reread the methodology and still had trouble pinning this down. In my experience, preprocessing-heavy papers can accidentally win by expanding search space rather than by introducing a principled encoding. That possibility is not really ruled out here.

Another thing: the paper keeps gesturing toward “unknown interpretation” numeric features. I get the motivation, but this is a very slippery framing. If semantics are unknown, some transformations will help and others will absolutely destroy signal. The paper seems too optimistic on this point. Maybe I’m being harsh, but I wanted more failure cases.

There is something potentially valuable here as a practical toolkit paper. The family-tree abstraction is kind of neat, and I can imagine practitioners using it. But the paper needs a much crisper contribution statement, stronger baselines (including modern tabular deep models and non-deep models), and more rigorous ablations. Right now I’m not convinced this clears the bar for a top ML venue. My leaning is reject, though not because the idea is useless — more because the paper has not yet made the research case.

8.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — keep activations in FP storage, but align significands on the fly before MatMul so the actual multiply-accumulate runs in integer arithmetic — is genuinely clever. I’ve seen a lot of “we get FP accuracy with integer simplicity” papers over the years, and most of them collapse under closer inspection. This one at least has a concrete mechanism. Figure 3 helped; before that, the proposal felt hand-wavy.

That said, I had to read Sections 3 and 4 twice to pin down what is actually guaranteed. The claim in the abstract about achieving “the same level of errors as FP arithmetic” is a little slippery. Equation (6) and the surrounding discussion on p.5 seem to argue not equality of outputs but rough equivalence in rounding behavior under certain conditions. That is much weaker. Maybe I’m being too picky, but the title overreaches. “Winning both” is not really established unless the hardware overhead of pre-alignment is negligible across workloads, and I don’t think Table 2 fully proves that.

On the positive side, I do think the paper is technically more serious than many hardware/quantization submissions. The authors clearly understand floating-point datapaths, and the observation that standard FP MAC is itself not exact is important and often ignored. I also appreciated the accuracy results in Table 3; the drops seem small, and unlike some papers, they did not cherry-pick only tiny models. Still, the experimental section is not fully convincing. I could not tell from p.7 whether the hardware numbers are post-layout, synthesized, or partly analytical. That matters a lot. In my experience, alignment logic and control can eat into the neat “integer simplicity” story very quickly.

Another issue: the scope is narrower than the framing suggests. This is really for FP activations + quantized weights in MatMul-heavy settings. Fine. But the introduction (lines 48–71) reads as if this is a broader replacement for FP arithmetic in DNNs, which it is not.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is novel and potentially useful, but I’m not entirely sure the paper is as complete as it wants to be. If accepted, I’d want the authors to tone down the claims and clarify the exact error-equivalence statement and hardware evaluation methodology.

9.review_text:
I had mixed feelings reading this paper. The core idea — letting agents send “peer evaluation” signals that gradually reshape each other’s rewards — is interesting, and honestly a bit more novel than a lot of the standard CTDE variants people keep repackaging. I can see why the authors are motivated: in semi-cooperative settings, the usual shared-reward story breaks down, and the paper is trying to address a real gap. That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the method is either clearly formulated enough or empirically solid enough for a top conference.

A lot hinges on the exact mechanism of reward reshaping, but I found the exposition around Eq. (3)–(6) pretty hard to follow. On page 4, the distinction between the environment reward, the peer evaluation signal, and the reshaped reward seems conceptually simple, but the actual update rule is not explained with enough care. Is the peer signal trained to predict counterfactual utility, immediate preference, or just a scalar “approval” heuristic? I might be wrong, but it reads more like a learned shaping term than a principled game-theoretic object. If so, the claims about inducing cooperation are a bit overstated.

The empirical section also left me unsatisfied. The prisoner’s dilemma example is fine as a sanity check, but by itself not very meaningful. In the larger tasks (I’m referring to the results in Table 2 and Fig. 3), the baselines feel weak and somewhat mismatched. Comparing mainly against CTDE-style methods while emphasizing that CTDE is unsuitable here is convenient. I would have liked to see stronger decentralized MARL baselines, or even simple hand-designed shaping / reciprocity heuristics. In my experience, these can be annoyingly competitive. Also, scalability is claimed repeatedly (page 2 and again near the conclusion), but I didn’t see convincing evidence beyond a modest increase in agent count.

Another issue is that the paper sometimes slides between “semi-cooperative” and “willingness to cooperate” without defining either rigorously. That sounds minor, but it matters because the whole setting depends on it. What equilibrium concept is the method targeting, if any? The discussion around non-stationarity is also a bit hand-wavy.

Still, I don’t want to be too negative. There is a seed of a good idea here, and Fig. 2 actually helped me understand the intuition better than the text did. But right now the paper feels under-theorized and under-validated. I lean weak reject. If the authors clarified the method substantially and strengthened the experiments, I could imagine being more positive.

7.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, but also narrower and less convincing than it initially sounds. The main claim is that “Decomposed Learning” mitigates or removes grokking by parameterizing linear maps as \(U\Sigma V^T\), and the experiments on modular division mod 97 do show faster generalization in several settings. Figure 3 and Figure 4 make that point visually. Still, after reading this twice, I’m not convinced the paper has really isolated a mechanism rather than introduced another form of architectural/optimization bias that happens to help on this toy task.

A lot hangs on the modular division experiments, and that’s part of my problem. The paper repeatedly hints at broader relevance to transformers (including in the abstract and again near the end, around p. 8), but everything is on one synthetic task with one modulus. In my experience, grokking results can be annoyingly sensitive to setup details, and here I wanted much more evidence that this is not just a lucky reparameterization for mod-97 arithmetic. Table 1 is promising, yes, but I really wanted multiple moduli, at least some variation in train fraction, depth, width, weight decay, and seed counts reported clearly. Maybe this is in the appendix, but in the main paper it feels underdeveloped.

I also found the method description a bit slippery. The discussion around the SVD-style factorization (I think this is Eq. 3 or thereabouts) makes it sound as if the benefit comes from “compact, generalisable representations,” but that is more of an intuition than an argument. Why should this factorization specifically encourage the right inductive bias for modular division? Low-rank structure? Better conditioning? Implicit regularization? The paper gestures at all three at different points. I’m not entirely sure the authors themselves know which story they want to tell.

There are also some fairness issues in the comparison. If the decomposed model changes parameter count and optimization geometry simultaneously, then a baseline with matched parameter budget and maybe another low-rank or factored parameterization would be necessary. Otherwise the comparison is a bit too easy. I kept thinking of prior work on low-rank adaptation / factorized linear layers; this paper did not do enough to distinguish itself from that literature beyond applying it to grokking.

That said, I do think the empirical phenomenon is real, and potentially useful. The training curves are clean, and the reduction in grokking delay is substantial. So I’m torn. As a workshop paper I’d be more positive. For a top conference main track, I think this needs either stronger mechanistic analysis or much broader empirical validation. Right now it feels like a neat observation packaged as a general method.

10.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, but also a bit slippery in the way the claims are framed. The core idea—using an uncertainty proxy (matrix entropy) to drive adaptive compression of both hidden states and KV cache—is actually pretty sensible, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to attack prefilling cost rather than only decode-time cache size. That part is real and matters in practice. In particular, the discussion around hidden-state eviction/compression on p. 3–4 is one of the more useful aspects here, since a lot of prior KV compression work ducks that issue.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates what is genuinely new from what is a somewhat elaborate heuristic package. The uncertainty estimate itself, around Eq. (5)–(7), feels under-justified. Why matrix entropy specifically, and why should it correlate with “importance” in a way that is stable across models/tasks? The intuition is hand-wavy. Fig. 2 gives a qualitative story, but I wanted a stronger validation that this signal is not just rediscovering attention sparsity or token redundancy. Maybe I missed it, but I did not see a convincing ablation where the same grouping/compression policy is driven by simpler statistics (attention mass variance, norm-based scores, random-but-layer-calibrated baselines, etc.). Table 3 is helpful, but not enough.

I also think some of the efficiency claims need more care. The abstract advertises 1.6x prefilling speedup and 1.4x inference speedup, but from Table 2 and the setup details on p. 6, it’s not obvious how much of this survives outside the specific implementation/hardware stack. Compression methods often look great until kernel overheads show up. The paper says “training-free” and “seamlessly integrated,” which honestly felt a bit marketing-ish to me.

On the positive side, the needle-in-a-haystack result is intriguing. If Fig. 4 really holds consistently, outperforming the full KV cache at 9.38% cache size is surprising enough that I’d like to believe it—but it also made me suspicious. Usually when compressed context beats full context, there is some implicit denoising effect, and then the comparison should be discussed more carefully. Is this robust across retrieval depth, distractor style, and prompt formatting? I’m not entirely sure.

Overall, I think the paper has a good practical instinct and some promising empirical results, but the methodological story feels less solid than the empirical story. Weak accept / borderline for me. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to tighten the justification for the uncertainty metric and add stronger ablations.

11.review_text:
I found this paper reasonably clear, but I’m not convinced the contribution is as strong as the framing suggests. The basic idea—learn a regression model for layer/runtime prediction on a target platform—is sensible and useful, and I’ve seen variants of this in systems/performance modeling work for years. What seems to be new here is mostly the use of a residual network for the predictor itself. I’m not against that, but the paper oversells it a bit.

After reading the method section twice, I still felt the setup was under-specified in places. On p. 4, around the discussion of the input features to ResPerfNet, I wanted a much more explicit accounting of exactly which layer attributes are included and how categorical choices are encoded. This matters a lot for whether the model is actually learning something transferable versus memorizing a narrow benchmark space. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text also move too quickly; I think the authors assume the architecture is self-explanatory, but it isn’t. Figure 2 helps somewhat, though even there I had to infer details that should have been stated plainly.

The experimental section is mixed for me. Table 2 looks good on the surface, and 8.4% MAPE on GTX 1080Ti for LeNet/AlexNet/VGG16 is respectable. But honestly, those are old models and relatively easy targets. I would have been much more impressed by results on modern architectures with depthwise convolutions, residual blocks, attention, or at least more diverse operator patterns. The claim of “variety of platforms” also felt thinner than advertised; maybe I missed something, but the platform diversity in the results tables did not seem broad enough to support the generality claims. In my experience, these predictors often break once kernel libraries, precision modes, or memory behavior shift, and I did not see enough stress-testing here.

I also think the baseline comparison is a weak point. Comparing against “previously published works” is necessary, yes, but the paper should include stronger non-deep baselines and simpler ML regressors. If a gradient-boosted tree or even a plain MLP gets close, then the residual design is not very meaningful. I’m not entirely sure the ablation on p. 6 settles this.

That said, the paper is not bad. It addresses a practical problem, and the results are directionally promising. I just think the novelty is modest and the evaluation too narrow for a top ML venue. My leaning is borderline to reject, mostly because the paper feels more like an incremental systems modeling paper than a strong ML contribution.

12.review_text:
I liked this paper more than I expected to. The core question—process supervision vs. outcome supervision for mathematical reasoning—is important, and unlike a lot of recent “reasoning” papers this one actually tries to isolate the supervision signal rather than just stacking tricks. The main empirical claim, that step-level supervision wins pretty clearly on MATH, is believable to me, and the scale of PRM800K is honestly impressive. In my experience, collecting step-level annotations at this scale is painful, so I do give the authors credit for doing real work here.

That said, I also found the paper a bit frustrating in places. The headline number (78% on the representative subset of MATH, from the abstract and later in Table 1) sounds strong, but the paper leans heavily on this subset evaluation. I understand why—they want a manageable, repeatedly-used benchmark—but it still leaves me wondering how robust the conclusions are on the full test set, or on anything outside MATH. After reading the experimental section twice, I’m still not entirely sure whether the main scientific conclusion is “process supervision is better in general” or the narrower “for this data/model/training setup, the particular PRM pipeline works better.” Those are different claims.

I did appreciate the ablations around active learning. I think the discussion around label efficiency is one of the more valuable parts of the paper. Figure 4 (if I’m remembering correctly) was especially useful in showing that not all human labels are equally valuable. But I wanted more detail on annotator agreement and what exactly counts as a “correct” intermediate step. Step-level correctness is messier than the paper sometimes admits. On pages around the reward-model setup and the rubric for labeling steps, the presentation felt a little too clean. Real chains of thought are ambiguous; equivalent algebraic transformations can look very different. How often did annotators disagree because of style vs actual mathematical error?

I also think the comparison to outcome supervision is maybe not as strong as it could be. If the outcome-supervised baseline is under-optimized, then the paper’s central takeaway weakens a lot. I might be wrong here, but I wanted either a stronger baseline or a more explicit argument that both sides got equal tuning budget.

Overall, though, I think this is a significant paper. The dataset release alone will matter, and the empirical result is important enough that I’d lean positive. Not flawless, but useful and substantive, which is more than I can say for a lot of papers in this area.

13.review_text:
I found this paper interesting in a slightly old-school way: it takes a data-mining idea (association rules) and applies it to QUBO samples, which is not something I’ve seen written down explicitly before. The basic intuition is sensible. If an annealer repeatedly returns samples where certain bits co-occur in predictable ways, maybe those regularities can be exploited to simplify the instance or at least understand what the sampler is doing. That part I buy.

That said, I struggled quite a bit with the paper as written. The presentation is rough, and in several places I was not sure whether the authors were making an empirical heuristic claim or something stronger. The abstract already has awkward phrasing (“Are there any value in these association?”), and unfortunately the paper continues in that style. Normally I wouldn’t dwell on language, but here it genuinely made technical points harder to parse.

More substantively, the core method around FARM is only partially convincing to me. On p. 4–5, where the rule-mining adaptation is introduced, I kept looking for a cleaner statement of what exactly constitutes a “transaction” and how support/confidence are defined when variables can appear as both 0 and 1 assignments. I think Eq. (3) is trying to formalize this, but I had to read that section twice. Also, the workflow in Fig. 2 is helpful at a high level, yet some important choices seem heuristic and under-justified: threshold selection, rule pruning, and especially when a rule is “safe” enough to actually fix variables and reduce the QUBO. In my experience, this is exactly where these pipelines can quietly overfit to the sampled distribution rather than reveal structure of the true optimum set.

The experimental section has some nice intuition, especially the visualizations, and Table 2 does suggest there is signal in these associations. But I’m less persuaded by the stronger claim about “closing the gap between samples and ground truth.” Is this really due to the rule-mining procedure, or just a post hoc bias toward frequent patterns in already-good samples? I might be wrong, but the causal story is not nailed down. I also wanted more baselines. Comparing against simpler variable-fixing heuristics from persistency/backbone literature would make a big difference. Right now the paper risks reinventing a weaker version of ideas that optimization folks already know.

So overall: neat idea, potentially useful tool, but the paper feels underdeveloped and somewhat overstated. I’m leaning borderline reject in its current form, though I do think there is a workshop or later conference paper hiding in here if the authors sharpen the definitions and make the evaluation much more rigorous.

14.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core claim — that “reliance on single directions” tracks generalization better than more standard unit-level interpretability quantities — is potentially important, and in places the empirical evidence is fairly compelling. In particular, the progression over label corruption levels (I think this is summarized around Fig. 2 / Table 1) is the strongest part of the paper for me. The trend seems robust enough that I don’t think this is just a cherry-picked visualization. I also liked the attempt to connect batch norm, dropout, class selectivity, and generalization in one story. That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has isolated the right mechanism.

A lot hinges on the definition of “single direction reliance,” and I found the presentation of this metric somewhat slippery. The formalism around the relevant equation (I believe Eq. 2 or 3) is not hard to parse mathematically, but conceptually it remains murky: is this really measuring causal reliance on a direction, or just sensitivity under a particular intervention? Those are not the same thing. In my experience, papers in this area often overinterpret ablation/sensitivity-style quantities, and I worry this one does too. The language in the abstract and discussion is stronger than the evidence supports.

I also had trouble with the batch normalization claim. The authors say BN “implicitly discourages” reliance on single directions, partly via reduced class selectivity. Maybe! But the evidence looked mostly correlational to me. The plots later in the paper (I think Fig. 5/6) show association, not mechanism. I might be wrong, but I wanted at least one cleaner intervention: e.g., directly manipulate selectivity while controlling architecture/optimization, or remove BN at matched train/test error and compare. As written, there are too many confounds.

Another issue is novelty. The paper positions itself against the “single interpretable neuron” literature, but the broader message — distributed representations generalize better than highly localized ones — is not exactly shocking. The contribution is therefore only as strong as the metric, and I’m not sure the metric is mature enough yet.

Still, I don’t want to be overly negative. The experiments are broad, and I appreciate that the authors looked across corruption, hyperparameters, ensembles, and training time rather than a single canned setup. That gives the paper some weight. If accepted, I’d want the claims toned down and the methodological caveats made much more explicit. Right now I’m leaning borderline reject, though not by a huge margin.

16.review_text:
I had to read this twice, and I’m still not fully convinced I understand what the actual technical contribution is beyond “we add some alignment modules and fine-tune for richer captioning.” The paper is clearly aiming at an interesting problem — expressive / detailed image captioning is underexplored compared to short factual captions — but the presentation makes the method feel more grandiose than it is.

The main issue for me is clarity. On p. 3–4, where MAM is introduced, I found the description very vague. Equation (3) looks like a modified attention score, but it is not obvious what exactly is being changed relative to standard cross-attention, and why this should specifically address “visual versus textual knowledge disparity.” That phrase appears several times, but I never felt it was operationalized. Same for TCM on p. 5: the idea of “topic control” sounds reasonable, but the mechanism seems closer to a lightweight conditioning/gating module than something genuinely topic-aware. I might be wrong, but Figure 2 actually made this more confusing rather than less, because the arrows suggest several interacting sub-networks that are not really reflected in the equations.

I also think the experimental section is weaker than it should be for a paper making fairly strong claims. Table 1 reports gains, yes, but the baselines don’t feel sufficiently competitive. If you are positioning this as a general framework for PLMs / LLMs / VLMs, then I would expect stronger comparisons against recent PEFT-style multimodal tuning methods, not just generic fine-tuning setups. The ablation in Table 3 is useful, but still incomplete: I wanted to see MAM-only, TCM-only, and especially whether the gains come mostly from extra parameters or from the proposed alignment idea. Right now that is not disentangled. The claim about “low computational cost” also needs actual numbers — training time, GPU hours, parameter counts, something concrete.

On the positive side, I do think the task motivation is good, and some of the qualitative examples (Figure 4) are genuinely better than standard short captions. There is something here. But in my experience reviewing multimodal papers, if the method is real, the paper should make it easy to pin down exactly why it works. Here it feels slippery. Too many high-level claims, not enough precise grounding.

So overall I’m leaning negative. Interesting direction, but the paper needs a cleaner technical story, stronger baselines, and much better exposition before I’d be comfortable accepting it.

17.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — extracting the “hierarchical” supervision signal that generative retrieval gets from document ID tokenization and then instantiating it with a dense index — is actually quite sensible. In fact, after reading the abstract and then the method section, I thought “yes, this is probably the right way to think about why these models sometimes work.” The claim around sibling-node contrastive training is, to me, the most valuable part of the paper. Eq. (3) and the surrounding discussion on p.4 make the intuition reasonably clear, although I had to reread that section because the notation is not especially clean.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper nails the argument empirically. The comparisons in Table 1 and Table 2 look favorable, but I kept wondering whether the generative baselines are really competitive enough by current standards. The paper cites DSI/NCI heavily, but those are now fairly established baselines, and in my experience they are quite sensitive to decoding setup, ID assignment, and corpus partitioning. I might have missed it, but I did not see enough detail on beam size / decoding constraints / how much tuning the generative systems got. If those details are buried in the appendix, they should be surfaced more clearly in the main text.

Figure 2 is helpful conceptually, though a little cartoonish. More importantly, the paper’s central claim that generative retrieval performance “lies in” sibling contrastive learning feels too strong. Maybe “is substantially explained by” would be more defensible. There are other things going on in sequence generation models besides hierarchical separation. I’m not entirely sure the ablations isolate this. The ablation around hierarchy depth (I think on p.6) is suggestive, but not decisive.

I do like the practical angle a lot: being able to add/remove documents without retraining is a real advantage, and frankly one of the main reasons pure generative retrieval has always felt awkward to deploy. This part rang true.

Presentation-wise, the writing is okay but uneven. Some claims are overconfident, and a few passages around lines 180–220 felt hand-wavy. I also would have liked more discussion of failure cases: when does HCE lose to standard dense retrieval, not just to generative methods?

Overall I lean positive because the paper has a real idea, not just an engineering tweak. But I’m hesitant. If accepted, I think it would be for the conceptual reframing and practical relevance, not because the experimental case is airtight.

15.review_text:
I found the paper reasonably clear and, more importantly, actually motivated by a real pain point. A lot of graph-based goal-conditioned RL work looks good at evaluation time because the planner rescues a weak policy, but the learning itself remains annoyingly inefficient. This paper’s core idea—distilling subgoal-conditioned behavior into a target-goal-conditioned policy—is simple, maybe even obvious in hindsight, but I mean that as a compliment. In my experience, these are often the ideas that matter.

That said, I’m a bit torn on how much is genuinely new here. The main self-imitation idea is intuitive, but after reading Sec. 3 twice I still think the conceptual novelty is somewhat incremental relative to prior hindsight relabeling / policy distillation / planner-guided RL lines of work. Eq. (4) and the training objective around it are straightforward. The “stochastically skipping executed subgoals” trick is probably the most interesting part to me, though the justification on p. 4 felt a little hand-wavy. I get the intuition, but I’m not entirely sure when skipping helps versus when it injects inconsistency into the supervision signal. Some failure analysis would have helped.

The empirical section is pretty solid overall. The gains in Table 1 and Table 2 look meaningful, especially on the longer-horizon tasks where these methods usually fall apart. Figure 3 was useful, although I wish the authors had included variance across more seeds; the shaded regions looked narrow, but I could not tell from the caption whether this was 3 seeds, 5, or more. If it is only 3, I would be cautious. Also, the comparisons seem mostly against “base + your method,” which is fine, but I wanted a stronger direct comparison to other ways of using planning during training, not only execution. Maybe I missed one in the related work section on p. 2, but it felt a bit selective.

One thing that bothered me: the claim that target-goal- and subgoal-conditioned policies “should be similar” is true only under fairly benign geometry and dynamics. In cluttered or irreversible environments, they may differ a lot. The paper acknowledges this only briefly (around the discussion near Fig. 2), and I think that limitation should be more front-and-center.

Still, I think the paper is practically valuable. Clean idea, easy to plug in, decent results. Not a huge theoretical leap, but probably useful to the community. I lean weak accept, assuming the camera-ready clarifies the novelty relative to existing imitation/distillation approaches and tightens the experimental reporting.

18.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of “interpretable RL” submissions I’ve seen lately. The core point — that concept bottleneck approaches in RL quietly assume a stream of concept labels that nobody can realistically provide online — is absolutely valid. In my experience, this is exactly where many concept-based papers fall apart when you try to imagine deploying them. So I appreciated the framing.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as clean or as general as the paper sometimes suggests. The main algorithm on p. 4–5 is sensible enough: interleave policy learning with concept model updates, use ensemble disagreement for active querying, and add the decorrelation heuristic. But the novelty feels somewhat incremental. Each ingredient is fairly standard in isolation, and the paper is really about combining them for this setting. That can still be a good conference paper, but then the empirical case needs to be extremely tight. I’m not sure it is.

A big issue for me is the evidence around “no cost to performance.” Looking at the main results tables (I assume Tables 1 and 2), I would have liked clearer variance reporting and stronger statistical comparisons to the full-label baseline. Some of the gains/losses seem small enough that seeds matter a lot. Figure 3 is suggestive, but not definitive. Also, the choice of labeling budgets (500 in some envs, 5000 in others) feels a bit hand-tuned; I wanted a more systematic budget-performance curve. Maybe this is in the appendix, but if so it should be more central.

I was also confused by the decorrelation story. The paper claims this is important, but the mechanism sounded almost too simple, and I couldn’t tell from the ablations whether it really helps consistently across environments or just in one awkward case. Equation (if I’m remembering right, around Eq. 3/4) and the surrounding text could be clearer here.

The VLM annotation section is useful, though a bit underdeveloped. I actually liked that the authors admitted it works unevenly. That felt honest.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical relevance, but not a slam dunk. My leaning is weak accept if the experimental claims are tightened, otherwise weak reject. The problem is important; I’m just not entirely sure the current evaluation fully supports the strength of the claims.

19.review_text:
I found the core idea in this paper quite reasonable, and honestly more convincing than a lot of recent “sparse decoding” submissions that just reshuffle overhead from one place to another. The observation that critical KV indices are similar across nearby queries / heads / layers is intuitive, but the authors actually seem to exploit it in a practical way rather than stopping at a correlation plot. Figure 2 and the discussion around p.4 made the paper click for me. In particular, the hierarchical sharing design is simple enough that I can imagine it surviving contact with real systems.

That said, I’m not fully sold by how complete the evaluation is. The headline numbers are strong—up to 8.6x self-attention speedup and 2.7x end-to-end throughput is definitely attention-grabbing—but I had to read Table 2 and Table 3 a couple times to understand what exactly is being compared. Some baselines seem stronger than others, and I’m not entirely sure the comparison to FlashAttention2 is the fairest framing when HShare is changing the sparsity/computation pattern rather than just improving dense attention kernels. The GPT-fast comparison on throughput is probably the more relevant one, but then the hardware/software setup needs to be crystal clear. I think this is partly described on p.7, but not cleanly enough.

I also wanted more detail on the greedy configuration algorithm. Equation (6) and the surrounding text are a bit too high level. What is the actual search cost during decoding-time adaptation? Is this amortized over long generations? If the method requires nontrivial warmup or calibration, that should be stated more bluntly. Right now it reads a little too clean. In my experience, these methods often have hidden knobs.

Accuracy retention seems decent, but I wish the paper were more candid about failure modes. On long-context retrieval-heavy tasks, does aggressive sharing across layers break in specific model families? Figure 5 suggests some degradation patterns, but the text kind of glides past them. Also, for LLaMA3-70B, I would have liked per-task variance or at least multiple seeds somewhere; the current presentation feels overly averaged.

Overall, I think this is a solid systems-ish paper with a good empirical idea and enough novelty to matter. Not a perfect paper. Some claims are a bit overpackaged, and the exposition around the algorithm could be sharper. But I’d lean positive because the method addresses a real bottleneck and seems more practically deployable than many dynamic sparse attention papers I’ve seen recently.

20.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of “agent tuning” submissions lately, and most of them are really just prompt engineering plus a benchmark story. This one is a bit better than that, but I’m still not fully convinced by the evidence as presented.

The main idea — synthesize multimodal tool-use trajectories, verify them, and then fine-tune a VLM controller — is reasonable. I think the paper’s clearest contribution is actually the data pipeline, not the agent itself. The MM-Traj dataset (20K tasks) sounds useful, and I appreciated the attempt to include both query-file verification and trajectory verification. Figure 2 lays out the pipeline fairly clearly. That said, after reading pages 3–5 twice, I still found myself unsure how much diversity is really in the generated tasks versus how much is template variation driven by GPT-4o mini. The paper says “high-quality” many times, but the evidence for quality is thinner than I wanted. Table 1 gives dataset statistics, but not enough about failure modes, deduplication, or semantic diversity.

I also had trouble understanding the training formulation. Equation (3) looks like standard supervised trajectory tuning; if I’m not missing something, the novelty is not in the optimization but in the synthesized trajectories. That’s okay, but then the paper should be more direct about that. Right now it oversells the “T3-Agent” method a bit. In my experience, naming the tuned model as a new agent can make the contribution sound larger than it is.

The benchmark results are promising but also leave questions. The claimed ~20% improvement on GTA and GAIA is substantial, and Table 3/Table 4 do show gains for MiniCPM-V-8.5B and Qwen2-VL-7B. But I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are fully fair. Were the base VLMs given the same tool specifications and prompting budget? The setup around page 6 is a little hand-wavy here. Also, GAIA is notoriously sensitive to execution details. I wanted more variance estimates, or at least multiple runs. Right now the numbers look clean in a way that makes me slightly nervous.

Another issue: the paper argues for efficient tool usage, but I didn’t see a serious efficiency analysis. Number of tool calls, latency, token cost, success-per-call, something. Figure 4 qualitatively suggests better trajectories, sure, but that’s not enough. “Efficient” in the title feels unearned.

Overall, I think this is a decent paper with a practical dataset-generation recipe and solid empirical gains, but the methodological novelty is modest and the evaluation needs more rigor. I’m leaning weak accept if the conference values useful engineering/data contributions, weak reject if the bar is algorithmic novelty.

21.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because watermark robustness has been a weak point for a while, and the basic intuition here — that the local parameter neighborhood around a watermarked model contains many “easy” watermark-removed solutions — is actually pretty plausible. In my experience, a lot of backdoor-style watermarking papers hand-wave this issue, so I appreciated that the authors are at least trying to characterize it more explicitly and then optimize against it.

That said, I came away only partially convinced. The main minimax idea is reasonable, but after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not entirely sure what is genuinely new algorithmically versus a fairly standard adversarial/robust optimization view applied in parameter space. Eq. (6) and the surrounding discussion on p.5 seem to be the core, but the optimization target feels under-motivated. Why should the particular inner maximization used here correspond well to realistic watermark removal procedures? Fine-tuning, pruning, model extraction, etc. induce very different trajectories in weight space. The paper says the neighborhood contains many watermark-removed models, but Figure 2 is more suggestive than rigorous. I wanted either a stronger geometric analysis or a more careful empirical probe.

The experimental section is decent but a bit too eager. Table 2 and Table 3 show robustness gains, yes, but I found the baselines somewhat narrow. If the claim is “robust model watermark,” then comparison against more recent robust watermarking or IP protection methods is needed, not just a handful of standard backdoor baselines. Also, on p.7, the tradeoff between clean accuracy and verification success is glossed over. The drops are not huge, but they are there, and for deployment they matter.

I also found the threat model blurry. Are we assuming the attacker does not know the watermarking scheme? Full white-box? Some sentences around lines 210–230 suggest a strong attacker, but the attacks implemented look more conventional. Maybe I’m being picky, but these details change the significance of the result a lot.

One thing I did like: the paper is fairly clear at the high level, and the intuition behind “reducing parametric vulnerability” is memorable. But the technical contribution feels incremental, and the evidence is not yet strong enough for a top conference paper, at least for me. I might be wrong, but I think this is closer to a solid workshop paper unless the authors can sharpen the novelty claim and strengthen the evaluation.

22.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of “Transformer + one extra module” submissions. The core claim — that standard self-attention mostly routes object-level/sensory features and does not explicitly process relations as first-class entities — is a useful framing. I think the discussion around this on pp. 2–4 is the strongest part of the paper. In particular, the distinction they make before Eq. (3) helped me understand what exactly is missing in vanilla attention, even if I’m not completely convinced the dichotomy is as clean in practice as the authors suggest.

The proposed Dual Attention Transformer is fairly intuitive once you get to Fig. 2, though I had to read that section twice. The relational attention equations around Eqs. (6)–(9) are a bit dense, and I think the notation overload makes the mechanism seem more novel than it maybe is. I might be wrong, but parts of it felt close in spirit to edge-aware / pairwise interaction modules people have explored before, just packaged in a cleaner Transformer-compatible form. The paper really should do a better job situating itself relative to relational networks, graph transformers, and some of the more recent “slot” or object-centric attention variants. The related work section on p. 5 felt too brisk.

Empirically, the synthetic relational tasks are convincing. The gains in Table 1 and Table 2 are substantial, and the data-efficiency story looks real rather than cherry-picked. I was less persuaded by the “real-world” results. The language modeling improvements in Table 4 are modest, and I’m not sure they justify the added architectural complexity. Same for the visual experiments: Figure 5 looks nice, but I wanted more evidence that the gains come specifically from relational processing rather than just more parameters or an additional inductive bias. The ablations in Table 3 help, but not enough. In particular, a compute-matched baseline would have been important here.

I also found some claims overstated. The abstract says the Transformer “lacks an explicit computational mechanism” for relational information; maybe, but self-attention plus MLPs can and do learn relational structure implicitly. So the paper is stronger when it argues “better bias for relational tasks” than when it implies a hard architectural deficiency.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper has a real idea, not just benchmark gaming, and the synthetic evidence is quite good. But I’m not fully sold on the breadth of the claims. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to tighten the positioning and tone down some of the grander language.

23.review_text:
I found this paper interesting but also a bit frustrating to read. The problem setting is important — fully decentralized cooperative MARL with no communication and agents “oblivious” to one another is definitely worth studying — and I appreciate that the paper tries to get actual sample-complexity guarantees rather than just another empirical method. In that sense, the stage-based V-learning idea is probably the main contribution. The intuition, as I understand it from the discussion around the algorithm and the theorem statements, is that the stage structure makes the environment effectively stationary enough for each learner to treat the others’ behavior as slowly changing. That part makes sense to me, and I think it is the right technical lever.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by how strong the main claims are relative to what is actually proved. The abstract leans hard on “decentralized” and “arbitrary number of agents, without suffering from exponential dependence on the number of agents,” but after reading the assumptions and guarantees twice, I think this is a little slippery. The dependence may not be exponential in the theorem statement, but the constants and the smoothness/team-optimality conditions seem to hide a lot. The “nearly team-optimal Nash equilibrium” result in particular felt quite conditional. I might be wrong, but it reads more like a qualified corollary than a robust main theorem.

I also found the presentation uneven. The algorithm description around the policy update/SGD subroutine is harder to parse than it should be; I kept flipping between the pseudocode and the assumptions to understand what information each agent actually uses. If this is truly oblivious learning, I wanted a cleaner statement early on of exactly what is observed at each time step. Relatedly, the relation between the V-learning update and standard Q-learning-style exploration is not explained crisply enough. Equation-level details matter here, and I felt the paper sometimes jumped from intuition to theorem without enough bridge.

On the empirical side, the simulations are fine but not especially persuasive. They corroborate the theory in a narrow sense, but I didn’t see strong evidence that the proposed method is competitive with reasonable baselines, or that the stage-based design is essential rather than just analyzable. Figure results (especially the main learning curves) looked more illustrative than diagnostic.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I respect the technical ambition. But in its current form I’m leaning weak reject: promising theory paper, not yet fully convincing, and the writing makes the contribution feel narrower than the abstract suggests.

24.review_text:
I found the paper reasonably interesting, but also a bit underdeveloped in ways that matter for a top conference. The core idea — using attention plus disentangled representation learning to separate task-relevant from nuisance visual factors for zero-shot transfer — is intuitive and, frankly, something many people in sim2real / visual RL have been circling around for a while. What matters here is whether SADALA is actually a meaningful advance beyond prior “ignore distractions” methods, and after reading this twice I’m not fully convinced.

The presentation is decent but sometimes frustratingly vague. On p. 3 around the method description, the transition from the compressed state learning stage to the joint disentangling + policy optimization stage is too hand-wavy. Eq. (2) and Eq. (4) seem to be doing most of the work, but I had trouble understanding what exactly prevents the “unimportant” branch from still leaking task information, beyond architectural hope. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted a clearer identifiability argument or at least a stronger empirical probe. Fig. 2 helps a little, though it is more conceptual than diagnostic.

The empirical section is mixed. The gains in Table 1 on Visual Cartpole are nice, but that environment is not very persuasive anymore; it is too toy-ish to support broad claims about domain adaptation. The DeepMind Lab results in Table 2 are more relevant, and there the method looks better, but I really wanted stronger baselines. Why no comparison to more recent invariant representation or data augmentation RL methods? Comparing mostly to disentanglement-based RL and domain randomization feels selective. In my experience, methods like RAD/DrQ-style visual robustness baselines can be annoyingly strong, and leaving them out weakens the case.

I also found the “attention” part somewhat overstated. From Fig. 3 and the discussion on p. 5, the masks do appear qualitatively sensible, but qualitative masks are cheap. I would have liked ablations removing the soft attention module entirely, or swapping in a simpler bottleneck, to show the attention is not just decoration. The ablation table (I assume Table 3) is helpful but still not granular enough.

That said, I do think the paper has a real signal. The cross-domain transfer setup is meaningful, and the method seems practically useful. I’m just not convinced the evidence is strong enough yet for the venue. My leaning is weak reject, though I could be persuaded if the authors clarify the objective design and strengthen the baseline/ablation story.

25.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more useful than flashy. The central claim — that quasi character-level models can be preferable in low-resource MT, but do not really solve catastrophic forgetting — feels plausible and, from my experience, also worth documenting carefully. There is a lot of folklore around character/subword tradeoffs, and this paper tries to pin some of it down.

That said, I had a somewhat mixed reaction after reading it twice. The low-resource part is generally convincing, especially where the authors vary training set size and compare against larger subword models. The trends in the experimental section (I’m referring in particular to the figures showing performance as data scales down; I think Figures 2 and 3, though I may be off by one) are pretty clear. I appreciated that the paper does not oversell the result: quasi character-level is not universally better, it just seems to have a regime where it helps. That is believable.

Where I got less convinced is the catastrophic forgetting angle, which feels bolted on. The abstract already kind of gives away the conclusion (“they do not mitigate the catastrophic forgetting problem”), and in the body this part reads more like a negative result than a fully developed contribution. Negative results are fine, to be clear, but here I wanted a sharper framing. What exactly was the hypothesis? Why should quasi character-level representations help forgetting in the first place? I might have missed it, but the motivation around that claim felt under-argued. Also, the “greater consistency between domains” statement needs to be made much more precise. Is this measured by variance across test domains, relative drop from in-domain to out-of-domain, or something else? Table 4 (if I remember correctly) hints at this, but the paper should define the metric and interpret it more carefully.

I also think some comparisons are not entirely fair. If the quasi character-level models are smaller or implicitly regularized differently, then saying they beat “large subword-level versions” is not enough; parameter count, training budget, and decoding cost all matter here. The abstract mentions computational expense, but I wanted a more explicit accounting in the main paper. Wall-clock and memory numbers would help a lot. Right now the practical story is incomplete.

Overall, I think this is a solid empirical paper with a modest but real contribution. Not groundbreaking, and the forgetting part is weaker than the low-resource study, but there is signal here. I’d lean weak accept if the experimental details are solid and reproducible; otherwise borderline.

27.review_text:
I went through this paper fairly carefully, and I’m somewhat mixed, though overall I lean positive on the empirical side and skeptical on the methodological novelty side.

The core idea — bidirectional interaction between sequence and “spatial structure” features, plus a dynamic selection mechanism for hierarchical multi-label prediction — is reasonable and, at least in the way it is packaged here, practically useful. I think the paper is strongest in the experiments. Table 2 and Table 3 show fairly consistent gains over Graph2GO, DeepGraphGO, and CFAGO, and I appreciate that the authors evaluate across different GO branches rather than cherry-picking one setting. In protein function prediction, this kind of multimodal fusion often looks good in the abstract and then collapses under proper baselines, so I was glad to see improvements that appear stable.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I still found parts of BInM and DSM under-explained. The formulation around the bidirectional mapping (I believe Eq. 6–9, unless I mixed up the numbering) reads a bit like cross-modal projection plus gating, which is not exactly new. I’m not saying the method is trivial, but the paper oversells novelty. “Bidirectional interaction” is a broad label, and I’m not entirely sure what is fundamentally different from prior multimodal attention/fusion work beyond the application domain. The DSM is also a little hand-wavy. On page 6, the text says it selects the representation “most favorable for current protein function prediction,” but operationally this seems more like learned soft weighting than actual selection. That distinction matters.

I also had concerns about the biological setup. The abstract and experimental section focus on human datasets only. That is fine for a benchmark, but then the claims should be narrower. Protein function prediction methods often behave quite differently across species and annotation density regimes. In my experience reviewing this literature, single-species gains can come from dataset-specific correlations, especially with PPI-derived features. I would have liked more discussion of possible information leakage or split strategy; I may have missed it, but the train/test protocol was not emphasized enough.

Figure 4 (the t-SNE) didn’t add much for me. Frankly, these plots are usually decorative, and this one is no exception. The Davies-Bouldin score is a bit better, but still only weakly connected to function prediction quality.

Overall, I think this is a competent paper with solid results, but the method should be framed more modestly and the experimental protocol justified more carefully. If accepted, it would be more for empirical value than conceptual breakthrough.

26.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than flashy. The core point — that for protein data, “leakage” is not a binary issue but a continuum induced by evolutionary relatedness — is absolutely right, and in my experience a lot of ML-for-biology papers still sweep this under the rug with random splits or very forgiving clustering thresholds. The argument around the failure mode of threshold-based clustering is one of the stronger parts of the paper. The discussion around giant connected components (I’m looking especially at the analysis around Fig. 2 / Fig. 3) felt very familiar from practical dataset construction headaches.

What I think the paper does well is make a fairly simple but important move: instead of insisting every point be kept, remove the highly connected/central examples so the remaining graph can actually be partitioned into meaningful train/test regimes. That idea is intuitive, and I appreciated that the authors did not oversell it as some deep theoretical breakthrough. It’s more like: here is the thing practitioners should probably have been doing already, but formalized and tested. That has value.

That said, I do have some concerns. First, the notion of “optimal” or “principled” removal is a bit stronger in the abstract than what I think is actually justified in the main text. Unless I missed something in Sec. 3, this is still a heuristic graph-pruning strategy, not an optimality result in any strong sense. I might be wrong, but Eq. (2) / the objective around the removal criterion did not fully convince me that the method is uniquely well-motivated rather than one reasonable choice among many. I wanted more ablations against simpler baselines: random removal, degree-based removal only, maybe connected-component-aware greedy pruning.

Second, I found the experimental framing slightly slippery. The paper says it enables assessment of “extreme generalization regimes,” which may be true, but after reading the results tables twice (I think Tables 1 and 2), I was not always sure whether we are learning about the splitting methodology or about representation quality. The function-prediction benchmark becomes a bit of a vehicle for both claims at once. I would have preferred a cleaner separation.

Also, there is an uncomfortable tradeoff here: if you remove many “central” proteins, are you constructing a benchmark that is clean but biologically less representative? This is touched on, but not enough. On page 6-ish (sorry, exact line unclear), the authors mention retention statistics, but I wanted a more serious discussion of selection bias induced by pruning.

Overall, I like the paper and think the problem matters a lot. The contribution is practical and the empirical message is useful. I’m just not fully sold on the strength of the methodological claim, and I think the paper would benefit from being a little more modest and a little more rigorous in justifying the pruning criterion. Still, this is a solid submission.

28.review_text:
I have somewhat mixed feelings about this paper. The general direction is sensible, and I do think the paper is asking the right question: a lot of prompt-based CIL papers have been treated as if the two-stage prompt selection + classification pipeline is obviously the right abstraction, but in practice that pipeline can be brittle. I appreciated the attempt to actually dissect where it fails instead of just adding another module and reporting one more table. The analysis around the mismatch between stage-1 confidence and stage-2 correction is, at least qualitatively, interesting. Figure 1 and the discussion around p.3–4 make a reasonable case that the second stage can actively hurt performance even when task inference is already “good enough”.

That said, I found the paper a bit messy in how it turns this diagnosis into a method. The threshold filtering idea is simple, maybe too simple, and I’m not fully convinced it is more than a calibration trick on top of prior prompt-based systems. Eq. (6) (if I’m reading it correctly) just gates whether the sample is sent to stage 2 based on stage-1 confidence, but then a lot depends on how that threshold is chosen. I did not see a satisfying discussion of sensitivity. Table 3 reports gains, yes, but I wanted a more systematic sweep, especially because in my experience confidence thresholds are notoriously dataset-dependent. The OOD gains are nice, but are they robust or just tuned?

I also struggled with the self-supervised part. On p.5 the paper argues that adding the SSL objective improves plasticity and yields a better “task identity provider”, but the mechanism is only half-explained. After reading this twice, I still don’t quite understand why the specific SSL objective used here should help incremental task discrimination rather than simply regularize features in the usual way. The unified task identity provider idea near the end is promising, but honestly it feels underdeveloped, almost like material for the next paper.

The experimental section is decent overall. The OOD setting is where the paper is strongest, and Table 4/5 are the main reason I’m positive at all. Under standard ID settings the gains seem marginal (“comparable” is accurate), so the contribution really lives or dies on the distribution-shift story. I would have liked clearer baselines against non-prompt CIL methods too; otherwise it can feel a bit inbred.

So: worthwhile empirical observations, a practical method, but not a deep methodological advance. Borderline accept for me, mostly because the paper challenges a design pattern people have been repeating uncritically.

29.review_text:
I went into this somewhat skeptical because “use a pre-trained diffusion model + a tracking module” is starting to sound like a template, but I have to admit the paper is interesting and, at least on the empirical side, fairly convincing. The main idea seems to be to repurpose the correspondence structure inside a text-to-image diffusion model for video object segmentation with arbitrary granularity, then stabilize it over time with an explicit tracker. That part came through clearly to me by around p. 3–4, especially around Fig. 2, although I had to reread the method section once because the division of labor between the diffusion features and the tracking mechanism is not explained as cleanly as it could be.

I think the strongest aspect is that the paper is trying to handle a genuinely annoying setting: not just standard VOS, but arbitrary part/object granularity from sparse visual prompts. In my experience, many papers claim generality here and then quietly evaluate on overly curated setups. This one at least makes a real effort to test across several segmentation regimes. The quantitative results in Tables 1 and 2 are good, and the visualizations in Fig. 4 / Fig. 5 are pretty compelling. Some examples are honestly impressive.

That said, I have a few reservations. First, the novelty feels a bit incremental. The paper is clearly well executed, but the core recipe—frozen generative backbone, mine correspondences, add temporal consistency—sits very close to a line of recent work. I’m not saying it’s trivial, just that the conceptual jump is modest. The authors should do a better job on p. 2 discussing exactly what is new relative to prior diffusion-feature matching papers and recent tracking-assisted VOS methods. Right now the related work discussion feels a little too eager to separate itself.

I was also not fully satisfied by the ablations. The tracker matters a lot, presumably, but Table 3 does not really unpack failure modes or sensitivity. How brittle is this to prompt quality, object scale, long occlusion, or fast motion? I might have missed it, but I did not see a serious runtime discussion either. For a method built on a text-to-image diffusion model, that matters. If this is expensive, that should be stated plainly.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, maybe not a super bold one. My leaning is positive because the problem is important and the results are strong, but I’m not entirely sure the methodological contribution is as deep as the paper sometimes implies.

30.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because systems papers for VLM training are often where a lot of practical value actually is, and in my experience many of them underspecify the setup so badly that it is impossible to tell what really helped. This submission is somewhat better than average on that front, but I still came away a bit unconvinced.

The core idea — balancing computation jointly from data, model partitioning, and recomputation/memory scheduling — is sensible. Frankly, it is also not very surprising.
