If you train heterogeneous vision-language stacks with 3D parallelism, of course you get pipeline bubbles and uneven stage loads. The paper’s contribution is really in making this work together in one framework. I think Figure 1 and the discussion around p.3 do communicate the motivation reasonably well. The reported 1.8x speedup over the InternVL-Chat open-source code is nontrivial, and Table 2 / Table 3 suggest the gains are not just on one toy setting.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I found the novelty a little slippery. The “balanced mini-batches” idea sounds like a scheduling/bin-packing heuristic based on sequence/image-token variability; the “search-based partitioning” on p.5 feels like a system tuning pass that many groups already do internally; and the adaptive recomputation policy is also fairly standard. Maybe the value is the coupling, as the authors say, but then I wanted a clearer ablation about interaction effects. Table 4 helps, but not enough. I could not really tell whether the gains are additive or whether one of the three components is doing almost all the work.

I was also confused by the terminology “omniverse computation balance.” Maybe this is just me being grumpy, but the name oversells what is basically a multi-axis load-balancing framework. Equation (7) and the surrounding text (around p.6) were especially hard to parse; I’m not entirely sure what objective is actually optimized by the search, versus what is approximated heuristically. Some pseudocode would have helped a lot.

Another issue is evaluation scope. The paper emphasizes “generalizability,” but most experiments are still clustered around InternVL-like setups. I would have liked at least one result on a substantially different VLM architecture, or some evidence that the balancing policy transfers without retuning. Also, wall-clock speedup numbers are useful, but I wanted more hardware details and variance across runs. Systems results can be annoyingly fragile.

Overall, I think this is a useful practical paper, but I’m on the fence. Good engineering, probably impactful for practitioners, but the conceptual novelty feels modest and the exposition is not always crisp. If accepted, I’d want the final version to be much clearer about exactly what is new and what is just careful integration.

31.review_text:
I read this with interest because I’ve reviewed a lot of “open-vocabulary for morphologically rich target languages” papers over the years, and most of them are either BPE with a new coat of paint or character decoders that are painful to train. This paper is more interesting than that. The latent morphology idea is sensible, and I do think the decomposition into a continuous lexical-semantic component plus approximately discrete morphosyntactic features is a good inductive bias. Figure 1 helped; without it I would have been fairly lost in the generative story.

That said, I found the presentation uneven. On p. 3 around Eq. (2)–(5), I had to read twice to understand what is actually conditioned on the source context and what is predicted autoregressively from previous target-side characters. The distinction between the latent “lemma-like” representation and the feature bundle is intuitive in prose, but less clear mathematically. I might be wrong, but the variational approximation also seems under-motivated: why this posterior family, and how sensitive is training to that choice? The discussion near the training objective (around p. 4, lines 140–170) felt too compressed for something so central.

The empirical results are promising. Table 2 and Table 3 show consistent gains over subword and character baselines, especially in the lower-resource settings, and I believe those gains are meaningful. I also appreciated that the paper tests multiple morphologically rich targets rather than just one cherry-picked language. Still, the comparisons are not quite as exhaustive as I wanted. In particular, given the claim that standard segmentation is task-agnostic, I really wanted stronger baselines with linguistically informed segmentation or more recent subword variants. The character-level baseline also matters a lot here, and I’m not entirely sure it is optimized hard enough; these models are notoriously finicky, and in my experience small architecture choices can swing results quite a bit.

My bigger concern is interpretability. The paper repeatedly suggests the discrete latent features correspond to morphosyntactic function, but the evidence is fairly indirect. The qualitative examples on p. 6 are nice, but honestly not enough. I wanted a more serious analysis: alignment with gold morphological tags, clustering purity, anything. Otherwise this risks being a nice latent-variable story that works, but not necessarily for the reasons claimed.

Overall, I think this is a solid and somewhat novel paper, with real practical value. I’m positive on it, but not wildly so. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to improve the model exposition and add stronger evidence that the latent variables are genuinely morphological rather than just useful hidden states with a convenient interpretation.

32.review_text:
I found the core idea interesting, and honestly more interesting than the paper’s terminology. “Nuisances via Negativa” is a cute title, but after reading the abstract and then Sections 2–3 twice, I was still not fully convinced the paper has a clean conceptual story. The semantics/nuisance distinction is intuitive in the cow/grass example, but in the actual benchmarks it gets slippery. On p. 3, around the paragraph introducing semantic corruption, the paper seems to assume that the augmentation removes semantic signal while preserving nuisance-related variation, but in practice many corruptions will scramble both. That matters a lot for the claim that the method “identif[ies] and adjust[s] for where nuisances drive predictions.” I think this is overstated.

That said, I do like the empirical instinct. Figure 2 made the method much easier to understand than the text did. And the results in Table 1 / Table 2 (especially on Waterbirds) are promising. In my experience, a lot of robustness papers get one visually compelling benchmark and then collapse elsewhere; here the authors at least made a reasonable attempt across vision, NLP, and medical imaging. I appreciated that. The Cardiomegaly setting is also a good stress test because shortcut features there are unfortunately very real.

But I have several concerns. First, the method seems quite dependent on choosing the “right” semantic corruption. Equation (4) and the training objective around it are generic, but the actual success appears to hinge on handcrafted augmentation design. That makes the contribution feel less like a robust principle and more like a recipe that may or may not transfer. I might be wrong, but I did not see enough ablations isolating augmentation severity/type. Figure 4 hints at sensitivity, but it is not enough. Second, the comparisons are a little hard to parse. On p. 6–7, are all baselines given the same augmentation budget / model selection protocol? I was not entirely sure. For robustness papers, tiny evaluation asymmetries can create fake gains.

I also think the paper undersells related ideas. There is a lot of overlap with invariance-via-augmentation and feature suppression work, even if the framing here is different. The novelty is moderate, not huge.

Overall, I lean positive on the empirical value, but I’m not fully sold on the mechanism or the generality. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to sharpen the claims and be more honest about how much expert knowledge is hidden in the augmentation design. Right now it is a good paper with slightly too grand a narrative.

33.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more compelling than the average “agentic RAG” submission, but I also came away with several concerns that the current draft does not really resolve.

The core idea — letting the LLM itself decide whether to continue retrieval, reformulate the query, or stop — is sensible, and the framing on p.2–3 around replacing hand-written iteration rules with learned decision-making is well motivated. I also appreciated that the method is described as natural-language interaction with the retriever rather than some opaque controller. Figure 1 helps. In principle this is a nice direction.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully clear what is genuinely novel here beyond instruction synthesis + SFT for an iterative RAG policy. The paper keeps emphasizing “autonomous” behavior, but a lot depends on how the synthetic trajectories are constructed. On p.4, the instruction synthesis pipeline seems to bake in the desired retrieval/refinement behavior quite heavily. So the autonomy claim feels overstated to me. The model is learning a controller, yes, but from teacher-generated traces that already encode the search strategy. I might be wrong, but this seems closer to imitation of a designed procedure than emergence of decision-making.

The empirical results are solid at first glance. Table 2 and Table 3 show broad gains, and I do think the six-benchmark coverage is better than many papers in this area. But I was frustrated by the baselines. Some of them feel weak or not fully optimized. In particular, comparisons against stronger recent iterative/agentic retrieval setups are either missing or under-discussed. If you are claiming “outstanding performance,” the burden is higher. Also, inference cost is not treated seriously enough. The whole pitch is reducing overhead from prompting/rules, yet Auto-RAG still does multi-turn retrieval-generation loops. Where is the wall-clock analysis? Average number of retrieval calls is not enough. This really should have been in the main paper, not buried or omitted.

I did like the analysis around adaptive iteration counts (I think Figure 4?) and the examples are reasonably interpretable. But even there, I wanted more failure cases. Some examples look cherry-picked. And the stopping criterion remains fuzzy: Eq. (3) gives a decision formulation, but I could not tell how robust this is when retrieval quality is poor.

Overall, I think there is a real paper here, and I’d lean weak accept / borderline depending on the venue bar this year. The idea is timely and the results are promising. But the paper oversells autonomy, underspecifies the data-generation procedure, and is too casual about efficiency and baseline strength.

34.review_text:
I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating. The topic is timely, and I do think the community has a tendency to make broad claims about “high-frequency adversarial perturbations” without really pinning down what is actually true during training. So I appreciated the attempt to connect empirical frequency observations with some theory. In particular, the discussion around the evolution of perturbation spectra during training (I think this is in Sec. 3, around Eq. (6)–(9)?) is pointing at something real. Figure 2 and Figure 3 were the most useful parts for me; they at least give a concrete picture of how the spectral mass shifts.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the theory is as strong as the abstract suggests. A lot seems to depend on assumptions that are either quite restrictive or not clearly justified for modern CNNs. I might be wrong, but the derivation on p. 4–5 seems to rely on a fairly idealized linearized view of the network and gradient dynamics, and then the conclusions are stated much more broadly than the setup warrants. Eq. (8) in particular felt like a big step: I could not tell whether this is an approximation, an asymptotic statement, or something exact under the stated assumptions. The writing there is honestly pretty murky.

I also had trouble with the empirical side. Table 1 shows robustness gains from low-pass filtering, but this is not yet a convincing defense story. In my experience, preprocessing defenses often look good under a limited attack setup and then collapse under adaptive attacks. I did not see a serious adaptive evaluation that accounts for the filter in the attack pipeline. If the attacker knows the defense, the gradients should just flow through the filter (or use BPDA / EOT if needed). Without that, “significantly improve robustness” is too strong, maybe misleading. This is a common problem, but still.

Another thing: the paper seems to oscillate between two claims — adversarial perturbations become more low-frequency during training, but the log-spectrum difference is more concentrated in high-frequency bands (abstract; also discussed near Fig. 4). These are not obviously contradictory, but the paper does not explain the distinction cleanly. I found this confusing.

Overall, I think there is a seed of a nice paper here, especially the attempt to formalize training-time spectral behavior, but the current version overclaims on the theory and underdelivers on robust evaluation. Borderline for me, leaning reject unless the authors can substantially tighten both the assumptions and the attack protocol.

35.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because Bayesian/domain generalization hybrids are relatively rare, and in principle this is a good direction. The paper is trying to combine variational Bayesian neural layers with an invariance objective, so that both representation learning and the classifier are “domain-invariant” while also expressing uncertainty. That sounds appealing. But after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the paper is as conceptually clean or as empirically decisive as it claims.

The main technical idea appears around the probabilistic formulation in Sec. 3, especially the variational treatment around Eq. (6)–(10). I think the authors want to say that invariance can be imposed as a prior/regularization principle across source domains, and that placing variational distributions over both feature and classifier parameters gives robustness to domain shift. Fine. But the derivation is pretty opaque. Eq. (8) in particular felt like a leap: I could not fully follow how the “domain-invariant principle” becomes the specific training objective used later. Maybe this is obvious to people already steeped in this line of work, but I found the connection between the probabilistic story and the implemented loss weaker than the prose suggests. In my experience, papers in this area often overstate the Bayesian part; here too I’m not entirely sure whether the uncertainty is doing real work or just acting as another source of regularization.

The empirical section is solid-ish but also frustrating. The results in Table 1 and Table 2 are good, yes, and on the standard DG benchmarks the method is competitive. Figure 3 is also useful in showing calibration/uncertainty behavior, although I would have liked error bars or multiple runs there. That said, the comparisons are a bit too convenient. Some baselines are older, and it is not clear to me from page 7 whether all methods use the same backbone, data augmentation, and model selection protocol. In DG this matters a lot. Small implementation choices can move numbers by more than the reported gains. I might be wrong, but several improvements look modest enough that this could be noise unless averaged over enough seeds.

I also found the ablation in Table 3 underpowered. If the claim is that Bayesian feature layers and Bayesian classifier layers both matter, then I want a cleaner disentangling of where the gain comes from. Right now it mostly shows “full model > partial model,” which is not that informative.

Overall, I think the paper has a reasonable idea and the writing is mostly competent, but the technical clarity is not where I want it to be for a top conference. My leaning is weak reject unless the authors can sharpen the derivation and make the experimental protocol much more airtight.

36.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more original than a lot of the recent “LLM + clustering” submissions. The core idea—discovering clustering criteria automatically and expressing them in language rather than requiring the user to provide them—is genuinely appealing. In my experience, most work in multiple clustering either stays in the latent-space/optimization world or assumes the semantic dimensions are already known. This paper is trying to bridge that gap, and I appreciate the ambition.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails the problem formulation. The definition of Semantic Multiple Clustering on p.3 feels a bit slippery: is the goal to find all “meaningful” partitions, the top-k most salient ones, or just some diverse set of plausible text-described criteria? Those are not the same thing. Eq. (2) and the discussion around it suggest some diversity/quality tradeoff, but I found the objective under-specified conceptually. Maybe I’m missing something, but a lot hinges on what counts as a valid discovered criterion.

I did like the benchmarks. COCO-4c and Food-4c are useful additions, and Table 1 makes the setup reasonably clear. Still, four criteria per dataset is pretty limited for a paper claiming automatic discovery at scale. It risks evaluating whether the method can recover the authors’ chosen axes rather than whether it can truly surface novel semantic structure. Figure 3 is nice qualitatively, but I wanted more failure cases. Right now the examples are a bit too clean.

My bigger concern is evaluation. On pp. 6–7, the metrics seem to reward alignment with predefined annotations, which is fine as a starting point, but somewhat at odds with the “discover novel criteria” narrative. The bias discovery and social media popularity case studies are interesting, but they read more like demos than rigorous evidence. I’m also not entirely sure how much of the gain comes from the proposed joint reasoning versus simply using a strong MLLM to generate candidate criteria and then clustering with CLIP features. The ablations in Table 3 help, but I wanted a more surgical decomposition.

One more thing: the method seems fairly prompt-sensitive, though this is only hinted at in the appendix. If true, that should be discussed more directly in the main paper. These systems can look robust until you change wording slightly.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a real idea, but the empirical story is not yet as airtight as it needs to be for a top venue. I lean weak accept / borderline, mostly on novelty and potential impact, with reservations about evaluation clarity and problem definition.

37.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit overdue as a topic. People talk a lot about uncertainty in classifiers, but for large diffusion models there is still surprisingly little that is practical. The core idea here — freezing most of a pretrained conditional diffusion model and only ensembling a small conditional subnetwork, then estimating epistemic uncertainty via PaiDEs — is sensible and, at least to me, reasonably well motivated from a systems perspective. The claim that only ~1/1000 of parameters need to be trained, with an 87% reduction in training steps, is probably the most compelling part of the paper.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story. The paper repeatedly says it “captures epistemic uncertainty,” but the evidence on ImageNet feels narrower than the framing suggests. The under-sampled class experiments are directionally encouraging (I think this is mainly in Sec. 4 and Table 1 / Fig. 3?), but I wanted stronger quantitative validation that the uncertainty is actually useful and not just correlated with class rarity. For example, can the method distinguish true distributional ambiguity from mere sample difficulty or low image quality? Right now I’m not entirely sure. In my experience reviewing uncertainty papers, this is exactly where things often look nicer than they are.

I also found the method presentation a bit slippery in places. Around the training description (page 3-4, near Eq. 4 I think), it was not immediately clear to me which components are shared across ensemble members and whether the frozen backbone induces a hard ceiling on ensemble diversity. If all members inherit the same pretrained representation, are we really measuring epistemic uncertainty of the full generative model, or only uncertainty in the conditional head? That is a pretty important distinction, and the paper kind of glides past it. Maybe I missed a sentence, but if so it should be much more explicit.

The use of PaiDEs is neat, though I would have liked more intuition for why pairwise sample distances remain reliable in such high-dimensional generated image spaces. Figure 2 helps, but only a little. Also, some comparison to simpler baselines would help: even a naive ensemble disagreement metric, or feature-space variance using CLIP/Inception embeddings, just to show PaiDE is really necessary.

Overall, I think the paper has a good idea and some real practical value, but the claims are a bit ahead of the evidence. Weak accept / borderline for me, depending on how competitive the venue is.

39.review_text:
I had a somewhat hard time with this paper. I think the core idea — generating symbolic music at the bar level using explicit feature labels and then modeling bar sequences with an LSTM — is reasonable, and honestly not crazy at all for this problem. In fact, I agree with the motivation in the abstract and around p.2 that bar-level musical characteristics are underused in a lot of generation work. That said, the paper in its current form feels underdeveloped and, at times, quite confusing.

The biggest issue for me is clarity. After reading it twice, I still was not fully sure what the exact pipeline is. My understanding is: first bars are converted into an “image-like” relational pitch representation, then a conditional GAN generates bars conditioned on feature labels/chords, and then an LSTM models valid sequences of these characterized bars. But the transitions between these stages are not explained cleanly. Figure 1 seems intended to summarize this, but it is too schematic to answer practical questions. For example, what exactly is the label space for the “feature labels”? Are these manually designed music descriptors, clustered latent labels, or something else? On p.3–4 this is described in broad terms, but not at the level I would expect for reproducibility.

I also found the novelty somewhat limited. The ingredients — cGAN for conditioned generation, LSTM for sequence modeling, symbolic music represented in piano-roll/image-like form — are all fairly standard. So the paper really stands or falls on whether the specific feature-label formulation gives a meaningful gain. Table 1/Table 2 (if I am reading them correctly) suggest some improvement, but the evaluation is weak. There is too much reliance on qualitative examples and not enough rigorous comparison to prior symbolic music systems. In my experience reviewing music generation papers, this is where many submissions fall apart. The claims about “understanding musical domain knowledge” are much stronger than what the experiments support.

Another problem is the writing. I don’t want to over-penalize language issues, but here it interferes with technical understanding. Several sentences are grammatically broken, and I was often left guessing what the authors meant. Equation definitions are also sparse; I might be wrong, but I think some notation is introduced only after being used.

Overall, I see a potentially interesting application paper here, but not yet a strong conference paper. The idea has some merit, especially the bar-characteristic angle, but the technical contribution and empirical validation both need to be sharper. Right now I lean negative.

38.review_text:
I found this paper quite interesting, and honestly more ambitious than most NAS papers I read. The core idea—learning synthetic data/curricula via a generator and differentiating through the learner’s training dynamics—is conceptually appealing, and the application to NAS is clever rather than gimmicky. After reading the abstract and then going through the method section twice, I think the paper is trying to do two things at once: introduce a broad framework (GTNs for supervised/unsupervised/RL) and also sell a practical system for NAS. In my view, the second part lands better than the first.

The technical setup around differentiating through the whole learning process is not new in spirit, but the instantiation here seems nontrivial. I liked the discussion around the meta-objective and learner unrolling (around Eq. 2–4, if I’m reading the numbering right), though I did find the exposition a bit hand-wavy at moments. For example, on the pages describing how the synthetic data generator is parameterized and how labels are produced, I wanted more precision. Figure 2 helped, but not enough. I kept asking myself: what exactly prevents collapse to bizarre shortcut signals that only work for the sampled initialization distribution? Maybe that is addressed experimentally, but the mechanism is not very convincing.

The NAS experiments are the strongest part. The results in the main comparison table (I think Table 1/2 depending on version) are genuinely impressive if they hold up: much cheaper candidate evaluation, and apparently better ranking of architectures than standard low-fidelity proxies. That said, I’m not entirely sure the fairness story is complete. The phrase “controlling for the search proposal mechanism” is doing a lot of work. In my experience, NAS comparisons can be very fragile, and I would have liked clearer ablations isolating gains from the synthetic teaching data versus gains from simply having a better-tuned proxy evaluation setup. The paper gestures at this, but not decisively.

I also think the broader claims are overstated. The abstract says GTNs are applicable to supervised, unsupervised, and RL, but the paper does not really validate that breadth in a serious way. It feels like a platform paper wrapped around one convincing application. That’s okay! But I’d rather the claims be narrower and tighter.

Overall, I lean positive. The idea is original enough, the NAS angle is useful, and the results are stronger than I expected. But the paper could be much clearer about limitations, failure modes, and what is actually learned by the teacher. Right now it’s exciting, but a little too eager to declare a general paradigm shift.

41.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea — using the non-negative rank / NMF of layer activations on batches of similar examples as a proxy for “non-linearity,” and then tying that to memorization — is novel enough that I paid attention. In particular, the empirical observation that deep-layer activations become harder to approximate in memorizing regimes is plausible, and Figure 1 (if I’m reading it right) does convey a reasonably clean separation. I’ve seen a lot of papers on memorization detection that are either just confidence-based or require access to labels/noisy labels, so this angle is refreshing.

That said, I’m not entirely convinced by the conceptual framing. The paper repeatedly says “proximity to a linear system” but the actual object being measured is the non-negative factorization rank of the activation matrix for a batch. That is not, at least not in any straightforward sense, a measure of network nonlinearity. It is a property of the representation on a specific batch. Those are different things. Around Eq. (2)–(4) this distinction gets blurred, and I think the exposition suffers for it. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t sure what theoretical claim the authors really want to make. Is this intended as a heuristic diagnostic, or is there some deeper statement about ReLU region complexity? If the latter, I don’t think the paper gets there.

I also worry the method depends heavily on the construction of “similar batches.” This seems absolutely central, but the discussion was too light (I think page 4 / lines near the experimental setup). How sensitive are the conclusions to the similarity metric, batch size, and the number of NMF components? In my experience, NMF can be annoyingly unstable, and Table 2 should really have had variance/error bars or sensitivity sweeps. Right now the early stopping claim feels stronger than the evidence supports.

The “distinct phases” story of memorization is intriguing, especially the layerwise plots in the middle of the paper, but honestly this part felt a little overinterpreted. The curves change over training, yes. Whether that implies discrete mechanistic phases is less clear.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the empirical signals seem nontrivial. But the paper overclaims theoretically and under-examines robustness. I’d lean borderline reject for a top conference, though I would not be surprised if others are more enthusiastic than I am.

40.review_text:
I like the core intuition of this paper, and honestly it is one of the more chemically sensible takes on graph masking I’ve seen in a while. The point made in the abstract—predicting a few carbons in benzene from the remaining carbons is too easy and not very informative—is exactly the kind of issue that bothers me with vanilla node masking on molecular graphs. The proposed motif-level masking seems like a natural fix.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the empirical case is as strong as the authors seem to think. The method itself is fairly straightforward: decompose into disjoint motifs, mask a whole motif, reconstruct node attributes. Fine. But the actual novelty feels incremental unless the decomposition and masking scheme are shown to be robust and genuinely better for reasons beyond “harder prediction task.” Around p. 4, where the motif decomposition is introduced, I found the description too terse. I might be wrong, but it was not clear whether the motifs are unique under the chosen chemistry toolkit/rules, and how sensitive results are to decomposition errors or alternative motif vocabularies. This matters a lot. In my experience, these chemically inspired preprocessing steps can quietly dominate the gains.

The experimental section is decent but also a bit frustrating. Table 2 shows consistent improvements on several datasets, but the margins are mostly modest, and I wanted stronger statistical evidence. Some results look within noise unless I missed the standard deviation discussion. Also, I was looking for a comparison to stronger pretraining baselines beyond plain random masking variants. If this is really about long-range dependencies, then comparisons to subgraph masking / graph-level contrastive approaches would be important. Figure 3 is intuitively nice, but more illustrative than probative.

I did appreciate the ablation on masking ratio (I think this is on p. 6 or 7), though even there the story is not entirely clean. The paper claims inter-motif structure is what is being learned, but I didn’t see a very direct analysis proving that. For instance, attention distance, probe tasks, or performance stratified by scaffold complexity would help. Equation (6) also felt standard enough that I wondered whether most of the gain comes from the masking policy alone.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar if the conference values practical molecular representation learning. But it is not a slam dunk for me. Good idea, reasonable execution, somewhat under-argued claims. My leaning is weak accept.

42.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The main claim — non-vacuous Rademacher-complexity bounds for CNNs on small-class image tasks — is definitely worth attention, because frankly a lot of the literature in this area produces bounds that are mathematically tidy and practically useless. So I appreciate the authors trying to break out of that. The contraction lemmas seem to be the real technical core here, especially the extension beyond scalar-valued Talagrand-style contraction to high-dimensional mappings with general Lipschitz activations. If that part is correct, it is probably the most valuable contribution.

That said, I struggled with the presentation. On p. 3–4, where the new lemmas are introduced, I had to read the statements more than once to understand what exactly is stronger than the classical contraction argument and under what assumptions. I think the paper oversells “extend and improve” a bit. Improve in what sense exactly — constants, dimensional dependence, applicability to vector contractions? It is there implicitly, but not cleanly articulated. Equation (6) and the discussion right after it are especially dense. I might be wrong, but I think many readers will not be able to tell what is genuinely new versus a careful repackaging of existing tools.

The use of the infinity norm on weight matrices is unusual and, in my opinion, potentially important. I liked that this gives a different lens from the now-standard spectral/Frobenius/path norm stories. But the intuition is underdeveloped. Why should practitioners care about this geometry? On p. 6, the move from the abstract complexity bound to the CNN specialization felt abrupt. Figure 1 (assuming I read it correctly) helps somewhat, but the bridge between theorem and architecture is not as transparent as it should be.

The empirical side is where I am less convinced. Table 1 shows non-vacuous bounds, yes, but only in a fairly restricted regime (“small number of classes” is doing a lot of work here). That is not fatal, but the paper should be more upfront about the limitations. In my experience, these kinds of results can be very brittle to preprocessing, margin choices, and the exact norm estimation procedure. I wanted more ablations or at least sensitivity analysis. Otherwise it risks feeling like a carefully chosen success case.

Overall, I think there is real technical substance here, and I lean positive on novelty. But the paper needs clearer exposition and a more honest accounting of scope. Borderline accept for me, mostly because the theory seems nontrivial and somewhat fresh, even if the practical significance is still pretty narrow.

43.review_text:
I found this paper genuinely thought-provoking, and also a bit frustrating in the way a lot of ambitious open-endedness papers are. The core idea — using a foundation model as a proxy for human “interestingness” — is clever and, honestly, kind of obvious in hindsight. I mean that as a compliment. The motivation on p. 2 is strong: learning progress alone absolutely does not solve the “trivial but learnable” task problem, and the examples there make the point well.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as principled as the framing suggests. The central claim seems to be that FMs “already internalize” human notions of interestingness from internet-scale data (Abstract, and again around p. 3). Maybe. But that’s a very slippery assumption. In my experience, these models often reflect salience, frequency, or stylistic familiarity more than genuine human value judgments. The distinction matters. I kept waiting for a sharper operationalization of “interestingness” versus “predictable to a language/vision model,” and I never quite got it. Eq. (2) gives the scoring function, but it still feels more like a heuristic than a model of the target concept.

I did like the empirical section more than I expected. Figure 3 is pretty convincing visually, and Table 1 shows a real gap over uniform sampling and pure learning-progress baselines. If those results are robust, they matter. But I have questions about how much is coming from the “human-interestingness” component versus just better semantic diversity. The ablation on p. 6 helps, but I wanted a nastier baseline: e.g., a diversity-only retriever, or even a hand-crafted novelty prior. Right now OMNI may just be rebranding novelty search with a foundation-model embedding.

Another issue is evaluation. The paper argues that tasks found by OMNI are more interesting, but the validation seems partly circular: the same or similar FM family defines the score and helps assess outcomes. I might be wrong here, but this felt under-addressed. A small human study, even noisy, would have gone a long way. Without it, the headline claim is stronger than the evidence.

Still, I think this is a meaningful paper. It opens an important direction, and I can imagine a lot of follow-up work building on it. I’m positive overall, though not unreservedly. My recommendation would probably be weak accept if the conference values forward-looking ideas, but I do think the authors should tone down some of the rhetoric about “models of human notions” unless they can validate that more directly.

44.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and also pretty frustrating. The core idea — connecting optimal covariate balancing to attention and then using that as a route to “causal foundation models” — is genuinely novel, or at least novel relative to most of the recent FM-for-science papers I’ve seen. The duality result is the main contribution here, and I do think there is something nontrivial in the derivation around Eq. (4)–(7). Figure 2 also helped; without it I honestly would have been a bit lost. That said, I’m not convinced the paper fully earns the very ambitious framing in the title and abstract.

On the theory side, the primal-dual connection is intriguing, but after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still think the assumptions are doing a lot of work. In particular, the move from the balancing objective to the transformer final-layer attention mechanism feels elegant algebraically, but less convincing as a statement about practical causal inference under realistic violations. I might be wrong, but the identifiability story seems underdeveloped. The paper repeatedly says “zero-shot causal inference” (e.g., page 2 and again near the start of page 5), but this is a very strong claim. Zero-shot with respect to what exactly — new covariate distributions, new treatment policies, new outcome models? Table 1 and Table 2 suggest some transfer, yes, but not enough to justify the broader rhetoric.

I also found the empirical section uneven. The synthetic experiments are useful, but the real-data evaluation left me with questions. On page 7, around the discussion of the unlabeled pretraining datasets, I wanted a much clearer account of dataset heterogeneity and whether there is any hidden overlap in support or semantics across train/test tasks. In my experience, these “foundation” claims often collapse when the held-out tasks are truly different. Figure 3 looks promising, but error bars / variance over seeds should be more consistently reported. Some baselines also seem a bit weak or under-tuned; if you are claiming to match or beat per-dataset causal methods, that comparison needs to be beyond reproach.

There are also some presentation issues. The paper is dense, and notation around the weights/queries/keys mapping is harder than it needs to be. I think Eq. (8) is where many readers will drop off. A simpler worked example would help a lot.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I appreciate the ambition. But right now the paper overclaims, and the empirical evidence does not fully support the “causal foundation model” framing. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can tighten both the claims and the experimental validation.

46.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and a bit frustrating in equal measure. The core idea—treating an unstable molecular geometry as a noisy sample and then “relaxing” it by reverse diffusion—is genuinely clever, and I do think there is novelty here beyond the usual score-based generation papers that get repackaged for chemistry. The claim in the abstract that the method trains only on stable unlabeled molecules is appealing. If that really holds in practice, it matters.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as clean conceptually as it wants to be. The time step predictor is obviously central, but on p.4 around Eq. (6)–(8) the connection between predicted diffusion time and actual geometric instability feels hand-wavy. I get the intuition, but I’m not sure the learned “pseudo energy surface” language is helping; if anything it obscures what the model is really doing statistically. In my experience, when papers start invoking energy surfaces metaphorically, it often leads to overclaiming. This paper comes close.

The empirical section is decent but also selective. Table 1 and Table 2 show competitive results, yes, but I wanted a much more careful accounting of where the method fails. Figure 3 is suggestive, though a few cherry-picked trajectories don’t tell me much. The comparison to the baseline is important, but I could not tell whether the baseline is fully optimized under the same data constraints. If the competing method benefits from unstable/labeled data and MoreRed does not, that is a fair point in MoreRed’s favor, but then the paper should be more explicit about the tradeoff: are we comparing best achievable relaxation quality, or data efficiency? Right now it sort of slides between those.

I did like the analysis of time step prediction outside molecules (the image experiment, I think on p.8 / later tables). It’s slightly random in the paper, honestly, but it supports the claim that this component is broadly useful. Still, the chemistry side needs more rigor. I was missing discussion of physically invalid outputs, sensitivity to OOD unstable inputs, and whether repeated reverse steps can drift into chemically nonsensical regions. Maybe this is in the appendix, but if so it should be in the main paper.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is fresh and potentially useful, but I’m not entirely sure the evaluation fully backs the stronger claims. Good paper, not yet a fully convincing one.

45.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and also a bit frustrating. The problem is absolutely important: a lot of the FL compression literature quietly assumes the server can decode structured sparse/quantized updates client-by-client, which is obviously at odds with secure aggregation. So I appreciate that the paper is trying to reconcile two things people too often discuss separately. In that sense the paper feels practically motivated, not just another compression trick.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced the technical contribution is as strong as the framing suggests. The basic adaptations of quantization and pruning to the secure aggregation setting are sensible, but they also feel fairly incremental. The more novel piece is Secure Indexing (introduced around Sec. 4, if I followed correctly), and that is where I wanted much more clarity. Figure 2 helps a bit, but the protocol description is still oddly high-level for something that seems to be the main idea. I might be wrong here, but I had trouble understanding exactly what information leakage changes relative to standard SecAgg, especially when the indexing structure is itself communicated compactly. The security discussion on p. 6–7 felt too hand-wavy for a systems/security-facing claim.

On the empirical side, the results are decent. Table 1 and Table 2 show strong compression numbers, and 40x with minimal degradation is definitely notable. But the evaluation is also narrower than the abstract implies. LEAF is fine, but all benchmarks are relatively standard and somewhat dated; I would have liked at least one larger modern workload or at minimum a stronger discussion of deployment realism. Also, the phrase “no meaningful loss in utility” is doing a lot of work. In some rows of Table 2 the drops are small, yes, but not always negligible depending on the application. Please just state the actual deltas more plainly.

A smaller but important point: I could not tell where the computational overhead lands. Communication is reduced, but what happens to client runtime, masking cost, and failure robustness under dropout? Eq. (3) and the surrounding text suggest additional bookkeeping that may matter a lot in cross-device FL. In my experience, these protocol-side costs are exactly where elegant compression papers go to die in production.

Overall, I lean positive on relevance, but only weakly. The paper has a good premise and some useful empirical evidence, yet the novelty/security story feels underdeveloped. If accepted, it should be because the community needs this problem addressed, not because the paper fully nails it.

47.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantial than a lot of recent “inversion for X model” submissions that are mostly engineering. The core idea—casting RF inversion through an optimal control / LQR lens and then showing equivalence to a rectified SDE—is clever, and at least to me it feels like a real conceptual contribution rather than just another sampler tweak. The discussion around the controlled dynamics on p. 3–4 and the derivation around Eq. (6)–(10) are the heart of the paper. I had to read that section twice, but it does hang together.

What I liked most is that the paper does not stop at theory. The Flux extension makes the work relevant, and the editing results in Fig. 1 and Fig. 5 are quite compelling visually. Table 1 and Table 2 also look strong. If I’m reading them correctly, the gains are not just tiny metric bumps but noticeable improvements over both diffusion inversion baselines and prior RF-style approaches. The human study is also a plus; in my experience these editing papers often hide behind LPIPS and call it a day.

That said, I do have some reservations. First, the presentation is harder than it needs to be. The jump from the deterministic RF ODE to the stochastic formulation is not very intuitive on first pass. Around line 180-ish (I may be off by a few lines), the paper starts asserting equivalences that are mathematically plausible, but the assumptions are not stated crisply enough. I’m not entirely sure about the scope of the theorem: is this exact only under the quadratic cost / linearized dynamics setup, or is the claim stronger? The wording sometimes overreaches.

Second, some of the experimental comparisons feel a bit selective. I would have liked a clearer compute accounting. The paper criticizes test-time optimization methods for cost, fair enough, but then the proposed method introduces stochastic sampling machinery whose runtime tradeoff is not fully transparent. Please put wall-clock numbers somewhere obvious. Also, the editing benchmarks are visually persuasive, but I wanted more failure cases. The examples shown are almost too clean.

A smaller issue: Fig. 3 helped, but Fig. 2 was oddly hard to parse. And I wish the caption of Table 3 explained the protocol more explicitly.

Overall, I think this is a strong paper. Not perfect, a bit dense, and maybe slightly overclaimed in places, but novel and likely impactful. I would lean accept.

48.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and I do think there is a real idea here, but I’m not fully convinced by the presentation or by how broadly the claims are supported.

The core intuition — that the discriminator should use its capacity in a way that yields diverse gradients to the generator — makes sense to me, and I liked the attempt to operationalize this via binary activation patterns in ReLU networks. The BRE regularizer itself is pretty simple, which is a plus. If I understood Eq. (4) and the discussion around p. 3 correctly, the method is basically encouraging marginal balance and low pairwise correlation of activation bits, as a proxy for higher joint entropy. That is clever. Also, Fig. 1 does a decent job motivating why partitioning the space more finely could help G avoid getting stuck with poor discriminator extrapolations. This part felt more insightful than a lot of GAN regularization papers I’ve read.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still found the argument a bit hand-wavy in places. The jump from “higher entropy in internal binary codes” to “better learning signal for G” is plausible, but not established very rigorously. I might be wrong, but the paper seems to lean heavily on intuition here. The approximation of joint entropy using only marginal and pairwise terms also seems potentially weak; I wanted more discussion of when this is a good surrogate and when it is not. The text around Eq. (7)–(9) moved too fast for me.

The experiments are encouraging but a little uneven. The synthetic examples are nice, though somewhat expected. On the real-data side, the improvements in the tables (especially Table 1 / semi-supervised results) are meaningful, but it is hard to disentangle whether BRE is helping because of the proposed “capacity allocation” story or simply because it acts as another stabilizing regularizer on D. In my experience, GAN papers often over-interpret this kind of result. I also would have liked stronger ablations: which layer(s) get regularized, sensitivity to the coefficient, effect with/without the “generated samples near real data” heuristic mentioned around p. 4, etc. Figure 3 is suggestive, but not enough.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a novel angle, and I appreciate that it tries to reason about discriminator geometry rather than just throwing tricks at GAN training. But the theory is looser than the paper seems to think, and the empirical section, while positive, does not completely nail down the mechanism. Borderline accept for me, leaning positive.

49.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit more practically useful than many continual pretraining papers I’ve seen lately. The core idea — add experts to an MoE model and “chill” the existing parameters with a lower LR — is simple, maybe even obvious in hindsight, but that’s not a bad thing. In fact I think the simplicity is part of the appeal. The empirical study on forgetting across model size / data size / replay is also helpful. I appreciated that the paper is not overselling a fancy algorithm.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper isolates why the method works. The main proposal seems to combine two interventions at once: increasing capacity and changing optimization dynamics. From Table 2 and the discussion around Fig. 3 (if I’m reading it correctly), it looks like both matter, but I wanted a cleaner ablation: add experts without chilling, chill without adding experts, maybe also freeze router vs. not freeze router. Right now the causal story is a bit hand-wavy. Eq. (4) gives the LR scaling, but I’m not sure why this specific schedule is justified beyond “it works”.

I also found the evaluation somewhat narrow. The forgetting metrics are reasonable, but the “new domain learning” side is easier to improve if you just add capacity. So the comparison should really include a parameter-matched dense expansion or standard MoE expansion baseline. Otherwise the method gets some free lunch. I might be wrong, but I didn’t see a fully fair compute/parameter accounting in Table 1. The paper talks a lot about avoiding retraining cost, but the added experts are not free, and serving-time implications matter for MoEs.

Some presentation issues also got in the way. On p.5, the explanation of replay setup is too compressed; I had to jump back from the experiments section to understand what exact data mixture was used. Fig. 2 is conceptually nice but the caption is vague. And around lines 210–230, the distinction between “forgetting” and “distribution specialization” felt slippery. In my experience reviewing adaptation papers, this distinction matters a lot because perplexity regressions on old domains can reflect a routing shift rather than true catastrophic forgetting.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with real practical value, but not quite a slam dunk scientifically. I lean positive because the results seem useful and the idea is deployable, yet I wanted sharper ablations and a fairer baseline set before being fully enthusiastic.

50.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because single-source cross-dataset ReID is actually a setting I think is more realistic than a lot of the standard DG framing, and the “heterogeneous” part (class spaces not matching) is a real issue that people often hand-wave away. So I appreciate the problem setup. That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as novel or as solid as it wants to be.

The core idea — jointly training a feature extractor with a domain adversarial branch and a class adversarial branch, then modulating perturbation strength with some diversity-based factor — is reasonable, but after reading Sections 3.2 and 3.3 twice, I still found the method description a bit slippery. In particular, Eq. (6) and Eq. (7) introduce the perturbation-related weighting, but I had trouble understanding whether this factor is computed per mini-batch, per identity, or globally over training. The text around p.5, lines roughly 168–190, is not very precise. This matters, because the claimed “adaptive” nature of AAA seems to hinge on exactly this mechanism. Right now it feels half intuitive, half under-specified.

I also think the DAN/CAN distinction is not as clean as the paper suggests. The class adversarial component sounds interesting in principle, but it is not obvious to me that it is doing something fundamentally different from encouraging confusion over source-specific identity boundaries once transferred. Maybe that is the point, but then the paper should say this more directly instead of overselling it. Figure 2 helps a little, though honestly I found the arrows and training flow somewhat cluttered.

The experiments are decent and probably the strongest part. The cross-dataset results in Table 1 and Table 2 are competitive, and the gains are nontrivial on the harder transfers. Still, the ablations in Table 3 are not enough for me. I really wanted to see sensitivity to the perturbation factor and some evidence that the diversity metric is better than simpler proxies (feature variance, entropy, even a fixed schedule). Without that, the adaptive weighting risks looking like one more heuristic. Also, the comparison to prior DG-ReID methods is a bit uneven; I might be wrong, but I think some baselines are stronger with different backbones/training tricks than what is reflected here.

Overall, I think the paper is tackling an important setting and the empirical results are promising, but the technical contribution feels more incremental than the framing implies, and the presentation needs tightening. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the method and strengthen the ablations.

51.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than flashy. A lot of multimodal benchmark papers feel incremental, but this one is trying to evaluate something people actually care about: whether models can do nontrivial scientific figure understanding beyond toy plots. The examples in Figure 1 and the discipline spread described around p. 3 make the motivation pretty convincing. In my experience, most existing “science VQA” datasets are either heavily biomedical, too small, or not actually graduate-level once you inspect them closely. This seems broader.

That said, after reading this twice, I still have some concerns about what exactly is being measured. The paper leans hard on “graduate-level” and “discipline-specific expertise,” but I did not see a very rigorous operationalization of that claim. The taxonomy over 72 disciplines sounds impressive, but breadth is not the same as depth. On p. 4–5, where the data construction is described, I wanted much more about annotation quality, question-writing protocol, and leakage control. If the MCQ answers can be inferred from superficial caption cues or article text artifacts, then the benchmark may partly measure retrieval/guessing rather than figure understanding. I might be wrong, but Table 2 and the near-chance performance of many models could also simply reflect poor question design or ambiguous distractors, not just task difficulty.

I also found the evaluation for captioning a bit underdeveloped. If I understood Eq. (3) correctly, the metrics are largely n-gram/semantic similarity style metrics plus LLM-based judgment? For scientific captions, correctness is often about one key relation or one wrong noun, and standard generation metrics are notoriously noisy here. Some qualitative error analysis is shown (Figure 5), which helps, but I wanted more systematic breakdowns: per figure type, per discipline, and especially whether models fail on visual grounding versus scientific terminology. Right now the main conclusion is “captioning is hard,” which is true but not very diagnostic.

The fine-tuning results are nice, though. The claim on p. 8 that Qwen2-VL-2B reaches GPT-4o-level MCQ performance is attention-grabbing, but I would be cautious: was there any overlap in article domains or visual styles between training and test splits? The split strategy matters a lot here and was a little too briefly described for my taste.

Overall, I lean positive because the dataset seems genuinely valuable and the experiments are broad. But I’m not fully sold on the benchmark validity yet. This is close, though. With clearer annotation details, stronger contamination controls, and deeper error analysis, I’d be much more enthusiastic.

52.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—using a generative diffusion model as a self-supervised denoiser for diffusion MRI—is timely, and in general I do think the paper is aiming at an important problem. In vivo dMRI denoising without paired clean targets is genuinely valuable. I’ve reviewed a lot of medical imaging denoising papers over the years, and many of them are basically “Noise2Void plus domain jargon.” This is at least more ambitious than that.

That said, after reading the method section twice, I’m still not fully convinced I understand what is actually novel algorithmically versus what is a fairly standard conditional diffusion setup with a custom initialization / posterior interpretation. The three-stage framework around the “intermediate posterior distribution” is central, but the presentation around Eq. (6)–(10) felt too compressed. In particular, the jump from the statistical denoising prior to the diffusion-chain construction on p. 4 is not as clean as the authors seem to think. I might be wrong, but it reads like the probabilistic story is partly motivational rather than fully derived. Figure 2 helps a little, but not enough.

The experimental section is solid in breadth: 4 in-vivo datasets is respectable, and I appreciated that the authors did not only report generic image metrics. The clinically relevant quantities in Table 2 and Table 3 are more convincing than the usual PSNR/SSIM parade. Also, the visual comparisons in Figure 4 are honestly pretty good. The method seems to preserve structure better than some baselines while avoiding the plasticky oversmoothing that these papers often produce. That matters.

Still, there are some gaps. I really wanted stronger ablations. The paper claims the three stages all matter, but I did not see a convincing component-wise study isolating the effect of the statistic-based denoising part, the conditioning mechanism, and the choice of diffusion timestep at inference. If that is in Table 1, it is too limited. Also, runtime and memory are barely discussed. For MRI, especially 3D/volumetric settings, this is not a side issue. It is a practical bottleneck.

Another concern: the baselines. On p. 6–7, the comparison set seems reasonable, but I’m not entirely sure it is strong enough on the modern self-supervised side. Some methods look a bit dated. If the claim is “superior denoising,” the burden is higher.

Overall, I lean positive on significance, but weaker on technical clarity. My score would probably be in the weak accept / borderline range. The idea is promising and the empirical results are encouraging, but the paper needs a clearer methodological account and better ablations to really land.

53.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and honestly more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “let’s just bind everything to one modality and hope for the best” work. The core point — that fixed-anchor alignment can be arbitrarily limiting depending on the anchor choice — is intuitive, but the paper does a decent job making that criticism concrete. I especially liked the discussion around the failure to preserve non-anchor inter-modal structure; the toy analysis around Eq. (3)–(6) on p. 3 made the issue clearer than in prior papers I’ve seen.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the theoretical framing yet. The claims around “capturing” intra-modal learning, inter-modal learning, and multimodal alignment feel a bit too cleanly packaged. The proposition on p. 4 (around lines 148–171) reads more like a characterization of the objective than a strong guarantee about learned representations in realistic regimes. Maybe that is all the authors mean, but the wording in the abstract and intro oversells it. I might be wrong, but I don’t think the theory really establishes robustness in the broad sense claimed.

The empirical side is solid but a little uneven. Table 1 and Table 2 are encouraging, and Figure 3 does suggest the dynamic centroid is less sensitive than fixed text/image anchors. Still, I kept wanting stronger baselines. If the main target is ImageBind-style binding, then okay, compare to that, but why not include more recent joint embedding alternatives or even a simple late-fusion/control model? Also, the gains on the real-world benchmark (Table 2, p. 6) are not huge. They are consistent, yes, but this is one of those cases where “superiority” feels stronger than what the numbers justify.

I was also confused by implementation details. On p. 5, around the training description, it was not fully clear to me how the centroid is computed under missing modalities, and whether gradients flow through all modality encoders symmetrically at every step. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t totally sure. This matters, because otherwise the method may quietly reintroduce an implicit anchor.

Overall, I think the paper has a good idea and a respectable execution. It is not a slam dunk for me, mostly because the theory/claims mismatch and the experimental scope feels narrower than the framing. But I do think this is a meaningful contribution, and in my experience these “simple correction to a widely adopted assumption” papers can age well. Weak accept / borderline accept.

54.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because the general problem is real: long-context LLMs still struggle badly once the context gets huge, and in practice people do end up bolting retrieval onto them at inference time. So the paper is aiming at something useful. That said, I came away fairly unconvinced that the technical contribution is as novel as the writing suggests.

The core idea — query rewriting + semantic retrieval + some form of conditional embedding injected at test time — feels very close to standard RAG engineering, just applied in a long-context setting. I might be missing a subtlety, but after reading Section 3 twice, especially around Eq. (2) and the paragraph below it on page 4, I still could not pin down what exactly is new about the “novel conditional embeddings.” Are these learned adapters? Soft prompts? Retrieved-document-conditioned prefix vectors? The paper stays frustratingly high-level there. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. If this is the main novelty, it needs a much sharper and more concrete description.

I also found the experimental setup under-specified. The headline gains in Table 1 look nice, especially HotpotQA, but I don’t know if this is apples-to-apples against strong long-context baselines. On page 6, the baselines seem to mix models with different context windows and possibly different retrieval budgets. That is not a minor detail. In my experience, these systems are incredibly sensitive to chunking, top-k, reranking, and even prompt wording. Yet the ablations in Table 2 are pretty shallow. I really wanted to see: what happens with only rewriting, only retrieval, only conditional embeddings, and a stronger off-the-shelf RAG baseline with the same retriever and generator budget? Right now the attribution of gains is murky.

Another issue is the “1M to 9.6M tokens” claim in the abstract. This sounds impressive, maybe too impressive. But the paper does not really convince me the model is understanding that much context, as opposed to searching over chunked text and surfacing relevant snippets. That is still useful! But it is not the same claim. The wording oversells things.

I did like that the authors tested across HotpotQA, QASPER, and NQ, and I think the practical angle is strong. But the paper currently reads more like a solid systems combination than a clear algorithmic advance. If accepted, it should be on empirical usefulness grounds, not novelty. My leaning is weak reject unless the camera-ready version can clarify the method and tighten the comparisons substantially.

55.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically useful than a lot of recent “preference learning for code” submissions. The core idea is not wildly novel in isolation — use execution-derived signals, do on-policy data collection, optimize preferences — but the combination is sensible, and for code it fits the domain much better than generic reward-model-heavy RLHF pipelines. In my experience, papers in this area often hand-wave the data collection loop; here the pipeline is at least concrete.

That said, the presentation is a bit sloppy in places. The abstract says “PLUM levitates the need to train reward models,” which I assume means “alleviates” or “eliminates”; this sounds minor, but it reflects a broader issue that the writing could use another pass. I also had to read the method section twice to pin down exactly how the automatic test generation interacts with preference labeling. Around the main algorithm description (I think this is Sec. 3, especially the part near Eq. (2)/(3)), it is still not fully clear to me how noisy generated tests affect pairwise preference quality. If the generated tests are weak or systematically biased, the whole signal could become brittle. The paper acknowledges this, but I did not see a sufficiently deep error analysis.

The empirical results are good. Table 1 / Table 2 (assuming I’m mapping them correctly) show consistent gains over the SFT baselines, and the LiveCodeBench improvements are especially notable since that benchmark is less gameable than HumanEval. I liked that the authors evaluated multiple base code LMs instead of overfitting the story to one model. Figure 3 was also useful in showing the effect of on-policy/online collection, which to me is actually the most convincing part of the paper. The claim that on-policy matters is not new, but here it seems to genuinely hold in code generation too.

Still, I’m not entirely sure the comparisons are as strong as they should be. Some execution-feedback baselines look under-tuned, or at least the paper does not provide enough detail to rule that out. I also wanted more compute/accounting detail: how expensive is repeated candidate sampling plus test generation in practice? If this is much more costly than the baselines, that matters. And for a paper centered on automatic tests, I expected a more careful breakdown by test quality, maybe with examples of generated tests that are wrong but still induce “useful” preferences, or cases where they fail badly.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar for me, but not a slam dunk. The idea is pragmatic rather than conceptually deep. If accepted, I’d want the camera-ready to tighten the method description and include more analysis of test-case noise and annotation reliability.

56.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantive than a lot of the recent “online alignment” submissions that mostly repackage old ideas with LLM terminology. The contextual dueling bandit framing is a sensible one, and I think the connection made on p.2–3 is one of the cleaner parts of the paper. In particular, the way Eq. (3) and the discussion right after it tie online RLHF / DPO-style preference collection to a budgeted exploration problem felt conceptually right to me. I’ve worked on bandits and preference learning before, and this is the first paper in this batch that at least seems to understand that active data collection is the actual bottleneck.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the “unified algorithm” claim. After reading Sec. 4 twice, SEA looks like a fairly natural Thompson-sampling instantiation around an existing preference-learning loop, rather than a genuinely new algorithmic idea. Maybe that is enough, but the paper oversells this a bit. The description around Algorithm 1 and the approximation used to make posterior sampling practical (p.5, lines ~180–210) is also somewhat hand-wavy. I might be wrong, but it seems the practical behavior depends heavily on modeling choices that are not really justified theoretically.

The experiments are decent overall. I appreciated that Table 1 spans 1B/2.8B/6.9B and multiple objectives (DPO, IPO, SLiC), because too many papers cherry-pick one setup. Figure 3 is probably the most convincing result: SEA appears consistently more sample efficient than the baselines. Still, the setup is a little too “oracle preference” centric for my taste. On p.7 the authors acknowledge this, but I think it matters a lot. Real human feedback is noisy, inconsistent, and often prompt-dependent in ugly ways. I would have liked at least one simulation with annotator noise or misspecification. Otherwise the claim of practical alignment efficiency feels premature.

I was also confused by the distinction between the two application scenarios in Sec. 5. They are presented as different, but experimentally they blur together. Figure 2 helped, but not enough. Some notation is also overloaded; by p.4 I had to flip back to check what exactly was being sampled.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably above the acceptance bar, but not a slam dunk. The main value is the formulation plus a solid empirical demonstration, not some major algorithmic breakthrough. If accepted, I’d want the claims toned down and the practical assumptions discussed more candidly.

57.review_text:
I came away fairly unconvinced by this paper, although I do think the underlying goal is interesting. Generalizing from short proofs to longer ones is absolutely a real problem in automated theorem proving, and I appreciate that the authors are at least trying to isolate that phenomenon instead of reporting another generic “RL helps search” result. But after reading the paper twice, I still feel the empirical setup is too synthetic and the claims are stronger than what is actually demonstrated.

The core issue for me is that the paper’s main evidence rests on a synthetic arithmetic benchmark with a hand-designed notion of “similar structure” across proof lengths. That may be fine as a first step, but then the title and framing (“Towards Finding Longer Proofs”) feel a bit too broad. In my experience reviewing theorem proving papers, this kind of benchmark often bakes in the curriculum in a way that makes curriculum learning look better than it would in a more heterogeneous setting. I might be wrong, but that is exactly the impression I got from the way the tasks are generated (I’m referring especially to the benchmark construction around p. 3–4, and the progression shown in Fig. 2).

I also found the method section somewhat underspecified. The paper says it uses “state-of-the-art RL approaches previously not applied in theorem proving,” but it is not always clear what is genuinely novel here versus a straightforward transplant of existing RL machinery. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text on p. 5 describe the training objective, but I was left wondering about stability, reward sparsity, and the amount of tuning required. Those details matter a lot. Table 1 reports the headline gains, but I could not tell whether the baselines were equally well tuned. Frankly, with RL papers, that is half the battle.

Another thing: the comparison to prior learning-based proof guidance feels a little selective. There is a difference between showing improvement over a weak learned heuristic on this dataset and showing a meaningful advance for ATP. The paper gestures at broader implications, but I didn’t see evidence it would transfer to realistic proof domains. A small experiment on a standard theorem proving benchmark, even if negative or partial, would have helped a lot.

I did like the curriculum ablation; that part is probably the most convincing piece of the paper. The trend in Fig. 3 is intuitive and believable. Still, intuition is not enough. Right now this reads more like a well-executed proof-of-concept than a strong conference paper. If the authors toned down the claims and added stronger baselines / more realistic evaluation, I’d be much more positive. As it stands, I lean reject.

58.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core question—what can CNNs learn when the input is effectively infinite-dimensional—is important, and I do think the authors are aiming at something more meaningful than the usual “fixed d, n→∞” theory that has dominated a lot of approximation papers. In that sense, the paper is timely. The mixed/anisotropic smoothness angle is also sensible; in my experience this is exactly the kind of structure one needs if one wants to say anything non-vacuous in high or infinite dimension.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper delivers as cleanly as it claims. The main statements around approximation and estimation seem technically nontrivial, but the presentation is quite compressed. I had to read the theorem statements around the middle of the paper twice to understand what is actually being assumed on the smoothness sequence and how this avoids dimensional dependence. I might be wrong, but it felt like a lot of the “infinite-dimensional” advantage is coming from fairly strong decay/summability assumptions encoded in the anisotropic smoothness class. That is not illegitimate, of course, but then the practical message “this supports the great success of CNNs” (already in the abstract) is overstated.

I did like the distinction between standard and dilated convolutions, especially the claim that dilation helps when the smoothness has a sparse structure. That part felt more specific and less generic. But again, I wanted a clearer interpretation. For example, the result corresponding to the dilated case (I think around the later theorem/equation block—sorry, I don’t have the exact numbering in front of me) is mathematically neat, yet I was missing even a toy example showing a target function class where the benefit is tangible. A small schematic or figure would have helped. As is, the paper is theorem-heavy and somewhat austere.

Another issue: the assumptions and notation are dense. The function spaces are introduced quickly, and several constants/rates are hidden in ways that make comparison hard. Table 1 or a concise comparison table with prior rates would have gone a long way; without it, it is difficult to gauge what is genuinely new relative to existing neural approximation results for mixed Besov/Sobolev-type classes. I know some of that literature fairly well, and the novelty here seems plausible but not totally transparent.

Overall, I think there is real technical merit here, and I would not dismiss it. But the paper currently reads more like a specialized theory note than a broadly compelling conference paper. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can clarify the assumptions, sharpen the comparison to prior work, and tone down the claims.

59.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than I expected from the title. The main empirical claim — that intermediate representations can outperform the penultimate layer for OOD generalization — is plausible, and the experiments mostly support it. Figure 1 makes the point clearly, and I liked that the authors did not overcomplicate the method: attach simple classifiers to intermediate layers and compare. Sometimes these straightforward papers are the ones people actually use.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper has isolated the mechanism it claims. The abstract and discussion suggest that intermediate layers are “less sensitive” to shift, but the evidence for that felt a bit hand-wavy. If I understood Eq. (2) correctly, the classifiers are trained in a fairly standard way on frozen features, so part of the gain may simply be that the penultimate layer is more specialized to the source task and therefore more brittle. That is not a deep new insight by itself; people in robustness / transfer have been circling around this for a while. The paper needs to situate itself better relative to prior work on layer-wise transferability and linear probing. I was surprised this was not discussed more directly around p. 2–3.

I also had some confusion about the exact protocol. On p. 4, the distinction between zero-shot OOD evaluation and few-shot adaptation is a bit slippery, and Table 2 mixes settings that are not directly comparable unless one reads the fine print. Maybe I’m being picky, but these details matter a lot here. In my experience, small changes in probe training or model selection can swing these numbers.

The breadth is good: multiple datasets, architectures, and shifts. That helps. But some of the analysis sections felt thinner than the empirical claim deserves. Figure 4 is suggestive, not conclusive. I wanted more layerwise diagnostics beyond accuracy — e.g., calibration, invariance measures, or even something as simple as sensitivity to label noise. Also, are the “best” intermediate layers selected with OOD validation? If yes, that weakens the practical takeaway quite a bit. If no, say it more explicitly.

Overall I lean positive because the phenomenon seems real and practically relevant, but I’m not enthusiastic. Good empirical paper, somewhat overstated interpretation, and the novelty is moderate rather than high.

61.review_text:
I found this paper interesting but also somewhat overclaimed. The core idea — adding a correlation/distribution-aware regularizer for tabular generative models — is sensible, and honestly this is the kind of practical contribution that often matters more than another brand-new architecture. I appreciated the breadth of the benchmark: 20 datasets and 10 baselines is not nothing, and Table 3 / Table 4 do suggest the method is fairly robust. In particular, the gains on the smaller, mixed-type datasets seem real rather than cherry-picked. I also liked that the authors tried to disentangle fidelity metrics from downstream utility instead of pretending one number captures everything.
