That said, after reading this twice, I still think the paper is trying to do too much. There are really three papers mashed together here: a new loss, a new BO aggregation method, and a benchmarking/statistical testing protocol. The abstract admits this, but the main text never fully resolves the consequence: what exactly is the primary scientific claim? By page 6, around Eq. (7)–(10), the loss definition is introduced, but the intuition is still a bit hand-wavy. I understand the ingredients, but I’m not entirely sure why this particular composition should be preferred over simpler discrepancy terms. Figure 2 helps a little, though I found Figure 3 much clearer.

My bigger concern is attribution of gains. The ablations on page 8 are useful, but not sufficient. If I’m reading Table 5 correctly, some improvements are modest and within what I would expect from careful tuning alone. That makes the IORBO part awkward: if your tuning method is better than standard BO, how much of the final improvement should be credited to the loss itself? I might be wrong, but the paper doesn’t cleanly isolate that. I would have liked a fixed-budget comparison where all methods get identical tuning resources, plus a “new loss + old BO” and “old loss + IORBO” matrix.

A more minor point: the statistical testing framework is ambitious, but the presentation around page 10–11 is dense and a bit self-congratulatory. Also, some claims of “significant improvement” need more careful wording depending on which metric and dataset family we are talking about.

Overall, I think there is a solid contribution here, especially on the empirical side, but the paper needs sharper scoping and cleaner causal evidence. Borderline accept from me, though I’m not fully convinced yet.

60.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than many benchmark papers I see, even if I’m not completely convinced by all of the methodological choices. The core contribution is straightforward: a university-level math benchmark with 1,100 open-ended problems, spread across six subjects, plus a multimodal slice (20%), and a companion judging set, μ-MATH, for evaluating LLM-as-judge. That fills a real gap. Most current math benchmarks are either too school-level, too small, contaminated, or overly multiple-choice. So at a high level, I buy the motivation.

What I liked most is the ambition of the dataset design. The paper makes a credible case that university math is underrepresented, and the examples/figures (especially the visual ones; I think Figure 2 or 3, depending on the version) do show problems that are qualitatively different from GSM8K-style arithmetic or olympiad-style symbolic tricks. The multimodal aspect is also a good addition, not just gimmicky. In my experience, many “multimodal math” datasets are really OCR benchmarks in disguise; this one seems more grounded in actual teaching material.

That said, I have several concerns. The biggest is evaluation reliability. The paper leans heavily on LLM-based judging because answers are open-ended, but the abstract already admits the best judge only gets 80% F1 on μ-MATH. That is not terrible, but it is not strong enough for me to fully trust the leaderboard-style comparisons, especially when model differences may be small. I kept wondering, after reading this twice, whether the reported model rankings on U-MATH would remain stable under human grading or stricter rubric-based checking. The discussion around this felt a bit too casual. If Equation (or protocol) for final scoring is on p. 5–6, I would have liked more sensitivity analysis there.

I also wasn’t fully satisfied with the dataset construction details. The paper says the problems are unpublished and sourced from teaching materials, which is good, but I wanted more transparency on provenance, filtering, and potential leakage. “Unpublished” does not necessarily mean absent from the web or model pretraining. Maybe I missed it, but the contamination discussion felt brief.

The results themselves are believable and useful: 63% on text and 45% on visual are strong evidence that this is nontrivial. Table 2/3 (again, going from memory) was probably the most convincing part of the empirical section. Still, I would have liked a finer error analysis by subject and by failure mode. Which subjects break current models? Is the visual drop due to diagrams, notation, or longer reasoning chains? Right now the paper diagnoses “hard benchmark” more than it explains why.

Overall, I lean positive. This is a meaningful benchmark contribution, and I suspect the community will use it. But the paper would be stronger with more rigorous validation of the judging pipeline and more concrete analysis of dataset provenance and errors. Weak accept.

62.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because the basic pitch is appealing: can we get more mileage out of weak adversaries by doing something smarter locally, instead of just cranking up PGD steps forever. In spirit, I like that. The “local ball around an adversary + global coupling term” idea is not obviously silly, and in fact Figure 1 helped me understand the intuition better than the formalism did.

That said, after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is either as novel or as well-justified as the paper claims. The core objective around Eq. (6)–(8) (I may be off by one equation number) felt under-motivated. Why exactly should improving robustness in balls centered at adversarial examples lead to better coverage of the full threat region, beyond a heuristic geometric story? The “global term” is doing a lot of conceptual work here, but I found its role vague. On p. 4, around the paragraph below Algorithm 1, the authors say this term “combines” local balls into global robustness, but that is more of an aspiration than an argument. I kept waiting for either a theorem or at least a sharper proposition. It never really came.

The empirical results are decent, maybe even strong. Table 2 and Table 3 show competitive robust accuracy, especially on CIFAR-100, and I appreciate that training time is reported rather than hidden. Still, I’m uneasy about the comparison protocol. If the claim is “weak adversaries suffice,” then the fairness of baselines matters enormously. I’m not entirely sure the baselines in p. 6–7 are tuned equally hard under the same compute budget. In my experience, robust training numbers move a lot with small recipe details. Also, I would have liked a stronger evaluation with transfer and AutoAttack-style checks; some of the reported gains under the training-time adversary could just be gradient masking. Maybe I’m being too skeptical, but this literature has earned that skepticism.

There are also presentation issues. Figure 2 is conceptually overloaded, and some notation is introduced late. The writing is not terrible, but it is a bit “conference-optimistic,” if you know what I mean.

Overall, I think the paper has a real idea in it, and the runtime angle is valuable. But the current version feels too hand-wavy theoretically and not airtight enough empirically for a top venue. Borderline for me, leaning reject.

64.review_text:
I found the core idea interesting: instead of learning \(V^\pi(s)\) for just the current policy, learn something closer to \(V(s, \pi)\) and hope to generalize across nearby policies. This is not a crazy idea at all, and in fact I was a bit surprised it has not been explored more directly in actor-critic style methods. The paper’s notion of “local generalization” along the policy improvement path is probably the most compelling part for me. Figure 1 and the discussion around p.3–4 helped motivate this reasonably well.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper cleanly separates conceptual novelty from engineering. A lot hinges on the policy representation. The method is only as good as the embedding of \(\pi\), and in Sections 4.1/4.2 (around the contrastive/action-prediction discussion), the paper feels a bit opportunistic: several encoders are tried, and the positive result seems to come from “some representation works okay” rather than from a strong principle. I might be wrong, but this makes the theoretical framing feel stronger than the actual algorithmic contribution.

The theory also left me with mixed feelings. The claims around value generalization and the derivation of the modified GPI are intuitively fine, but I did not find the assumptions especially satisfying. Around the proposition/theorem statements in the middle of the paper (I’m referring to the pages with the local generalization bounds; I think around p.5–6), the guarantees seem to rely on smoothness/Lipschitz-type behavior of values over policy space, which is exactly the part that is hard in deep RL. So yes, under these assumptions PeVFA helps, but I’m not sure the analysis explains why it should help in PPO specifically.

Empirically, the MuJoCo results are promising, and Table 1 / the learning curves do show fairly consistent gains over vanilla PPO. But the evaluation is also a bit narrow. I really wanted stronger ablations: how much comes from extra capacity, how much from conditioning on policy input, how much from representation pretraining, and how much from simply giving the critic historical information about past actors? Also, wall-clock / sample-efficiency tradeoffs are not discussed enough. If the critic is now more expensive and trained with additional objectives, that matters.

Overall, I think this is a good paper with a neat idea, but I’m not at the level of enthusiasm the authors seem to have. Interesting, probably worthy of discussion, though not completely airtight. My score would lean slightly positive if the conference values novel RL formulations, but I do have real reservations.

63.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—generate FOA directly from silent video—is genuinely novel, or at least novel enough for this community. I’ve seen a lot of work on video-to-audio and some on spatial audio upmixing/rendering, but direct video-to-FOA is not something I recall seeing in a convincing form. So on ambition alone, this stands out.

That said, after reading it twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the evaluation. The new spatial metrics based on energy maps/saliency (Sec. 4, around Eq. 7–10 if I tracked correctly) are reasonable in spirit, but they feel somewhat self-serving. They reward spatial consistency under the authors’ representation, but I’m not sure they correlate with perceptual spatial realism. Table 2 looks good for ViSAGe, yes, but I kept wondering whether the model is just learning coarse directional priors rather than truly grounded spatial acoustics. A listening test would help a lot here, and I think the paper really needs one. For a problem this perceptual, the absence is noticeable.

The dataset contribution is potentially big. 102K clips is not small. But I had questions about YT-Ambigen that the paper only partially answers. On page 4, the filtering/curation pipeline seemed under-described. How much camera motion is present? Are there lots of clips where the audio is off-screen, dubbed, or edited? In my experience with YouTube-sourced AV datasets, this matters enormously, and the paper is a little too casual about it. Figure 3 gives some examples, but cherry-picked examples are cheap.

On the model side, the directional + visual guidance mechanism is sensible, and I appreciated that the authors compare against a two-stage baseline rather than a strawman. Still, I’m not entirely sure the baseline is strong enough. If the video-to-audio front end is mediocre, then of course the downstream spatialization suffers. I would have liked to see a stronger modern diffusion-based or large-scale codec LM baseline, unless I missed it in Appendix C.

Figure 5 is nice and qualitatively persuasive. Some of the viewpoint adaptation examples are honestly cool. But the paper occasionally overclaims; “high-quality” feels too strong given the evidence.

Overall, I lean positive because the task and dataset are important and the system seems nontrivial. But this is not a slam dunk for me. I’d probably put this in the weak accept / borderline area.

65.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — replacing the hard max in SAM with an exponentially tilted aggregation over local perturbations — is actually pretty natural, and I’m somewhat surprised it hasn’t been explored more directly before. The connection “TSAM → SAM as \(t \to \infty\)” is clean, and the smoothing argument around the objective is, at least at a high level, plausible. I liked the intuition in the early part of the paper and the geometric story about reweighting local minima rather than committing to a single worst-case perturbation.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the theory says as much as the paper claims. In particular, the statement that TSAM “explicitly favors flatter minima as \(t\) increases” felt stronger than what was actually established. Around the theorem/proposition section (I think this is on pp. 4–5, around Eq. (6) onward), the notion of “flatness” seems tied to local curvature under the tilted measure, but the bridge from that to the actual minima found by SGD-like dynamics is pretty loose. I might be wrong, but this reads more like an appealing interpretation than a rigorous characterization. Also, the Hamiltonian-dynamics motivation for the algorithm is interesting, but I wasn’t convinced it is necessary; the final method looked to me like a fairly heuristic optimizer design wrapped in a physics narrative.

The empirical section is solid but not knockout. The results in the main tables (Table 1/2) show gains over ERM and usually over SAM, but many of the margins are modest, and I would have liked a clearer accounting of extra tuning. The new hyperparameter \(t\) is not free. If TSAM beats SAM only after substantial search over \(t\), the comparison is less compelling than the paper suggests. I also wanted more ablations on sensitivity to neighborhood size and computational overhead per update. Figure 2 was helpful, but I wish the paper quantified “flatter local minima” more directly instead of relying mostly on test accuracy plus a landscape visualization.

Overall, I think the paper has a real idea in it, and it is more than a trivial variant of SAM. But the presentation oversells the theoretical guarantees, and the empirical advantage, while real-looking, is not yet decisive. Borderline accept for me if the bar is novelty + decent evidence; otherwise weak reject.

66.review_text:
I have mixed feelings about this paper. The core idea — scaling both the residual contribution and the update by \(1/\sqrt{L}\) to get a meaningful infinite-depth limit with nontrivial feature learning — is interesting, and honestly this is one of the more substantive “infinite width/depth” theory papers I’ve read recently. I’ve followed the Tensor Programs line of work for a while, and this one is more ambitious than most. The theorem statements around the infinite-depth limit are nontrivial, and I do think there is a real contribution here.

That said, I found the paper harder to parse than it needed to be. After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I still wasn’t fully confident I understood what is genuinely new relative to prior \(\mu\)P/maximal update parameterization work versus what is a depth-specific restatement. The informal explanation early on is actually pretty good, but then the presentation becomes very compressed. In particular, the transition from the motivating discussion to the formal scaling rule (around Eq. (3)/(4), if I’m tracking correctly) felt abrupt. I wanted a cleaner statement of “here is the baseline that fails, here is Depth-\(\mu\)P, here is precisely what it preserves.” Maybe this is there, but it is buried.

I liked the empirical section more than I expected. The experiments with depth up to \(2^{10}\) are not huge by current standards in width/compute, but for a depth-scaling paper they are appropriate, and the plots showing stable optimization across depth are compelling. Figure 2 and Table 1 (I may be off by one) were the most convincing pieces for me. The hyperparameter transfer claim is important, and I appreciate that they actually test it rather than just asserting it. Still, I wanted stronger baselines. Comparing mainly against naive/de facto scaling is not enough; there is a lot of residual-network scaling folklore, DeepNorm-style ideas, etc., and I think the paper sidesteps that literature a bit too much.

One thing that bothered me: the “maximizing feature learning and diversity among nearby layers” language in the abstract feels stronger than what is rigorously established. I might be wrong, but the theory seems to show existence/nontriviality of the limit and stable scaling, not an optimization of diversity in any formal sense. That wording should be toned down.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably accept, but not a clean one. The technical contribution is real. The exposition and experimental positioning need work, and some claims are oversold.

67.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thought-provoking than the average “theory + slightly better sampler” submission. The core idea — interpreting denoising as approximate projection and then relating diffusion sampling to gradient descent on the Euclidean distance to the data manifold — is clean and, at least at a high level, quite intuitive. I liked the geometric perspective in Sec. 3, especially the connection between the denoiser and projection error. Figure 1 also helps; it’s one of those figures that actually does some work instead of just decorating the intro.

That said, after reading the theory sections twice, I’m not fully convinced the analysis says as much as the paper wants it to say. The assumptions around projection error (around Def. 3.1 / Thm. 4.2, if I tracked the numbering correctly) feel very strong and somewhat detached from the actual denoisers used in practice. The manifold hypothesis is doing a lot of heavy lifting here. On page 5, the discussion of noise being “approximately orthogonal perturbation” is plausible locally, but the paper slides past curvature, nonuniform density, and the fact that diffusion models operate far from the manifold for a substantial part of sampling. I might be wrong, but this seems exactly where the geometric story becomes least reliable.

The convergence analysis for DDIM is nice in spirit, but I found the presentation a bit too “straightforward” in the way theory papers sometimes say that when they mean “we hide the hard part in assumptions.” Equation (8) and the subsequent bound are readable, but I wanted more interpretation of constants and whether they are even vaguely realistic. Right now the theorem feels more explanatory than predictive.

Empirically, the results are good. Table 1 is strong, especially the 5–10 NFE regime, and I appreciate that the method is tested on pretrained models rather than requiring retraining. That makes the contribution more practically relevant. But I was missing a more careful ablation of the two proposed modifications to DDIM. As presented, it’s hard to tell whether both are necessary, whether one dominates, or whether this is mostly careful tuning. Figure 3 suggests robustness, but not decisively.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper has a real idea, not just benchmark chasing. Still, I don’t think the theory is as solid as the framing suggests, and the empirical section could do more to isolate why the sampler works. Borderline accept for me, maybe weak accept depending on the bar for theory-vs-practice in this venue.

68.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more substantial than a lot of the recent “optimizer as SDE” papers I’ve seen. The main contribution seems to be the derivation of SDE limits for SignSGD, RMSprop(W), and Adam(W), together with a claim that these are quantitatively accurate enough to explain behavior beyond vague diffusion-level intuition. I think the SignSGD part is the strongest by a clear margin. The discussion around convergence/stationary behavior and robustness to heavy-tailed noise (around Sec. 3, especially the theorem statements near Eq. (8)–(12)) is sharp and, at least to me, felt like a real insight rather than just another continuous-time rephrasing.

That said, I’m less convinced by the AdamW/RMSpropW story. After reading Sec. 4 twice, I still found the role of the preconditioner/noise coupling somewhat muddy. The paper says the role of noise is “much more complex,” which is true, but that also becomes a bit of an escape hatch. In particular, the interpretation of the drift/diffusion terms around Eq. (15) and Eq. (18) is not as clean as the SignSGD case, and I’m not entirely sure which conclusions are actually robust versus artifacts of the approximations used. I might be missing something, but the assumptions needed there felt stronger and less realistic than the messaging in the introduction suggests.

The empirical side is good overall. I appreciated that the authors compare numerical integration of the SDEs to actual optimizer trajectories on multiple architectures; Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 were convincing, and Table 1 is useful. Also, thank you for including Transformers instead of stopping at tiny CNNs. Still, I wanted more detail on where the approximation breaks. Some curves in Fig. 5 seem visibly off in later training, and the text kind of glides past that. If the claim is “quantitatively accurate,” then the failure modes matter a lot.

I also have a small but nagging concern about novelty relative to prior SDE analyses of Adam/RMSprop. The paper does improve on previous formulations, yes, but the exact conceptual delta should be stated more explicitly, maybe in the related work or around p. 3–4. Right now it reads a bit too much like “previous SDE bad, ours good.”

Overall, I lean positive. The SignSGD theory is genuinely strong, and the paper is ambitious in a good way. But the adaptive-method generalization feels less airtight than the abstract implies. I’d support acceptance if the bar is “interesting and useful theoretical lens,” not “definitive theory of adaptive optimizers.”

69.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more practically motivated than a lot of “LM auditing” submissions. The core idea—treating behavioral shift detection as a statistical testing problem between a baseline model and a modified/deployed model—makes sense, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to control false positives rather than just report deltas on ad hoc benchmarks. In my experience, this is exactly the kind of thing labs say they want but usually operationalize very badly.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the contribution is as broad as the paper claims. The formalism on p. 3–4 (around Eq. 1 and Eq. 2) defines “qualitative properties” via some scoring function/classifier over generations, but then the practical success of the method seems to depend almost entirely on that external measurement pipeline. If the toxicity classifier is noisy or brittle, then the test is auditing the classifier-induced distribution, not really the LM behavior in any robust sense. I think the paper knows this, but it undersells the issue. The guarantees are only as meaningful as the property measurement. This should be stated much more plainly.

I also found Figure 2 a bit too clean relative to the messy deployment story the introduction motivates. The theory appears to assume i.i.d. sampling from both models under a fixed prompt distribution, but in deployment prompt mix drift is usually entangled with model drift. Maybe I’m missing something, but the setup on p. 5, lines ~160–180, seems to duck that problem rather than address it. If the intended use is post-deployment monitoring, this is a major limitation.

The case studies are decent but narrow. Toxicity is the obvious one, and translation quality in Table 1 is useful as a “non-safety” example, but I wanted at least one more open-ended behavior where the property is less reducible to a standard scalar metric. As is, the empirical section feels a bit too curated. Also, “hundreds of examples” is promising, but the exact sample efficiency depends heavily on effect size; the paper should be more careful not to oversell that.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and I like the statistical framing. But the current version feels more like a method for detecting shifts in proxy scores over generations than a general behavioral auditing framework for LMs. I’m leaning weak accept / borderline, mostly because the problem is important and the method is clean, but I do think the claims need tightening.

70.review_text:
I found the paper interesting in intent, but honestly pretty underdeveloped as a conference submission. The basic idea — compress the encoder outputs with a pre-trained autoencoder so that a much smaller decoder can be trained — is sensible, and I do think model compression for summarization is worth studying. In practice, though, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper really isolates what is new here versus a fairly straightforward bottlenecking trick.

A lot of the presentation is too vague. The abstract already says “96%, 66%, 50%, 44%” reductions, but I had to infer what exactly is being reduced (latent dimensionality? decoder parameter count? total model size?) and this ambiguity persists in the main text. If Eq. (3) is supposed to define the AE compression mapping, it needs much more detail: what loss was used to pretrain the AE, on what corpus, and with what stopping criterion? I might have missed it, but I could not find a clear statement of whether the AE is trained to reconstruct token-level hidden states from the frozen encoder or something pooled/sequence-level. That distinction matters a lot.

The empirical section also feels thin. The paper leans heavily on ROUGE, mostly R-1, and the claim in the abstract of a 5% increase with DistilBERT sounds nice, but without confidence intervals or multiple runs it’s hard to know if this is noise. Table 2 (if I’m reading it correctly) compares several encoders with different compression rates, but the baselines are not strong enough. Where is the direct baseline of just shrinking the decoder width to the same parameter budget without the AE? That seems like the obvious comparison, and without it I can’t tell whether the AE is actually helping or just changing capacity allocation.

I was also confused by the choice to keep the encoder frozen throughout. That makes the setup cheaper, yes, but it is also a somewhat artificial regime for summarization. In my experience, frozen-encoder summarization can behave quite differently from standard fine-tuning, so I’m not sure the conclusions transfer. Figure 1 gives the architecture at a high level, but I wanted actual tensor dimensions at each stage.

There is maybe a workshop paper in here, or at least a preliminary systems note. But as it stands, the experimental evidence is too narrow and the method feels incremental. My overall reaction is lukewarm.

71.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than a lot of “LLM-as-a-judge” submissions I’ve seen lately. The core idea — use multiple weak evaluators, build a preference graph, then denoise it into a DAG to remove cyclic inconsistencies — is sensible, and I appreciate that the authors are attacking a real pathology rather than just squeezing a few points on yet another benchmark. The motivating issue of A > B, B > C, C > A is very real in practice. I’ve run into exactly this when using GPT-4-style judges for pairwise ranking.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. The theoretical story seems cleaner than the empirical setting actually warrants. On p. 4 around Eq. (3)-(5), the aggregation assumptions feel fairly strong, and I’m not entirely sure the noise model matches what modern LLM judges do. These evaluators are not just “noisy”; they are biased in correlated, prompt-sensitive ways. If the weak evaluators share the same blind spots, I don’t see why graph ensembling should help as much as claimed. The theory section reads nicely, but I had the feeling it is proving something a bit adjacent to the real problem.

I also found the denoising step somewhat underexplained. Figure 2 gives the high-level pipeline, but the exact optimization objective and computational cost were not as transparent as they should be. Maybe I missed it on first pass, but after reading that section twice, I still wasn’t sure how ties / near-ties are handled, and whether forcing a DAG may erase legitimate uncertainty rather than just contradictions. In preference learning, cycles are not always pure noise.

The empirical section is solid overall. Table 2 and Table 3 are the main reason I’m positive on the paper: the fact that ensembles of 7B/8B judges can beat a 72B judge is actually notable, not just statistically significant-but-whatever. Still, I wanted more ablations. How much comes from ensembling versus the DAG projection itself? What happens if one evaluator is adversarial or simply terrible? The paper gestures at robustness, but I didn’t see a convincing stress test. Also, ten benchmarks sounds broad, but some of these judge benchmarks are highly overlapping in style.

My overall take: good paper, but a bit overclaimed. I think there is a real contribution here, especially for scalable evaluation, though the theoretical framing may be stronger than the evidence strictly supports. I’d lean weak accept if the conference values practical evaluation methodology, but I do have reservations.

72.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than many benchmark papers that just reshuffle existing datasets and call it a day. The core point — that single-turn evaluation misses a lot of what matters once models have to use tools and respond to feedback over multiple turns — is correct, and overdue. I especially appreciated the setup on p.2–3 where the authors motivate the gap between benchmark performance and actual deployment. In my experience, this is exactly where a lot of current LLM evaluation is too clean.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the benchmark, as instantiated here, measures what the paper claims. The biggest issue for me is the simulated user feedback via GPT-4 (described around Sec. 3, and if I’m reading correctly the details are partly deferred to appendix/prompts). This is pragmatic, yes, but it creates a strange closed loop: models are evaluated on interaction quality with a synthetic user whose behavior is itself induced by another LLM. After reading this twice, I still wasn’t sure how robust the conclusions are to the exact prompting of the simulator, or to GPT-4’s own biases/preferences. The paper talks a lot about “natural language feedback,” but this is only one very specific kind of generated feedback. I would really want an ablation here: same tasks, different user simulators, maybe even a templated one, to see whether the rankings in Table 2/3 are stable. Otherwise, the benchmark may partly be a GPT-4-compatibility test.

I also think the headline claim that SIFT/RLHF “generally hurt” multi-turn capability is too strong given the evidence. It is intriguing, yes. But there are many confounds: model family, tool-use formatting, verbosity priors, refusal tendencies, etc. Figure 4 (if I interpreted it correctly) suggests a trend, but not one I’d call fully disentangled. I might be wrong, but this feels more like a hypothesis-generating result than a conclusion.

Some of the benchmark curation decisions are underexplained. The compact subset idea makes sense for cost reasons, but on p.4–5 I wanted more detail on what was excluded and whether that pruning changed task difficulty or interaction structure. Also, Python execution as the sole tool interface is convenient but narrow. That choice may advantage code-oriented models in a way that muddies the “multi-turn interaction” story.

Still, I liked the paper overall. The framing is good, the experiments are broader than average, and the negative result about single-turn performance not predicting multi-turn behavior is important. I’m leaning positive, but not enthusiastically so. For a top venue, I’d want stronger validation that the benchmark is not overly dependent on the simulator design.

73.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because multimodal molecular learning is a crowded space right now, and a lot of submissions basically just concatenate fingerprints with a GNN and call it a day. This one is a bit more ambitious. I do think the central idea — combining relational learning during pretraining with a fairly systematic study of early/intermediate/late fusion — is reasonable and, at least in parts, useful. The discussion around fusion stage is probably the most practically valuable part of the paper. Figure 3 and Table 2 were the places where I felt the paper was actually teaching me something, rather than just reporting another benchmark win.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. My main issue is that the claimed novelty feels somewhat overstated. After reading Sections 3.1 and 3.2 twice, I still think MMFRL is more of a careful engineering combination of known ingredients than a genuinely new framework. The relational learning piece, especially around Eq. (6)–(8), seems close in spirit to metric/contrastive variants already used in molecular pretraining, except with a richer similarity structure. That is not nothing, but the paper sometimes writes as if binary contrastive objectives are the only baseline worth comparing to, which is just not true. There is a lot of related work on soft relational supervision, graph similarity learning, etc., and I found the related work section a bit selective.

I was also confused by the experimental protocol. On page 6, around the dataset split/training details, it was not totally clear to me whether all baselines were re-tuned under the same scaffold split settings and budget, or partly copied from prior papers. This matters a lot on MoleculeNet. Some of the gains in Table 3 are decent, but not huge, and with these benchmarks small implementation differences can easily swamp the story. I might be wrong, but I also suspect the fingerprint/text/descriptor modalities are not equally available across tasks, and the paper glosses over the practical cost of constructing them.

The explainability claims felt pretty weak. The case study near Figure 5 is fine, but “valuable chemical insights” is too strong based on one or two qualitative examples. In my experience, these attribution visualizations are easy to over-interpret.

Overall, I think there is a solid empirical paper in here, but I’m not yet sold on the conceptual novelty or the rigor of the comparisons. Lean borderline reject from me, though I can imagine being persuaded if the authors clarify the baseline setup, tone down the novelty claims, and better position the relational objective relative to existing metric-learning style methods.

74.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating to read. The core idea—using a process reward model to provide denser, line-level feedback for code generation RL—is sensible and honestly quite natural. People have been saying for a while that outcome-only rewards for code are too sparse, so in that sense the paper is addressing a real bottleneck. I also appreciated that the paper does not stop at “use PRM as an auxiliary signal,” but tries multiple integration points, especially the value initialization angle. In my experience, that part is often more important than authors expect.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper fully establishes what is new beyond a fairly straightforward adaptation of process supervision ideas to code RL. The presentation around the PRM construction felt under-specified. On page 4, around the section introducing the line-level labels, I wanted much more detail on how these labels are actually obtained and how noisy they are. If the supervision is heuristic or derived from execution traces, then the whole method may be benefiting from a strong source of extra annotation rather than from the RL formulation itself. I might be wrong, but Figure 2 makes the pipeline look cleaner than it probably is in practice.

The empirical gains are real but modest on the main benchmark: 28.2% to 29.8% on LiveCodeBench is not nothing, but it is also not a slam dunk, especially given the added complexity. Table 1 (if I’m reading it right) suggests larger gains on the internal benchmark, which is fine, but that always raises the usual concern about benchmark fit. I would have liked more breakdown by problem difficulty / solution length, since the abstract claims benefits “especially for long-horizon scenarios.” I didn’t see convincing evidence for that beyond a few aggregate statements. A length-binned analysis would help a lot.

I was also confused by the credit assignment story. Equation (3) and the surrounding text seem to mix PRM-based dense rewards with final unit-test rewards, but it is not very clear whether the PRM is shaping behavior in a way that is aligned with actual pass@1. This matters because line-level “correctness” in code can be pretty misleading; locally plausible lines can still lead nowhere.

Overall, I think the paper has a good instinct and some useful engineering, but the novelty is moderate and the evidence is not yet as strong as the claims. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the missing details and analyses are addressed.

75.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, to be honest, more thoughtful than a lot of the recent “just scale the prompt and hope” long-text T2I submissions. The core idea is pretty simple—segment the long prompt, encode pieces separately, then do preference optimization with a decomposed CLIP reward—but I think the authors do enough analysis to make it feel like more than engineering. The discussion around the decomposition of preference scores (around Eq. 6–9, if I read it correctly) was probably the most valuable part for me. In particular, the claim that the text-irrelevant component drives overfitting during alignment tuning is plausible and matches what I’ve seen in RLHF-style fine-tuning for generative models, though here the evidence is still a bit indirect.

I liked the empirical section overall. Table 1 and Table 2 show consistent gains, and the fact that a tuned SD1.5 can outperform stronger base models on alignment is notable. Figure 4 was also helpful in showing failure modes with long prompts. Some of the qualitative examples are genuinely convincing. Not all, though. A few looked cherry-picked to me, especially when the prompt contains many attributes but only 2–3 are visually checkable. I wish the authors had been a bit more honest there.

My main issue is that the paper slightly oversells novelty. Segment-level encoding is kind of the obvious thing to try, and I’m not entirely sure the paper establishes that their particular segmentation/aggregation design is the key ingredient rather than just “more text gets through.” On page 5, the explanation of how segment representations are fused into the diffusion conditioning felt too brief; after reading it twice I still wasn’t fully sure what is frozen, what is learned, and whether positional information across segments is preserved in a meaningful way. This matters. If cross-segment dependencies are weak, then the method may help with prompt length without really solving long-text compositional understanding.

I also have some concerns about evaluation. The paper relies heavily on CLIP-derived or preference-model-derived metrics, while the method itself is built around CLIP-based decomposition. That is a bit circular. Human evaluation is present (Table 3, I think), but it seems limited, and I would have liked more detail on annotator instructions and inter-rater agreement. Also, comparisons to newer long-prompt baselines seem thin. Why not test against LLM-based prompt compression/rewriting pipelines? In practice, that is a strong baseline.

Still, I think this is a solid paper. Not flawless, but useful, and the decomposition angle is more interesting than the title initially suggests. Leaning accept.

76.review_text:
I went into this paper somewhat skeptical because “use multiple exits as experts” sounds like the kind of idea that is either trivially obvious or surprisingly effective, and after reading it twice I think it lands somewhere in between. The basic proposal is easy enough to grasp from Eq. (3)–(5): instead of making an exit decision based only on the current classifier confidence, BEEM aggregates confidence from neighboring exits when their predictions are consistent, and then compares that to a threshold derived from exit error rates. I do think this is a reasonable idea. In fact, I’m a little surprised it hasn’t been explored more directly.

That said, I found the presentation sloppier than it should be. On p. 3, where the “experts” framing is introduced, it sounds more principled than it actually is. This is not really a mixture-of-experts model in the usual sense; it is more like confidence smoothing across exits with a consistency check. That’s fine, but the terminology feels inflated. I also had to work a bit too hard to understand exactly which neighboring exits are used and whether the aggregation is causal at inference time or peeks ahead. I think Fig. 2 is supposed to clarify this, but honestly it did not fully do so for me. Maybe I’m missing something, but the implementation details matter here.

The empirical section is mixed. The results in Tables 2 and 3 do show nontrivial speedup gains over standard EE baselines, and I appreciate that the paper includes both vision (COCO captioning) and NLP (GLUE) rather than only one domain. Still, I was bothered by the evaluation protocol. For GLUE especially, “improves over the final layer” should be treated very carefully because these tasks have variance, and I did not see enough discussion of seeds or confidence intervals. Some of the gains look small enough that they may not be robust. On the other hand, the COCO captioning results seem more convincing, although comparing captioning quality under early exit is messy and the paper moves past that too quickly.

I also think the threshold-setting method on p. 4 is under-justified. It feels heuristic. Maybe that is okay in practice, but then say so plainly. Don’t oversell it as if there is a strong theoretical guarantee.

Overall, I think there is a decent practical contribution here, but the paper currently feels more empirical hack than solid methodological advance. I’m leaning borderline positive because the idea appears useful and the results are mostly credible, but I would want clearer exposition and a more careful ablation before feeling enthusiastic.

77.review_text:
I found the paper interesting but also somewhat under-baked. The core idea — using intermediate interaction traces and “reflection” to iteratively improve agent prompts without requiring a final checker — is timely, and honestly pretty sensible. In practice, a lot of agent settings really do have richer intermediate signals than clean end-task rewards, so I buy the motivation in the abstract and the setup around p.2–3. This is one of those ideas that feels obvious in hindsight, which can be a compliment or a warning sign.

What I struggled with is that the actual method seems less crisp than the paper wants it to be. The “gradient descent-like” framing (around Eq. 1 / the method section, I think p.4) is catchy, but I’m not convinced it is more than an analogy. After reading that section twice, I still don’t have a precise sense of what the optimization variable is beyond “the prompt text,” how the reflection signal is converted into an update, and what prevents the system from drifting toward verbose but not actually better instructions. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. I wanted either pseudocode with a very explicit update rule, or a clearer decomposition of reflection -> critique -> prompt edit. Right now it feels a little hand-wavy.

The experiments are promising but limited. The PDDL generation results in Table 1 seem meaningful, although I wish the authors were much clearer about how intermediate feedback is produced there. Is it parser errors, execution failures, LLM self-critique, or all of the above? These are very different signals. Likewise for travel planning: the gains in Table 2 are nice, but I’m not entirely sure the benchmark is stable enough to support strong claims. In my experience, travel-planning style agent evaluations are extremely sensitive to prompt wording and model versioning, and I didn’t see enough variance reporting or repeated runs. Maybe I missed it.

I also think the paper undersells related work a bit. This sits somewhere between automatic prompt optimization, self-refinement, and agent trajectory learning, and the distinctions from prior reflection-based methods were blurrier than they should be (discussion around p.7 especially). There is novelty here, yes, but it is incremental novelty.

Overall, I lean mildly positive on the idea but not on the current presentation. If accepted, this should be for the practical framing and empirical intuition, not because the method is theoretically clean. Right now it reads more like a workshop paper with a good hook than a fully convincing conference paper.

78.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and I do think the basic question is timely: neural collapse has been studied to death for multiclass problems, but much less is understood in multi-label settings. Extending the geometry to pick-all-label training is, at least on paper, a reasonable and nontrivial direction. The main theorem around the unconstrained feature model (I think this is the result in Sec. 4, around Eq. (8)–(11)?) is the core contribution, and the claim that global minimizers still induce an ETF geometry, with multi-label examples represented by scaled averages of class ETFs, is elegant.

That said, I had a somewhat mixed reaction after reading this twice. The theory is clean, but I’m not convinced the paper fully bridges the gap between the stylized model and actual multilabel deep nets. In particular, the assumptions in the UFM setup seem quite strong, and the jump from those assumptions to the empirical claim of “improved training efficiency” in the abstract feels bigger than what is actually demonstrated. Table 1 / Table 2 (if I’m reading correctly) show some trends, but I wanted more careful ablations and baselines. Improved relative to what exactly? Standard BCE training? Some calibrated multilabel objective? This was a bit slippery.

I also found parts of the exposition confusing. The definition of the pick-all-label loss early in the paper is intuitive, but the notation becomes dense around the theorem statements. On p. 5, near the derivation following Eq. (6), I had to stop and reconstruct what was being optimized over. Maybe that’s on me, but I suspect other readers will also struggle. Figure 2 is helpful visually, though I wish it were tied more explicitly to the theorem assumptions rather than presented as qualitative confirmation.

My bigger concern is novelty relative to prior NC theory. I do think there is novelty here, to be clear, but a lot of the proof strategy feels like a fairly direct lift of known multiclass NC arguments with the label simplex replaced by label subsets/averages. That may still be publishable if the extension is genuinely subtle, but the paper should do a better job explaining where the real new difficulty is. Right now it undersells that, oddly enough.

Overall, I lean positive on the idea and the mathematical result, but I’m less sold on the empirical side and on the strength of the paper’s broader claims. Borderline accept from me, though not enthusiastically.

79.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, though I’m not fully convinced by all of the claims. The basic idea—connecting adversarial training to a Wasserstein DRO formulation via a Lagrangian relaxation—is clean and, at least to me, more principled than a lot of the “just run PGD and hope” style papers. I appreciated the attempt to say something about population robustness rather than only empirical adversarial risk, which is honestly where many papers in this area get hand-wavy very fast.

That said, after reading the theory sections twice, I still think the presentation is harder than it needs to be. The jump from the DRO objective to the practical training rule around Eq. (4)–(6) felt a bit compressed, and I had to go back and forth with the assumptions on smoothness and the definition of the transport cost. I might be wrong, but the guarantees seem to rely on a fairly benign local linearization regime, and this is exactly where adversarial robustness papers often look good on paper and weaker in the actual deep-network setting. The wording in the abstract (“certify robustness for the population loss”) is also a little too strong for what is actually shown. The certification here is not the same thing that many readers will think of as per-example certified robustness.

The experimental section is decent but not overwhelming. Table 1 and the discussion around Figure 2 suggest the method is competitive with heuristic adversarial training, but I would have liked a more careful accounting of attack strength. Were all baselines attacked to convergence under the same threat model? In my experience, robustness comparisons can swing a lot depending on those details, and the paper moves through them a bit quickly. Also, the “little computational or statistical cost relative to ERM” claim seems only partly substantiated. Training-time overhead did not look negligible from the numbers given, unless I misunderstood the setup.

One thing I did like quite a bit is the emphasis on moderate robustness rather than pretending to solve the whole problem. That felt intellectually honest. The theory around smooth losses is probably the main contribution here, and it is nontrivial. Still, the paper undersells the limitations of those assumptions for modern ReLU networks, and overstates the practical certification angle.

Overall, I lean positive because the paper has a real idea and some technical substance, not just benchmark chasing. But I’m hesitant. If accepted, I’d want the authors to tighten the claims, clarify the derivation around the main objective, and be much more explicit about what exactly is and is not being certified.

81.review_text:
I had a fairly hard time with this submission, both technically and in terms of presentation. I’ll be blunt: in its current form I don’t think it is close to conference standard.

The core proposal seems to be using the absolute value as an activation function, i.e. effectively \(f(x)=|x|\). That is an extremely simple idea, and of course not every simple idea is bad, but here I did not find a convincing theoretical or empirical case that this is useful in any broadly meaningful sense. The paper leans heavily on vague claims about “individualization” versus “universality” (already in the abstract, and again around Fig. 1 from what I could tell), but these terms are never defined in a way that lets the reader evaluate them. I read the abstract twice and still could not pin down what precise hypothesis is being tested.

There are also immediate technical concerns. Absolute value is non-differentiable at zero, and unlike ReLU it is symmetric in a way that collapses sign information at the output of the activation rather than “keeping the negative parts,” which is actually the opposite of what the abstract claims. I might be wrong, but saying that \(|x|\) preserves negative information “as equal as the positive parts” seems conceptually confused. It maps both \(+a\) and \(-a\) to the same value. So if anything, the sign is discarded. That issue should have been addressed very explicitly, probably with equations and discussion of backpropagation behavior near zero.

The experiments are too weak to support the conclusions. MNIST with fully connected networks / small CNNs and an autoencoder is about the lowest bar possible in 2026. Table-level comparisons (if I’m interpreting the results correctly) seem to involve only ReLU, leaky ReLU, and the proposed function, with no serious hyperparameter tuning discussion, no variance over seeds, and no statistically meaningful analysis. Claims like “less likely to be over-fitting” need proper learning curves, multiple runs, and stronger datasets. A slightly “shaken” accuracy curve is not evidence of anything.

The writing is, frankly, a major problem. Many sentences are very difficult to parse, and some of the biological motivation feels more like personal philosophy than machine learning. I’m not opposed to intuition from nature, but it has to connect to a formal argument or solid empirical evidence. Here it mostly does not.

Overall, I don’t see novelty, I don’t see rigorous evidence, and I found the main conceptual claim internally inconsistent. My recommendation is rejection.

80.review_text:
I found this paper interesting but also somewhat frustrating. The topic is important — people throw around “diversity” all the time in generation and recommendation papers, and in my experience the evaluation side is much sloppier than authors admit. So I liked the ambition here. The paper is basically a conceptual/axiomatic critique: survey existing diversity measures, show pathologies, propose three axioms (monotonicity, uniqueness, continuity), and then prove impossibility for existing measures while giving two existence constructions that are impractical. That is a reasonable theory paper shape.

That said, after reading this twice, I am still not fully convinced the axioms are the right ones, especially uniqueness. As stated around p.4 (if I’m reading Definition 3 correctly), uniqueness seems to force a very strong identification between the structure of the set and the diversity value. I understand why the authors want to rule out collisions, but this is a huge ask. In practice, many useful functionals are not injective, and I’m not sure “there exist non-isomorphic sets with the same diversity score” is actually a fatal flaw. It feels more like the paper builds toward a negative result by choosing a demanding axiom set. Maybe that is the point, but then I wanted a more serious defense of why these are the right desiderata, not just mathematically clean ones.

I did appreciate the counterexamples to existing measures. The discussion around the failures of common pairwise-distance-based quantities (I think this is in Section 3, with examples near Fig. 1 / Table 1) is nice and concrete. Some of those examples are genuinely instructive. But the survey part also feels a bit selective. There is a lot of older ecological/phylogenetic diversity literature and some determinantal-point-process-adjacent work that seems relevant, and I wasn’t sure the coverage was complete enough to support “none of the existing measures...” as strongly as claimed.

The existence constructions are mathematically fine, but honestly a bit deflating. By the time I got to the final theorem (p.8 or so), the message seemed to be: here are axioms, everything practical fails, here are two impractical objects that satisfy them, open problem. That’s not nothing, but it’s also not a satisfying landing. I might be wrong, but I think the paper would be stronger if it either weakened the axioms to recover something computable, or gave a hardness result explaining why practicality is elusive.

Overall: intellectually serious, but I’m not sold on the central definition of success. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can better justify the axioms and narrow the scope of their impossibility claim.

82.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and honestly a bit refreshing, because it tackles a very practical problem that a lot of vision papers just sidestep by assuming perspective images forever. The core idea—replace standard convs with geometry-aware “RectConv” operators so a pretrained network can be run on fisheye imagery without retraining—is appealing. In spirit it reminds me of older work on spherical/distortion-aware CNNs, but this paper’s emphasis on not retraining at all is the part that stood out to me.

That said, after reading this twice, I still think the presentation leaves some important gaps. The method description around the RectConv construction (especially the transition from the camera model to the resampled receptive field; I think this is around Eq. 3–5 on pages 3–4) is harder to follow than it should be. I understand the intuition, but I’m not entirely sure I could reimplement it correctly from the paper alone without the released code. Figure 2 helps, but not enough. In particular, the way kernel support changes across the image and through layers seems under-specified. Are intermediate feature maps assumed to correspond to rays under the original camera model, or is there an implicit reprojection after every layer? This matters.

I also wasn’t fully convinced by the experimental scope. The results in Tables 1 and 2 do show gains over direct application and naive rectification, which is good, but the baselines feel a bit soft. There is a large literature on distortion-aware/spherical convolution and also on fine-tuning with limited target data. I get that the selling point is “no retraining,” but in practice I would have liked at least one comparison to lightweight adaptation (even a tiny amount of fine-tuning) to calibrate how much performance is being left on the table. Otherwise the paper risks comparing against strawmen. Maybe I missed it, but I also didn’t see much discussion of runtime/memory overhead beyond a brief mention; this should be quantified. If RectConv is 3x slower, that changes the story.

I did like that the method is tested on both segmentation and detection, and Figure 5 qualitatively makes the failure mode of naive rectification very obvious. The software tool angle is also valuable. In my experience, papers like this can end up being more useful than flashier “novel architecture” papers.

Overall, I lean positive on novelty and practical relevance, but I’m not fully sold on the rigor of the evaluation or the clarity of the technical exposition. Borderline accept for me, though I could be persuaded either way depending on author rebuttal.

83.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and in a slightly old-fashioned way it is trying to answer a question many of us have had for a while: why are we still acting as if GCN-style averaging and GAT-style attention are fundamentally separate design choices? The proposed interpolation idea in L-CAT is simple, and I mean that as a compliment. After reading Sec. 3 twice, I think the core mechanism is basically “let the model decide, per layer, where to sit on the spectrum between GCN, GAT, and their proposed CAT.” That is a sensible contribution, and the fact that it adds only two scalars per layer is appealing.

That said, I’m less convinced by some of the framing than by the method itself. The claim around “there exists no clear winner” felt overstated. Sure, the empirical section supports this at a high level, but the theoretical discussion around expressivity/behavior (I’m referring to the analysis around Eq. (6)-(9), and the discussion near p. 4) is a bit too hand-wavy for such a broad statement. I might be wrong, but it reads more like an informed observation than a theorem with real bite. In my experience, papers often overclaim robustness/generality when the actual contribution is a smart architectural tweak.

I did like Figure 1 and Table 2. Figure 1 in particular makes the interpolation idea very clear. But some of the experimental choices are odd. On the benchmark side, the paper uses a decent spread of datasets, yet I wanted a cleaner accounting of hyperparameter budgets. The whole sales pitch is “reduced need for cross-validation,” but if L-CAT still benefits from similar tuning effort on hidden size, dropout, learning rate, number of heads, etc., then that claim should be toned down. Table 3 hints at robustness, but I wanted variance across seeds more consistently reported. Some results look close enough that this matters.

Another issue: the CAT layer itself is not, in my opinion, especially compelling as a standalone contribution. It feels mostly instrumental, i.e., useful because it completes the interpolation family. That’s okay, but then the paper should say that more plainly instead of trying to sell CAT as a major new primitive.

Overall I lean positive because the idea is practical, lightweight, and likely useful to practitioners. I can imagine people actually using this. But I also think the paper would be stronger with less marketing language and a more careful empirical story. Borderline accept.

84.review_text:
I went into this paper somewhat skeptical because “LLM + classical sequential model” papers are everywhere right now, and a lot of them are basically repackaging. This one is better than that, but I still have fairly mixed feelings after reading it twice.

The core idea—using textual event descriptions instead of fixed categorical IDs for TPP modeling—is actually reasonable, and in some application domains probably the right thing to do. I liked the motivation around semantic similarity between event types, which standard marked TPPs usually throw away. The discussion early in the paper (around p.2–3) is clear enough, and Figure 1 does a decent job of communicating the setup. I also appreciate that the authors did not just fully finetune a huge model and call it a contribution; the PEFT angle makes practical sense.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper really demonstrates a specifically “TPP” advance rather than a fairly generic text-augmented sequence model. The temporal modeling component felt underdeveloped to me. Equation (4) / the temporal embedding construction is presented as the key mechanism that compensates for LLMs being weak on timing, but in the experiments I had trouble isolating whether this is genuinely learning point-process structure or just adding time features in a standard way. Maybe I missed it, but I wanted a more explicit connection to conditional intensity modeling, or at least a clearer explanation of what is inherited from the TPP literature versus what is just next-event prediction with timestamps.

I also found the experimental section a bit slippery. Table 2 shows improvements, yes, but some margins are not large, and it’s hard to tell whether the baselines are equally advantaged by access to text. If the non-LLM baselines only get event IDs while TPP-LLM gets rich descriptions, that is not a clean comparison. The ablations in Table 3 help, but I still wanted a stronger control: e.g., replace the LLM with a much smaller text encoder, or randomize / paraphrase event descriptions to test whether semantics are truly driving gains. In my experience, these details matter a lot and often collapse the story.

There are also some writing issues. The claims around efficiency on p.6 felt overstated. PEFT is cheaper than full finetuning, sure, but the base model is still large, and inference cost matters in TPP applications. I’m also not entirely sure Figure 3 is doing much; I stared at it for a while and didn’t learn much beyond “our method is better on average.”

Overall, I think this is a solid idea with some empirical promise, but the paper currently oversells how novel the temporal component is. I’d lean weak reject / borderline. With tighter baselines and a more principled treatment of the point-process aspect, I could see this becoming a strong paper.

85.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because there is a real shortage of theory papers on adversarial training that actually say something non-asymptotic about the full bilevel procedure rather than just one stylized component. The main claim here — convergence guarantees for PGD-based adversarial training for two-layer Leaky ReLU networks, for any width and initialization, in the realizable setting — is potentially quite strong. If true in the stated generality, it is a meaningful result.

That said, I found the paper harder to parse than it needed to be, and I’m still not fully convinced by the practical significance of the main construction. The key idea, as I understand it from Sec. 3 and especially around Eq. (6)–(8), is to replace the usual inner maximization surrogate by a reflected loss that lower bounds the 0/1 adversarial loss. This is clever, and I do think the lower-bound perspective is mathematically neat. But it also feels somewhat tailored to make the proof go through. After reading pp. 4–5 twice, I’m still unsure whether this objective corresponds to anything one would actually want to attack with in practice, or whether it mostly serves as an analyzable proxy. The distinction matters, because the title sounds broader than what is really shown.

I also think the assumptions deserve more scrutiny. The realizable setting is doing a lot of work here. So is the very specific architecture choice. Two-layer Leaky ReLU is already restrictive; the fact that the slope parameter seems essential in several lemmas (I think Lemma 4.2 and the discussion right after, around line 210-ish) makes me wonder how brittle the argument is. In my experience, many such proofs break completely for standard ReLU, and if that is the case here, the paper should be more upfront about it. Figure 1 is helpful intuitively, but I wanted a clearer explanation of why the reflected surrogate is the “right” one rather than just a convenient one.

The theorem statements are reasonably clean, and the iteration complexity result in Table 1 is nice to have, but I would have liked at least a small empirical sanity check. Not a giant experimental section, just something showing that the proposed inner-loop objective behaves similarly to PGD on standard surrogates or that the convergence rates are not vacuous. Right now the paper is very theory-heavy in a way that makes it difficult to assess impact.

Overall, I think there is technical merit here, and some of the arguments look nontrivial. But I’m torn. The result is narrow, the presentation is dense, and the connection to practical adversarial training is weaker than the abstract suggests. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the authors can better justify the modeling choices and scope.

87.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea — doing population-based AutoRL for off-policy methods while sharing replay data across the population — is sensible and, frankly, kind of obvious in hindsight. For off-policy RL, not sharing samples across candidates always felt wasteful. So I do think there is a real contribution here, especially from a practical standpoint. The claim in the abstract about “up to an order of magnitude” fewer environment interactions than standard PBT seems plausible given the setup.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper nails down what exactly is new algorithmically versus what is a careful engineering combination of existing ingredients. The method description around the population updates and hyperparameter/architecture mutation (I think this is around Eq. 3 and the paragraph immediately after it) reads pretty close to population-based training plus replay sharing. That may still be enough for acceptance if the empirical case is strong, but then the empirical section has to be airtight, and I don’t think it quite is.

In particular, the evaluation seems very concentrated on TD3 + MuJoCo. Table 1 and Fig. 2 make the sample-efficiency story look good, but MuJoCo is also the friendliest possible setting for off-policy replay reuse. I kept wondering how much of the gain is just because all members benefit from a shared replay buffer in a low-diversity benchmark suite. I would have liked at least one harder domain, or some evidence that this does not collapse when the population’s policies become very different. The discussion near page 6 on experience sharing sort of gestures at this, but does not really analyze it. Is there any downside from off-distribution contamination between architectures/hyperparameters? Maybe not, but then please show it.

I also thought the neural architecture optimization part was underdeveloped relative to the headline claims. The paper says it optimizes architecture and hyperparameters simultaneously, but the architecture search space seems quite limited (unless I missed details in the appendix), and the ablations don’t really isolate whether architecture adaptation matters. Fig. 4 is suggestive, not convincing.

On the positive side, I liked that the method is general to arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms. In my experience, many AutoRL papers oversell generality and then only work for one brittle setup; this one at least seems more grounded. The writing is mostly clear, though a few definitions on page 3 are overloaded and I found the notation a little messy.

Overall I lean positive but not strongly. Useful idea, decent results, but the paper overclaims novelty and breadth a bit. I’d probably recommend borderline accept if the conference values practical AutoML-for-RL papers; otherwise borderline reject.

88.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more ambitious than the average “yet another GNS variant” submission. The core idea — wrapping MeshGraphNet-style simulation in a meta-learning setup and predicting trajectory structure via movement primitives rather than only one-step rollouts — makes sense to me, especially for low-data adaptation. In practice, this is exactly where a lot of learned simulators fall apart. So the problem is real.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the paper in its current form. The presentation is uneven. I had to read the method section twice to understand what exactly is latent, what is task-specific, and how the conditional Neural Process interfaces with the graph simulator. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text on p. 4 are doing a lot of work, but the notation is pretty compressed, and I think the authors assume too much familiarity with both Neural Processes and MGN-style simulators. Figure 2 helps, but not enough. In particular, I was confused about whether the latent variable is inferred once per trajectory/task or updated autoregressively. I think it is task-level only, but the wording around lines 168–184 made me hesitate.

I do think the movement primitive angle is the most compelling part. Predicting full trajectories rather than repeatedly compounding one-step errors is a sensible design choice, and Table 1 does suggest this matters. Still, the comparisons are a little awkward. The baselines seem mostly step-based, so of course a trajectory-level parameterization may have an advantage under long-horizon rollout metrics. I would have liked at least one stronger trajectory-prediction baseline, or even a non-meta version of the same primitive-based architecture to disentangle the gains. Maybe this is in the ablations on p. 6/Table 2, but if so it is not emphasized clearly.

Another issue is evaluation breadth. The abstract says “a variety of experiments,” but after reading the experiments section I’m not sure the diversity is that high. There are multiple scenarios, yes, but they seemed fairly close in flavor. I might be wrong here because the task definitions are a bit buried. Also, I would have appreciated more discussion of computational overhead: NP-style adaptation is not free, and one attraction of GNS in engineering is practical throughput.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and parts of the empirical results are promising. But the paper currently overclaims a bit relative to how clearly it isolates the source of improvement. Borderline for me, leaning weak accept if the bar is novelty, weak reject if the bar is rigor/clarity.

86.review_text:
I had mixed reactions to this paper. The high-level motivation in the abstract is interesting — yes, in many realistic settings the reward is the hard part, not the policy optimization — but after reading the paper twice I’m still not convinced the actual contribution matches the ambition. The title promises “harmonic learning in high-dimensional MDPs,” and the abstract claims a “theoretically well-founded algorithm” with significant sample complexity gains, but a lot of the technical development felt underspecified or, frankly, hand-wavy.

The main issue for me is that the problem formulation itself is not crisp enough. On p. 2, around lines 78–96, the paper says the learner has “no access to information on rewards provided by the MDP,” but then in Sec. 3 the proposed objective seems to optimize a surrogate signal that is clearly task-dependent. I might be wrong, but this reads less like reward-free RL in the standard sense and more like replacing the external reward with a learned energy / consistency objective. That can be fine, but then the paper should say so directly. As written, it overclaims. Eq. (4) is especially important here, and I found the notation confusing: the harmonic operator is introduced as if it were canonical, but it is really a design choice, and the assumptions needed for Theorem 1 are much stronger than the surrounding text suggests.

I also struggled with the theory. The sample complexity statement on p. 5 only seems meaningful under fairly restrictive representation assumptions, and I don’t see how those assumptions connect to the deep neural policy setting in the experiments. This is a common problem in RL papers, to be fair, but here the abstract leans heavily on the theory, so I think the gap matters. The proof sketch for Theorem 2 is too abbreviated to evaluate; several steps are pushed to the appendix, and even there I wasn’t fully able to reconstruct the argument.

On the empirical side, Figure 2 and Table 1 show gains, but the baselines seem oddly chosen. Why compare mostly against fairly old methods rather than stronger recent reward-free / unsupervised RL baselines? In my experience, if a method is truly robust in high-dimensional settings, it should survive comparisons to modern world-model or intrinsic-control approaches. Also, the environments are not that “uncertain” in any serious sense. That claim felt inflated.

There is probably an interesting idea here, and I did like the intuition in Fig. 1. But the paper currently feels caught between a theory paper and a deep RL benchmark paper, and doesn’t fully land as either. My inclination is rejection in its current form, mostly because the core claim is not presented with enough clarity or rigor.

89.review_text:
I’m a bit torn on this paper. The core question—how local perturbations in some pairwise preference probabilities propagate through a fitted preference model—is actually important, and in the alignment literature people often wave away this issue. So I appreciate the attempt to isolate a very specific failure mode. But after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the contribution is deep enough, or that the practical alignment claims are really supported.

The technical content seems to be centered on sensitivity properties of Bradley–Terry and Plackett–Luce under “strong” preferences, and the main message is that probabilities near 0/1 can induce large downstream changes elsewhere. Fine. But this is also not very surprising. These models are log-odds style parameterizations; once you push latent scores apart, small parameter changes can have amplified effects on implied comparisons. I kept waiting for a result that felt genuinely non-obvious or for a sharper characterization of when this matters in realistic data regimes. The abstract promises “specific conditions,” but at least as I understood the derivations around Eq. (3) and the later propositions (I think on pp. 4–6), the conditions are fairly generic and a bit tautological: sensitivity is high when probabilities are extreme and connected through the model structure. Yes, but then what?

I also found the framing too broad relative to the evidence. The title and abstract repeatedly invoke “value alignment” and “safety,” but the paper, at least in the version I read, is basically a theoretical note about classical preference models. That’s not the same thing. There is a jump from “these probabilistic ranking models can be sensitive” to “alignment may be brittle,” and I don’t think that jump is justified without either empirical demonstrations on LLM preference data or some argument about the training pipelines actually used in RLHF / DPO-style systems. Table 1 (if I’m remembering correctly) felt more illustrative than dispositive.

I might be wrong here, but I also think the paper under-discusses identifiability / gauge issues and whether the sensitivity measure is invariant to the parameterization chosen. That matters. Figure 1 helped a little, though I found the notation around the perturbation setup somewhat dense.

Overall: interesting question, decent math, but the paper feels narrow and overclaims impact. If positioned as a concise theoretical analysis of sensitivity in BT/PL models, I’d be warmer to it. As a top-conference paper on alignment robustness, I’m not sold. My leaning is weak reject.

90.review_text:
I like the core idea here more than I expected to. The paper is asking a pretty simple question—can we make retrievers behave more like instruction-tuned LMs—and, at least empirically, the answer seems to be yes. I’ve seen a lot of “LLMs but for retrieval” papers recently, and many are frankly cosmetic. This one feels more substantive.

The strongest part, in my view, is that the paper does not just claim “instruction following” in a vague sense. The FollowIR and InstructIR results are actually relevant to the claim. Table 2 and Table 3 (if I’m tracking the numbering correctly) show meaningful gains, and the robustness angle is especially interesting. The +12.9 Robustness@10 number from the abstract is the kind of result that made me pay attention. Figure 1 also does a decent job motivating the use case, though it is a bit sales-y.

That said, I had several concerns after reading this twice. First, the novelty is a little slippery. The paper says “first retrieval model able to be prompted like an LM,” but I’m not entirely sure that is as cleanly established as the authors imply. We’ve already had instruction-aware embedding/retrieval models, query rewriting approaches, and various prompt-conditioned retrievers in adjacent work. Maybe this is the first instance-level instruction-tuned dense retriever trained at this scale, which would be a more defensible claim. As written, it felt overstated.

I also found the training setup under-explained. The dataset curation from MS MARCO is important to the whole paper, but the discussion around instruction generation/annotation (around the method section, I think p. 4–5) felt too quick. What exactly are the instruction categories, and how noisy are they? Are these natural instructions or templated paraphrases?
