I found the core idea interesting and, honestly, pretty sensible: use language to specify what matters in state, then let an LM help instantiate an abstraction before doing imitation learning. This is a nice framing, and it targets a real bottleneck. In robotics especially, people absolutely do hand-engineer these abstractions, just quietly and expensively. So the problem is worth tackling.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the technical contribution. The method description around the LM-to-abstraction translation feels under-specified. Eq. (2) on p.4 gives the abstraction function in a fairly clean symbolic way, but the actual mechanism by which the LM maps a task description to the mask is a bit hand-wavy. Is this prompting plus heuristic parsing? constrained decoding? program synthesis? I think Fig. 2 is trying to clarify the pipeline, but it still leaves too much hidden in the box labeled LGA. In my experience, papers in this area often bury the real source of performance in those “translation” details.

The experimental section is mixed for me. Table 1 and Table 2 look good at first glance, especially on the spurious-correlation settings, and I do believe the claim that abstraction helps generalization. That part tracks with prior work. But I’m not sure the paper isolates the effect of language guidance versus just giving the learner a better inductive bias by feature selection. The comparison to manually designed abstractions is useful, but the paper needs a stronger baseline where the same masking budget is learned from demos without language, or perhaps from a generic LM prior not conditioned on the task description. Otherwise, I can’t tell whether language is actually doing the heavy lifting.

I also found the “ambiguous specifications” story a bit slippery. The examples around p.6–7 are intuitive, but I wanted a more systematic analysis of failure modes. When the language is wrong, incomplete, or subtly misleading, what happens? The abstract says “possibly incomplete” descriptions, but the robustness there is not really nailed down. Fig. 4 is suggestive, not definitive.

The Spot robot demonstration is nice and helps the paper feel less toy-ish, though I’ll admit it reads a bit like a showcase rather than a rigorous evaluation.

Overall, I lean positive on the idea but weaker on the evidence. This feels like a promising workshop paper or an early conference paper, but for a top venue I wanted more methodological transparency and tighter baselines. I might be wrong, but right now the paper seems more compelling conceptually than empirically.

122.review_text:
I went into this paper fairly sympathetic to the goal. The core motivation — CNNs latching onto context rather than object evidence — is real, and in medical imaging / small-data settings this is not just a cute robustness issue. I’ve seen too many papers wave at “causality” here though, and this one does that a bit too casually.

The main idea, as I understand it after reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, is to use sparse human-provided activation masks to bias intermediate feature learning toward regions/features a human considers relevant. That is reasonable. In fact, I think there is a potentially useful practical contribution here. But the paper oversells it as “causal” in a way that is not justified by either the method or the experiments. Human annotations of salient regions are not the same thing as causal variables. At best this is a human-guided anti-shortcut regularizer. That may sound harsh, but I think terminology matters.

I also found the method description somewhat under-specified. Equation (3) introduces the additional loss on masked activations, but it is not very clear to me how the mask is aligned with feature maps of different resolutions, or whether this is applied to one layer or multiple layers. Figure 2 helps a bit, but not enough. I might be missing something in the implementation details on page 6, but right now I don’t think I could reproduce this cleanly. Relatedly, the “minimal” in the title is never really quantified in a convincing way. Table 2 varies the amount of guidance, which is good, but I wanted a clearer annotation budget analysis: how many minutes per image, how coarse can the masks be, how sensitive is performance?

The empirical section is mixed. The gains in Table 1 are promising, especially in the lower-data regime, and the qualitative visualizations in Figure 4 do suggest reduced background reliance. Still, the baselines feel a bit dated and incomplete. If the claim is about avoiding spurious context, I would expect comparisons to more recent debiasing / attention supervision methods, not just vanilla CNN training and a small set of attribution-based variants. Also, some of the improvements are modest, and I’m not entirely sure the statistical significance is established.

Overall, I think there is a decent idea here, but the paper in its current form is not fully convincing. The practical method may have value. The causal framing, however, is too strong, and the experimental validation needs to be tighter. I’m leaning borderline reject unless the authors can substantially clarify the method and tone down the claims.

123.review_text:
I went into this paper pretty interested, because sequential experimentation with delayed / time-varying effects is a real problem in marketplaces, and most industry A/B testing pipelines are frankly pretty primitive about it. The paper has an appealing goal. But after reading it twice, I’m still not convinced the proposed “reinforcement learning framework” is really an RL contribution, or even that the methodology is presented clearly enough to assess.

My biggest issue is conceptual. On p. 2–3 the paper motivates the setting as long-term causal effect evaluation with sequential monitoring, which makes sense. But when I got to the actual procedure (around Eq. (3)–(6), if I’m tracking correctly), the RL angle felt mostly cosmetic. There is some adaptive updating, yes, but I did not see a genuinely sequential decision-making formulation with a meaningful state/action/reward structure beyond what could have been described as adaptive experimental design or sequential testing. I might be missing something, but if this is meant for an ML audience, the paper needs to be much more honest and precise about what part is “RL” versus standard online estimation/inference.

I also found the assumptions slippery. The marketplace setting clearly has interference and equilibrium effects, yet the theory seems to rely on fairly strong independence/stationarity conditions (p. 5, around the theorem statements). Those assumptions may make the results go through, but they also seem to strip away the exact complications that make two-sided platforms hard. In my experience, this is where many papers overclaim. The title suggests time dependent causal effects in A/B testing generally, but the actual theory appears to cover a much narrower regime.

The empirical section didn’t rescue it for me. The synthetic experiments in Table 1 and Figure 2 show some gains, but the baselines are weak and not especially current. I kept wondering: where are the obvious comparisons to standard group sequential methods, CUPED-style variance reduction, or adaptive allocation methods used in practice? The real-data case study is interesting, but frustratingly under-described. I couldn’t tell from the discussion around Figure 3 what exactly was randomized, over what horizon, and whether the observed “long-term effect” could be separated from temporal confounding. The paper says the method is “generally applicable,” but the evidence is much more anecdotal.

There is probably a kernel of a useful paper here. I do like the problem and parts of the theoretical ambition. But right now it feels oversold, under-specified, and somewhat mismatched to the RL framing. My recommendation is negative in its current form.

124.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit refreshing. The basic idea—avoid directly forcing image and point cloud features into one metric space, and instead “translate” both into a more comparable representation using pretrained models—is sensible and, in my view, closer to how these foundation-model pipelines are actually starting to work in practice. After reading the method section twice, I think the core contribution is really the combination of diffusion features for coarse semantic alignment and depth-based geometric features for refinement. That part came across clearly, especially around Fig. 2 and the discussion near Eq. (3)–(6).

What I liked most is that the paper does not oversell training tricks. The “training-free” angle is meaningful here, not just marketing. The results in Table 1 and Table 2 are strong, especially the registration recall gains. A 48.6% average improvement is not subtle. In my experience reviewing registration papers, many methods improve one metric while quietly getting worse on others, but here the gains seem fairly consistent across indoor and outdoor settings, which gives me more confidence than usual. I also appreciated that the paper tests across different benchmarks rather than tuning the whole story to one dataset.

That said, I do have some reservations. First, I’m not entirely sure the paper fully explains why the intermediate diffusion features should be semantically aligned between RGB images and projected depth in a way that is stable enough for correspondence. The empirical evidence is decent (the visualizations in Fig. 3 help), but the argument still feels a little hand-wavy. Maybe that’s fine, but then I would have liked a sharper ablation on which diffusion layer(s) matter most, and how sensitive the method is to the choice of pretrained model. I may have missed it, but the discussion around this on page 6 felt thinner than it should be.

Second, the dependence on monocular depth estimation is a bit of a potential Achilles’ heel. On paper this is elegant; in messy real scenes, monocular depth can be very wrong. The robustness analysis to depth failure modes seemed limited. Also, runtime/compute is not discussed enough. Using a diffusion model plus depth estimator is not cheap, and for a “free” method the practical cost matters.

Overall, I think this is a strong paper. Not perfect, and some of the intuition is still more empirical than principled, but the idea is novel enough and the gains are convincing enough that I lean positive. If accepted, I suspect people will try this baseline immediately, which is often a good sign.

125.review_text:
I found the paper interesting in premise, because “data pruning without fully annotating everything first” is actually a pretty practical problem, and object detection makes it harder than standard classification pruning. In that sense the paper is tackling something real, not a toy setup. The high-level idea of doing pruning in an annotation-aware way, with hidden-object mining plus class balancing plus similarity filtering, makes intuitive sense.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced by either the novelty or the experimental evidence. The method description around the two-phase pipeline felt a bit hand-wavy in places. In particular, the hard sample mining module (around Sec. 3.2, and I think Eq. (4)-(6)) seems to rely on signals that already presume a reasonably good detector and partially available annotations. I might be wrong, but then the savings claim becomes murky: how much manual annotation effort is actually avoided versus shifted into model bootstrapping? The paper talks about reducing both annotation and storage burdens, but I did not see a clean accounting of annotation cost. A plot of performance against actual human labeling hours would have helped a lot.

I also had trouble parsing the global similarity removal part. Figure 3 gives the pipeline, but the object-level similarity metric is not motivated very deeply. Why this representation and not alternatives? And for detection, redundancy is tricky: two visually similar objects can still be important if context differs. The paper acknowledges this a bit, but Table 2/3 mostly reports final AP with pruning rates and doesn’t really isolate failure cases. I wanted an ablation where each module is removed more systematically; Table 4 goes in that direction, but it’s still not enough for me.

On experiments: it is nice that the paper includes both 2D and 3D detection, and I appreciate that. But the “minimum pruning rate of 20%” claim in the abstract is oddly framed. Twenty percent is not especially aggressive by the standards of pruning papers, so the bar here should be stronger evidence of negligible loss and robust transfer across detectors. I’m not entirely sure that is shown. Some variance/statistical stability is also missing; several gains/losses are small enough that I’d want repeated runs.

Overall, I think the paper is promising but currently under-baked. The idea is practical, but the methodology needs sharper justification and the evaluation needs to be more honest about annotation-cost savings. Borderline reject from me.

126.review_text:
I think the paper is tackling a real gap. In few-shot learning, people have absolutely hammered cross-domain transfer, but spurious correlation in FSC is much less systematically evaluated, so the core motivation here made sense to me immediately. The idea of constructing a benchmark from “concept-context” pairs is, at least at a high level, useful and timely. I also appreciate that the benchmark is based on real images rather than synthetic color/background hacks, which frankly have become a bit tired.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as solid as it needs to be for a top venue. The benchmark construction details are not always clear enough. On p.4–5, where you describe the data collection and filtering process, I wanted much more specificity about how concepts and contexts were selected, and especially how ambiguous cases were handled. Figure 2 helps, but only a bit. Some of the examples look plausible, others look noisy, and I couldn’t tell how often the “context” is actually semantically entangled with the concept rather than genuinely spurious. In my experience, this distinction is where these benchmark papers often live or die.

I also have mixed feelings about the CLIP-based metric for quantifying spurious shift (I think this is Eq. (3) on p.6?). It’s clever, yes, but also circular in a way: you are using a powerful pretrained model with its own biases to certify the benchmark’s shift severity. I might be wrong, but this risks measuring CLIP’s notion of concept-context association rather than the dataset’s actual causal structure. The paper acknowledges limitations briefly, but not enough. Table 1 reports the metric values, but I wanted some human validation or at least correlation with annotator judgments.

The experiments are broad, and Table 2/Table 3 do show substantial drops under SC-FSC, which is the main empirical takeaway. But the experimental section felt a bit benchmark-y in the less flattering sense: lots of methods, many numbers, not enough insight. Why exactly do some self-supervised methods degrade less? Why do CD-FSC methods fail here—is it because they still latch onto context? Figure 4 hints at this, but the analysis remains shallow.

Also, small thing, the writing needs cleanup. The abstract has grammatical issues (“Despite CD-FSC has been...”), and there are several places where I had to reread sentences.

Overall, I like the problem and I do think the community could use this benchmark. But I’m not yet persuaded that the benchmark definition and shift metric are rigorous enough. Borderline for me, leaning weak reject unless the camera-ready-level details are substantially stronger than what’s currently written.

128.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, but also a bit frustrating. The core idea—doing video compression with a sequential VAE and explicitly separating local/per-frame latents from global/per-segment latents—is sensible, and in spirit it fits a lot of recent progress in learned image compression. I think the paper’s main technical contribution is around the probabilistic modeling/entropy coding side rather than some radically new architecture, and that is fine. In fact, I appreciated the attempt to make the latent structure interpretable. Figure 1 and the discussion around the generative model (around p.2–3) helped.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper is as strong as the claims suggest. The presentation is a bit uneven. Equation (3) / the ELBO decomposition is standard, but the transition from the variational objective to an actual bitrate estimate is too quick; I wanted a more careful explanation of what is actually coded at test time and how mismatch between learned prior and empirical marginal affects rate. Maybe this is obvious to people in compression, but it should not be left implicit. I also found the local/global split under-justified. Table 1 suggests gains, yes, but I’m not entirely sure whether the improvement comes from the semantic distinction or just from giving the model more latent capacity and a better prior. An ablation with matched total latent dimensionality would help a lot.

The experiments are okay, not great. The paper admits this is on small public videos, and that limitation matters. The “competitive” claim feels a little soft because comparisons to standard codecs are narrow and, from what I can tell on p.5–6 and Figure 4, mostly in regimes favorable to the model. I might be wrong, but I did not see enough evidence that this would generalize beyond low-resolution or short-horizon settings. Also, the specialized-content result is unsurprising: if you train on similar videos, of course compression gets very good. That is not a knock, but it should be framed more modestly.

One thing I did like was the temporal prior design; it is cleaner than some earlier sequential VAE papers I’ve read, and in my experience these models often collapse to framewise coding unless pushed carefully. So the fact that they got nontrivial use of both global and local latents is a positive sign.

Overall I lean mildly positive on novelty and technical soundness, but weak on empirical validation. If accepted, I would want the camera-ready to clarify the coding procedure, add stronger ablations, and tone down some of the broader compression claims.

127.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because I’ve seen a lot of geometry-for-typing papers over the years, and the basic instinct here — using hyper-rectangles to reflect ontology structure and type inclusion — is actually pretty reasonable. The idea is not crazy at all. In fact, on page 3 when the authors introduce the hierarchical hyper-rectangle mass representation, I thought “okay, this could be a cleaner alternative to some of the overly elaborate hyperbolic constructions people keep proposing.” Figure 1 also helps; it gives an intuitive picture of containment and overlap, though I wish it were tied more explicitly to the actual scoring function later.

That said, I found the paper harder to trust than to like. A lot of the writing is vague or overstated. The abstract says hRM “perfectly depicts” features of mentions and types, which is just not a serious scientific claim. Similar language appears later around page 5. I might be wrong, but this overclaiming made me scrutinize the technical parts more carefully, and there are places where the method is underspecified. For example, around Eq. (6)–(8), I could not fully reconstruct how the mass of a mention rectangle is normalized and how this interacts with the hierarchy constraints during training. Is the parent-child consistency enforced explicitly, or is it only expected to emerge from the geometry? After reading this twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure.

The experimental section is decent but not fully convincing. The headline “SOTA on two datasets” is fine, but Table 2 and Table 3 need more context: are the baselines using the same encoder backbone, same supervision, same ontology resources? Some seem stronger than others, and the comparison does not feel fully apples-to-apples. Also, the gains are not huge. The ablations in Table 4 are useful, especially for the hypervolume indicator and adaptive threshold, but they also suggest those heuristics carry a lot of the improvement. In other words, I’m not convinced the geometric model itself is the main reason for the gains.

Another issue is novelty. Box embeddings / rectangle-style geometric representations for hierarchies are not new, and this paper does not do enough to position itself against that literature. The distinction between prior box-based semantics and this “mass” formulation is, frankly, blurry in the current draft. Page 2 mentions related geometric methods, but the discussion is thin.

Overall, I think there is a kernel of a good paper here, and I do like the general direction. But in its current form, I see too much ambiguity in the method and too much puffery in the presentation. Borderline to weak reject for me.

129.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more thoughtful than the usual “here is yet another anti-oversmoothing trick” submission. The main idea — reframing a broad family of anti-oversmoothing methods as message passing on signed graphs, and then interpreting their behavior through structural balance — is genuinely appealing. I’ve read a lot of oversmoothing papers over the years, and many of them feel disconnected from each other; this paper at least tries to put them in one conceptual box. That part worked for me.

The theory sections are the strongest part, especially the asymptotic characterization around the signed propagation operators (I’m referring in particular to the discussion around Eq. (4)–(7), and the structural balance result later in Sec. 4). I think the connection between “loss of sign” and homogenization is insightful. Figure 2 also helped; without it I might not have bought the intuition as quickly. That said, I had to read parts of Sec. 3 twice. The notation gets a bit slippery, and I’m still not entirely sure the paper clearly separates what is proven for the linearized propagation dynamics versus what is claimed for actual trained deep GNNs. This is a very common issue in this literature, but it matters.

I also have some concerns about how broad the “unified understanding” claim really is. The paper says a wide class of methods can be viewed this way, but the mapping sometimes feels post hoc. For some architectures, especially those where normalization, residual pathways, or attention are doing heavy lifting, I’m not convinced the signed-graph interpretation is the right explanatory object rather than an approximate analogy. Maybe I’m wrong, but the paper overstates this a bit.

On the empirical side, the results look solid but not fully convincing. Table 2 and Table 3 show gains, yes, but they are not always dramatic, and I wanted more evidence that the improvements are specifically due to increased structural balance rather than just another form of label/feature-guided regularization. The ablation around balance enhancement (I think in Fig. 4 / Sec. 5.4) goes in the right direction, but I still wanted a cleaner causal story. Also, using label information to improve propagation always makes me a little nervous unless the train/val/test protocol is spelled out very carefully.

Overall, I think this is a good paper, probably above the bar, mostly because the framing is novel and the theory is more substantive than average. But I would encourage the authors to tone down some claims and clarify the exact scope of the theoretical conclusions. My score is weak accept.

130.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, more useful than many “LS hurts uncertainty” papers I’ve seen because it at least tries to explain the mechanism instead of just plotting risk-coverage curves and calling it a day. The main empirical claim — that label smoothing consistently hurts selective classification — felt believable to me, and the breadth of experiments seems reasonably strong. I especially appreciated the discussion around the rank ordering of correct vs. incorrect predictions; that is the right object to focus on for SC, not just calibration in the usual sense.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the causal story as currently presented. The gradient analysis around the logits (I think this is in Sec. 4, around Eq. (6)–(9)?) is suggestive, but it still reads a bit like a local training-dynamics argument being used to explain a global test-time ranking phenomenon. Maybe that’s fine, but I wanted a cleaner bridge. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t entirely sure whether the argument is “LS necessarily compresses the max-logit gap more for easy/correct examples” or just “often does so under the observed regime.” Those are different claims. The wording in the abstract and early introduction is stronger than what I think the analysis really supports.

I also think the paper overstates “consistently” unless the appendix is truly exhaustive. Table 1 / Fig. 2 (if I’m recalling correctly) do show the trend across architectures and datasets, but I wanted at least one case where LS does not hurt much, or some discussion of when the effect is weak. In my experience, these phenomena are rarely universal once you sweep smoothing strength, augmentation, and model scale hard enough. The paper would be stronger if it admitted that.

The post-hoc logit normalization fix is practical, which I like. But it is also a bit unsatisfying. It’s cheap and apparently effective, yes, but it feels more like “here is a decent patch” than a real fix. I wanted more comparison to temperature scaling, margin rescaling, or even training-time alternatives. Why should practitioners prefer this over simpler confidence correction baselines? The empirical section touches this, but not deeply enough.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper, probably above the bar. The problem matters, the empirical results seem real, and the explanation is better than average. I’m just not convinced the theory is as definitive as the paper wants it to be, and some of the claims should be toned down. Still, I learned something from it, which is more than I can say for a lot of submissions in this area.

131.review_text:
I went into this paper reasonably interested because agent-based economic simulation with LLMs is a genuinely promising direction, and the “preference-driven” angle is the right instinct. But after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the work is either conceptually clean or empirically solid enough.

The core claim — that standard LLM agents are “rationality-driven” whereas humans are “preference-driven” (abstract, and then again around p. 2) — feels overstated and a bit muddled. In my experience, current LLM agents are not especially rational in any economist’s sense; they are prompt-following sequence models with whatever heuristics the setup induces. So the paper starts from a premise that sounds plausible at a high level but is actually slippery. I kept waiting for a precise formalization of “preference” in the model. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text on p. 5 seem to define a weighted utility over goals, but then the “knowledge brain” is described more like a memory/retrieval/planning module than a preference-learning mechanism. These are not the same thing.

I also found the architecture description frustratingly vague. Figure 2 gives the boxes: knowledge brain, self-learning, reflection, experience update, etc., but not enough implementation detail to understand what is learned, what is retrieved, and what is manually designed. Is the “knowledge brain” a parametric model, a database, or a hybrid? I might be wrong, but the paper seems to use these terms somewhat interchangeably. The description on p. 6, lines 184–213, did not clear this up for me.

The empirical section is where I really struggled. Table 1 and Table 2 report gains on inflation/employment simulation accuracy, but the baselines are weakly specified. What exactly is the “existing framework” being surpassed? A plain LLM agent? A classical ABM? Some hand-crafted simulator? These are very different comparisons. Also, if real-time economic data are integrated, then part of the gain may simply come from feeding more relevant information, not from preference modeling. The ablations in Table 3 are a start, but they are not enough to isolate the contribution of reflection vs memory vs preference weighting. Figure 4 looks nice, but visual agreement with macro trends is not enough.

There is an interesting paper hiding in here. I do think the authors are aiming at an important problem. But right now it reads like a broad systems pitch with a lot of ambitious language and not enough rigor. My recommendation is weak reject. If they can sharpen the definition of preference, make the method reproducible, and run much cleaner baselines/ablations, I’d be much more positive.

132.review_text:
I found this paper ambitious and, honestly, a bit uneven. The goal — to give an information-theoretic account of SSL objectives and explain why generalization emerges — is important, and I appreciate that the authors are trying to unify a lot of disparate methods rather than just proving one narrow theorem. There is something valuable here. In particular, the discussion around deterministic networks early in the paper (around pp. 3–5, especially the transition into Eq. (4)/(5)) is interesting, because the standard “inject noise everywhere and call it mutual information” trick has always felt unsatisfactory. I’ve complained about that myself in reviews before.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not convinced the paper fully delivers on its central claims. The derivation of the IT quantities for deterministic networks is presented as a major conceptual advance, but I’m not entirely sure the object being optimized is always as meaningful as the paper suggests. Some of the assumptions seem to move the difficulty around rather than resolve it. I might be wrong, but the argument near p. 6, where the authors connect their formulation back to standard SSL losses, felt too slick. It “rediscovers” a family of methods, yes, but only after choosing assumptions that seem fairly tailored. So the unification is interesting, but perhaps less first-principles than advertised.

I also struggled with the generalization bound section. The theorem itself (I think this is the main result around pp. 8–9; sorry if I’m misnumbering) is mathematically clean, but the practical meaning is murky. Bounds of this kind are often vacuous, and I did not see enough discussion of tightness or when the bound would rank methods in a way that correlates with reality. Table 1 helps a bit, but not enough. Figure 2 is conceptually useful, though.

The paper is also written in a somewhat grandiose style. Claims like “optimality” and broad guidance to practitioners feel overstated relative to what is actually proven. In my experience, theory papers on SSL live or die by how honest they are about assumptions, and here I wanted more humility. On the other hand, I do think the paper has real intellectual substance, and it is more thoughtful than the average “information bottleneck for everything” submission.

Overall: borderline for me. Interesting ideas, some genuine insight, but the theory-to-practice connection and the exact novelty of the deterministic-network treatment need to be sharpened considerably. If accepted, I’d want the authors to tone down the claims and clarify the assumptions line by line.

133.review_text:
I went into this somewhat skeptical because “ControlNet for AR models” is an obvious enough idea that the main question is whether there is actually a technically meaningful contribution beyond repackaging conditioning. After reading the paper twice, I think there is something real here, but the paper also overclaims in a few places.

The central idea—using conditional decoding with per-token fusion instead of simply prefilling control tokens—makes sense, and the comparison on p. 4–5 is actually one of the more useful parts of the paper. I appreciated that the authors did not just say prefilling is bad in theory, but tried to show both quality and efficiency differences. Figure 2 is clear. In my experience with AR image models, this kind of token-budget issue is very real, so the motivation is credible. The lightweight control encoder also seems sensible rather than overengineered.

That said, I found the exact mechanism a bit underexplained. Equation (3) and the surrounding text (I think around p. 5, left column) describe the fusion as “similar to positional encodings,” but this analogy is too hand-wavy. Is the control signal additive, concatenative, projected into key/value only, or something else in each block? I might be missing it, but the implementation details seem scattered. This matters because the claimed efficiency gains depend strongly on where and how the control enters the transformer.

The empirical section is mixed for me. The qualitative results are good; some samples in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 are genuinely impressive, especially under edge and depth conditioning. But the claim in the abstract that ControlAR “surpasses” strong controllable diffusion baselines like ControlNet++ needs more care. Table 1/Table 2 (assuming I read them correctly) show wins on some metrics, but these benchmarks are notoriously sensitive, and I did not see enough discussion of sampling budget, guidance settings, or how much tuning the diffusion baselines received. Frankly, these comparisons often end up unfair. If the AR model is evaluated at a different compute/latency point, say so plainly.

The arbitrary-resolution claim is interesting but also the part I trust the least. The examples look decent, yes, but I wanted more systematic evidence, not just a few cherry-picked visuals. Some ablation on resolution extrapolation failure cases would help. Right now it feels a bit “surprisingly” because the authors were surprised too.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with a useful idea, but the writing needs tightening and some of the experimental claims should be toned down. Borderline accept for me, leaning positive.

134.review_text:
I found the core idea quite sensible, and honestly a bit overdue for tabular SSL. Predicting bin IDs instead of raw feature values is simple, but in this space simple inductive biases often matter more than fancy machinery. After reading the paper twice, I think the main contribution is less “a new pretext task” in the grand sense and more a very practical reformulation of masked feature reconstruction that better matches tabular structure. That is valuable.

The motivation on p.2–3 is mostly convincing, especially the discussion around heterogeneous targets and piecewise-constant behavior. I liked Fig. 1 conceptually, though I think it oversells the smooth-vs-irregular distinction a little. Eq. (3) and the surrounding text make the method look cleaner than it probably is in practice: a lot depends on how the bins are chosen. This is where I wanted much more detail. The paper says binning can provide “ordering information,” but I’m not entirely sure the loss actually exploits order unless you use the ordinal variant; otherwise it is just classification over discretized targets. That distinction matters and felt blurred in Sec. 3.2.

The empirical section is solid but not fully airtight. Table 2 and Table 3 show fairly consistent gains, and I appreciate that the authors compare against multiple encoder backbones rather than baking the method into one architecture. Still, I was missing a stronger ablation on the number of bins and binning strategy (equal-width vs quantile vs learned). The brief study in Table 5 is not enough. In my experience, tabular results can swing a lot based on preprocessing, and binning is preprocessing whether the paper wants to frame it that way or not. I also wondered about sensitivity across datasets with very different sample sizes; some of the gains look concentrated on a subset.

Another issue: the novelty is moderate. There is a long history of discretization/binning in tabular ML, and masked reconstruction over discretized targets is not a huge conceptual leap. So for me the paper stands or falls on whether the experimental evidence is strong enough. It is fairly strong, but I would have liked more statistical reporting (variance/significance) and a clearer explanation of why this is better than simply treating all features as categorical after standard preprocessing.

Overall, I lean positive. The method is clean, plausible, and likely useful. But the paper should be more careful not to overclaim theoretical depth where the real contribution is empirical robustness and a good inductive bias.

135.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, a bit more ambitious than most quantization-for-diffusion submissions I’ve seen lately. The main idea—bringing additive vector quantization/codebook methods over from the recent LLM quantization literature into diffusion models—does feel novel enough for this venue. In particular, the claim around sub-1.5 stored bits/weight with usable image quality is striking. If the numbers in Table 1/2 are solid, this is a meaningful result.

What I liked most is that the paper seems to actually grapple with diffusion-specific annoyances rather than just rebranding an LLM method. The discussion around convolutional tensor structure and heterogeneous layer behavior (I think this is around Sec. 3, maybe p. 4–5) is useful, and the note about “momentum-invalidating discontinuities” during QAT was one of the more original technical observations in the paper. That said, I wanted this part to be much clearer. After reading it twice, I still wasn’t fully sure whether the optimizer fix is essential to the headline results or just a stabilizer. Eq. (6)–(8) are written compactly, but the intuition is underexplained.

I also have some reservations. The paper leans hard on “data-free distillation,” but the setup/details are not fully transparent. What exactly is available during quantization/fine-tuning? On p. 6, the wording made me wonder whether class-conditioning prompts/statistics from the original training distribution are still indirectly used. Maybe I’m being overly suspicious, but for compression papers these details matter a lot. I would also have liked a very plain accounting of actual memory savings and wall-clock speed. The abstract talks about inference burden, but most of the results are quality metrics (FID/sFID/ISC). Figure 3 suggests some runtime tradeoff, but I couldn’t tell if the proposed VQ machinery is actually practical at inference or if the gains are mostly storage-side.

Another issue: comparisons. The empirical section is decent, but I think it is a bit too optimized around the authors’ preferred setting (LDM-4, ImageNet, 20 steps). I’m not entirely sure how robust this is outside that exact regime. A small experiment on another diffusion backbone, or even a step-count sensitivity table, would have helped. Also, the paper occasionally overclaims “state of the art” without enough caveats; in my experience, quantization baselines are notoriously fragile to evaluation protocol.

Overall, I’m positive. The paper has real novelty and the results appear strong, especially at W2A8 and below. But the presentation is uneven, and some crucial experimental details need to be nailed down before I’d feel completely comfortable. Tentative accept.

136.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and honestly more interesting than I expected from the title, which at first sounded a bit gimmicky. The core idea—using LM-generated task insights to post-process pretrained embeddings without any labels—is clever, and in a weird way kind of obvious in hindsight. I mean that as a compliment. After reading the method section twice, I think I understand the intended mechanism: extract “useful” and “harmful” semantic directions from task descriptions, then reweight the embedding space accordingly. The high-level pitch is clean.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper is as solid as the abstract suggests. The theoretical model (around Eq. 3–6, if I’m tracking correctly) is very stylized. I get why the authors did this—it’s probably the only way to say anything nontrivial—but the assumptions felt pretty disconnected from modern embedding geometries. The result is fine as intuition, but I would not oversell it as characterizing when the method works in practice. In fact, the theorem-to-experiment bridge felt shaky.

The empirical section is stronger. Table 1 and Table 2 show pretty substantial gains, and 15.98% average improvement is not trivial. I especially liked that they tested across both vision and NLP tasks rather than staying in one sandbox. Figure 3 was useful too. But I had a recurring concern: how much of the gain is really from “robustification” versus just better prompt/task-description engineering via the LM? The paper says zero-shot, yes, but there is still a lot of external information entering through the LM. Maybe that’s acceptable, maybe even the point, but then the framing should be more explicit. Right now it sometimes reads as if the method is modifying the pretrained model in a vacuum, which it definitely is not.

I also found parts of the method underspecified. On page 5 (lines ~180–210, I think), the procedure for selecting or aggregating the LM-generated insights was a bit hand-wavy. How sensitive is RoboShot to wording, number of generated insights, decoding randomness, etc.? In my experience, these pipelines can be brittle, and I didn’t see enough variance/error analysis.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is novel, practical, and the results are quite good. But I’m not at the “strong accept” level. The paper needs more honesty about what source of supervision is being used, and more robustness analysis of the LM component. If those issues were tightened up, this would be much easier to endorse.

138.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and also a bit frustrating. The core idea—learning eigenfunctions of symmetric operators with a neural network and stochastic optimization—is genuinely appealing, and it connects a few communities that usually do not talk to each other very clearly. The link between SFA, variational methods from physics, and modern deep learning is, in my opinion, the main contribution here. I’ve seen a lot of papers gesture at this kind of “unification,” but this one at least has some substance.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I still think the presentation is harder than it needs to be. The bilevel optimization formulation around Eq. (6)–(9) is central, but the intuition is buried. I think the authors understand it very well; I’m less convinced the reader will. In particular, the role of the moving covariance / whitening estimates in the online learning of multiple eigenfunctions is crucial, but the assumptions under which this is stable are not discussed carefully enough. I might be wrong, but it seems the method could be quite sensitive to minibatch noise and eigenvalue spacing. If two eigenvalues are close, what exactly prevents the learned functions from drifting within the eigenspace? This is only partially addressed around the orthogonality discussion on p. 4.

The experiments are a mixed bag. The quantum mechanics examples are nice and honestly the clearest part of the paper; Figure 2 makes the objective feel concrete. But the video experiments felt underpowered. The claims about “interpretable representations” are stronger than what I think the evidence supports. Table 1 is fairly limited, and the synthetic video setup is too clean. In 2010 this would have been more convincing; by current standards I wanted either stronger baselines or a more realistic benchmark. Why not compare more directly to deep SFA variants or contemporary unsupervised sequence models? The paper mentions SFA repeatedly, but the empirical comparison is surprisingly thin.

I did like the operator-centric viewpoint. In my experience, papers that make spectral methods trainable at scale tend to matter even if the first version is somewhat rough. Figure 1 is helpful, though I wish the notation matched the algorithm box more tightly. Also, there are a few places where the writing is sloppy—e.g., the transition from the operator objective to the stochastic estimator on p. 3 felt too abrupt.

Overall, I lean positive because the idea is novel and technically meaningful, but I’m not fully sold on the empirical depth. This feels like a good workshop paper that is close to being a strong conference paper. If accepted, I would want the authors to tighten the exposition and be more modest in the video claims.

137.review_text:
I found this paper interesting and, honestly, quite useful. There are lots of continual learning papers, and many implicitly assume feedforward settings; here the authors actually do the somewhat unglamorous but important job of checking what survives in RNNs. In my experience, these “evaluation + interpretation” papers can be more valuable than another tiny algorithmic tweak, and this one mostly justifies itself on that basis.

What I liked most is the central claim that the issue for weight-importance methods in RNNs is not really sequence length per se, but working-memory demand. That distinction is easy to say and harder to demonstrate. I think the experiments around the sequential benchmarks make a reasonably convincing case, especially when read together with the linear-RNN analysis later in the paper (I believe around Sec. 4 / equations in that section). The discussion around why repeated reuse of recurrent weights changes the stability/plasticity tradeoff felt thoughtful rather than hand-wavy. Figure 3 and Figure 4 were particularly helpful to me; after reading this twice, I think those are really the core of the paper.

That said, I do have some reservations. First, the “first comprehensive evaluation” claim in the abstract is a bit too strong. Maybe it is true under some narrow definition, but I would tone that down. Second, I’m not entirely sure the benchmark suite is as diverse as the paper suggests. There are several task constructions here, but I kept wondering how much of the story depends on synthetic memory-heavy setups versus more realistic sequential domains. Table 1 gives the impression of breadth, but some of these are variants of the same stress test. I would have liked at least one messier real-world sequence problem where recurrence is genuinely needed, not just convenient.

I also found the presentation uneven. Some implementation details are buried; for example, I had to hunt around the experimental section and appendix to understand exactly how hyperparameters were selected across methods. For CL comparisons this matters a lot, and I’m always wary when hypernetwork-based methods win unless tuning fairness is crystal clear. Maybe I missed it, but the protocol should be more explicit in the main text. Also, the theoretical section is nice, but the connection from the linear analysis back to the nonlinear gated RNNs is a little too neat. I buy it as intuition, not as strong evidence.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper and likely worth accepting. It is not flashy, and parts of it are slightly overclaimed, but the empirical message is clear and relevant. I’d recommend acceptance with some polishing, especially around claims of comprehensiveness and experimental transparency.

139.review_text:
I found the paper reasonably well-motivated. Split learning is getting used all over the place, and it is true that the theory side is much thinner than for FL. So the problem is worth studying, and I appreciated that the authors try to connect the theorem to practical questions like hyperparameter choice rather than just proving yet another generic nonconvex rate.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not convinced the technical contribution is as strong as the abstract makes it sound. The main theorem around the \(\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})\) rate (I believe this is Theorem 1 on p. 5, around Eq. (12)–(14)) is basically the standard nonconvex SGD-type result with additional terms to account for split training and non-IIDness. I might be wrong, but I did not see a genuinely new proof idea here. The assumptions also feel quite forgiving: smoothness, bounded variance, and the heterogeneity control term are all standard, and in some places a bit hidden in the notation. In particular, the quantity measuring client drift/non-IID effect introduced near p. 4 (around lines 150–170) seems crucial, but the paper does not really explain how large it is in realistic SL deployments. So yes, there is a theorem, but I’m not sure how informative it is beyond “SL behaves like SGD plus heterogeneity error.”

I was also confused by the comparison to FL. The claim in the abstract that SL “can outperform FL in terms of convergence rate” feels too broad. The comparison seems partly theoretical and partly empirical, but not on equal footing. Table 1 and Fig. 3/4 suggest SL uses fewer per-client computations, sure, but wall-clock, server bottleneck, and activation communication are not really accounted for. In my experience, SL often looks good only if one ignores these system costs. The paper sort of acknowledges this, then still leans on the stronger claim.

The experiments are okay but not especially convincing. The “mildly” vs “highly” non-IID distinction in Fig. 2 is intuitive, yet the setups are limited, and I would have liked at least one larger-scale benchmark or ablation on split point choice. Also, the theory suggests dependence on certain hyperparameters, but the empirical section does not really stress-test that. That feels like a missed opportunity.

Overall, I think this is a decent workshop-style paper, but for a top ML venue I wanted either sharper theory or more careful experimental validation. Right now it lands in an awkward middle ground: not wrong, not trivial, but not quite compelling enough. My leaning is weak reject.

140.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and the core idea is actually pretty natural in hindsight: if sliced OT gives you many 1D couplings, can you “lift” and average them into something that is genuinely a coupling in the original space? That is a sensible question, and I think the paper does answer it in a mathematically nontrivial way. The construction around the lifting operator and the expected sliced transport plan is, to me, the main contribution. I also appreciated the attempt to go beyond just “here is a plan” and ask whether the induced cost is a metric. That said, I’m less convinced by the overall impact than by the technical neatness.

After reading Sections 3 and 4 twice, I think the definitions are mostly sound, but the exposition is harder than it needs to be. In particular, the lifting construction around Eq. (6)–(8) (if I am tracking correctly) felt overly compressed. I had to jump back and forth to understand what object lives on the projected line and what object lives back on the ambient space. Figure 1 helps a bit, but honestly not enough. A more explicit toy example with 3–4 points would have helped a lot.

The theoretical result that the EST-weighted Euclidean cost defines a metric is nice, and probably the strongest reason to accept the paper. Still, I’m not entirely sure the practical significance is established. Table 1 and the numerical examples later on are fairly lightweight. They illustrate behavior, yes, but they do not really convince me that EST plans are useful in realistic ML settings where one actually needs couplings. The paper repeatedly motivates applications requiring explicit transport plans, but then does not really test one. This is a bit frustrating. In my experience, papers in OT live or die by whether the proposed object is only elegant or also usable.

I also think the connection to min-SWGG is somewhat underdeveloped. The statement is interesting, but it reads a bit like “by the way, here is a relation to recent work” rather than a fully integrated comparison. I would have liked a sharper discussion of when EST coincides with, upper bounds, or differs qualitatively from min-SWGG, maybe around the proposition on page 7.

Overall, I lean positive on novelty and correctness, but only mildly so. It’s a thoughtful theory paper, though a somewhat dry one, and the empirical side is too thin for a top conference unless the bar for theory submissions is explicitly high and application evidence is not expected. My score would be around borderline accept / weak accept.

141.review_text:
I went into this a bit skeptical because “make REDQ cheaper with dropout” sounds almost too straightforward, but after reading the paper twice I think the core idea is actually pretty sensible. The paper is easy to follow overall, and I appreciated that the claim is narrow: not “we solve offline RL” or “we beat everything,” but specifically that a small ensemble plus dropout/layer norm can recover much of REDQ’s sample efficiency without the ugly compute cost. That is a worthwhile question.

The empirical story is mostly convincing. The main plots/tables (especially Table 1 and the runtime/sample-efficiency comparisons around the experimental section) support the central claim reasonably well. I also liked that the authors compare against both REDQ and SAC rather than only against the stronger-but-slower baseline. In my experience, papers in this space often hide behind normalized scores and avoid wall-clock numbers, so I’m glad this one does not. Figure 1 (if I’m reading it right) makes the tradeoff pretty clear.

That said, I’m not fully convinced the paper really explains why this works. The method section felt a little thin. Around the equations defining the dropout Q-functions and target computation (I think Eq. 4–6), the paper reads more like “here is the implementation recipe” than an actual argument. Is dropout acting as approximate ensembling? Is the gain mostly from regularization? Is layer norm doing more of the heavy lifting than the title suggests? I might be wrong, but the ablation around dropout rate / ensemble size / no-layernorm did not feel deep enough to disentangle these effects. Right now the method seems empirically useful but conceptually under-explained.

I also found some fairness issues in the compute comparison a bit under-discussed. Wall-clock comparisons in RL are notoriously fragile: hardware, batching, implementation details, number of gradient steps, and parallelism matter a lot. The paper does mention setup, but not with the level of care I would have liked. If REDQ is implemented less aggressively than DroQ, the whole “doubly efficient” pitch weakens. Maybe this is addressed in the appendix, but it should be more front-and-center.

One more thing: the benchmark scope is standard MuJoCo-ish continuous control. Fine, but also a bit tired. For a paper whose contribution is mostly practical efficiency, I would have liked at least one harder setting where compute really matters, or some sensitivity study showing robustness across seeds beyond the usual handful.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper and probably accept-worthy, though not a slam dunk. The idea is simple in a good way, the results are useful, and practitioners will likely try this. But I do think the paper oversells the “doubly efficient” narrative a little and undersells the fact that this is, at heart, a smart engineering variant rather than a deeply new RL algorithm.

142.review_text:
I found the paper interesting but also a bit frustrating. The core idea — parameterizing a spatio-temporal Hawkes-style influence kernel with a deep low-rank non-stationary decomposition — is potentially useful, and I do think the paper is trying to solve a real limitation of standard stationary kernels and also of some RNN-based point process models. Figure 1 gives the right intuition, and the decomposition around Eq. (6) is probably the main technical contribution. In spirit, this feels like a reasonable next step beyond neural kernel parametrizations I’ve seen in the last few years.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the method is as novel or as clean as the paper claims. The low-rank factorization seems like a fairly natural tensor/product decomposition with neural parameterization, and I wanted a more precise discussion of what is actually new relative to prior neural Hawkes/kernel learning papers. The related work section felt thin on that point. I might be missing something, but the distinction from existing deep kernel parameterizations is not crisply made.

I also had trouble with the log-barrier treatment for non-negativity (around the objective, I think Eq. (11) or nearby). This is an unusual choice here. It may work in practice, but the paper does not really explain optimization stability, sensitivity to the barrier coefficient, or what happens near the boundary. In my experience these details matter a lot more than authors expect. The text claims computational efficiency, but Table 2 only partially supports that: yes, the model is faster than some baselines, but there are also missing implementation details, and I could not tell whether all methods had been equally tuned.

The experiments are decent but not totally persuasive. The synthetic results are okay, though somewhat favorable to the proposed factorized setting. On the real data side, I appreciated the inclusion of spatio-temporal benchmarks, but I wanted more ablations: rank choice, effect of depth, effect of the barrier term, and maybe a simpler stationary low-rank baseline. Figure 3 looks nice, but it’s more qualitative than diagnostic. Also, the paper repeatedly says “better performance” without enough statistical context. Are these gains robust across seeds? Hard to tell.

Overall, I think there is a solid idea here, and the paper is not bad. But right now it feels a bit under-explained and slightly over-claimed. I lean weak reject unless the authors can better clarify novelty and shore up the empirical case.

143.review_text:
I read this paper with some interest because the problem setting is important and, frankly, a lot of recent “offline-to-online” RL papers make very similar claims without being very clear about what is actually new. My overall reaction here is mixed, leaning skeptical.

The basic idea — treat offline and online data differently in the Q update, with pessimism on the offline part and less/no pessimism on the online part — is sensible. In fact, it is so sensible that I kept wondering what exactly the technical novelty is beyond a fairly direct combination of existing offline RL conservatism with standard online fine-tuning. After reading Section 3 twice, I still found the contribution somewhat blurry. Eq. (4) and the adaptive weighting around the two data sources seem to be the core, but the paper does not really convince me that this is more than a heuristic design choice. Maybe I missed something, but the theory in the later part (I think around the initialization discussion, page 6?) felt disconnected from the main algorithmic claim.

I also had trouble with the writing. Figure 1 gives the high-level picture, but the distinction between “greedy” and “no pessimistic” update for online data is not made precise enough early on. On page 4, the algorithm description is too compressed; I had to jump back and forth between the text and pseudocode to understand when the pessimism term is active. Table 1 reports strong gains, especially with random datasets, and honestly that is the point where I became a bit suspicious. Getting near-expert performance with very limited online interaction starting from random offline data is a strong claim. It may be true in the chosen benchmarks, but I would want a much more careful accounting of interaction budget, variance across seeds, and sensitivity to the offline dataset size/coverage. The experimental section feels a little too eager to sell the method.

To be fair, I do think the empirical trend is plausible, and I liked that the paper explicitly discusses policy evaluation under the offline setting rather than pretending it is solved. There is some practical value here. But in my experience reviewing RL papers, this bar is not enough if the novelty is incremental and the exposition is not crisp.

So: decent idea, relevant setting, but I’m not entirely convinced by the novelty or by the experimental claims as currently presented. I would probably argue for rejection unless the authors can clarify the precise technical distinction from prior offline-to-online RL methods and tighten the evidence substantially.

144.review_text:
I have fairly mixed feelings about this paper. I like the general direction a lot — MARL really does suffer from toy environments, and in my experience many papers “solve” benchmarks that are so abstract they tell us almost nothing about deployment. So a more realistic, physics-based, Unreal-based platform could be useful. The open-source angle also matters. That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m not convinced the submission is yet at the level of a top conference paper rather than a solid systems/demo paper.

The main issue is that the paper makes very broad claims (“authenticity”, “sim-to-real potential”, “high extensibility”) but the evidence is thinner than I expected. The environment description around pp. 3–5 gives a decent overview, and Fig. 2 is helpful, but I kept wanting more concrete technical detail. For example, what exactly is the physics stack configuration, what runs server-side vs client-side, how deterministic/reproducible are rollouts, and what are the performance numbers under scale? Table 1 gives some task stats, but not the sort of engineering characterization I would want for a platform paper.

The experimental section also feels underdeveloped. The benchmark tasks are interesting on paper — heterogeneous agents, multiple teams, sparse rewards — but I’m not entirely sure the evaluation cleanly demonstrates that UMAP reveals new algorithmic behavior rather than just making optimization harder. The results tables (I think Tables 2 and 3) mostly show that existing MARL methods struggle, which is believable, but not yet very informative. I would have liked stronger ablations on observation design, reward sparsity, number of agents, and perhaps comparisons to existing environments like MAgent2, Melting Pot, or even some of the more realistic robotics/game-inspired simulators. Right now the paper risks reading as “our environment is harder, therefore better.” That’s not enough.

I was also confused by the sim-to-real claim near the end. The physical experiment is interesting, yes, but from the description on p. 8 it seems quite limited. I might be wrong, but it looked more like a proof-of-concept than genuine transfer evidence. If so, I’d rather the paper be more modest there.

One more small thing: the writing needs cleanup. There are a number of awkward phrases and overclaims in the abstract/introduction, and some implementation details are buried while marketing language is foregrounded.

Overall, I think this is promising and potentially useful to the community, but the paper currently undersells the engineering specifics and oversells the empirical validation. Borderline reject for me, though I’d be happy to see a stronger revision.

145.review_text:
I like the basic idea here, and I do think the paper is tackling a real problem rather than a toy one. Learning useful policies from messy play data is genuinely hard, and the community has maybe been a bit too optimistic about just “scaling up” offline sequence models in robotics. This paper at least engages with that messiness directly. The combination of BeT-style multimodal action prediction with future conditioning is sensible, and in Figure 1 / the early method section (around p. 3) the high-level story is easy to follow.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced the contribution is as substantial as the framing suggests. The method felt more like a fairly natural combination of existing ingredients than a sharply new algorithm. Equation (1) and the discussion around it on p. 4 mostly instantiate a conditional version of an existing behavior transformer setup, and I kept asking myself: is the gain coming from the multimodal head, from the future-goal conditioning, or just from better training details / architecture scale? Table 1 shows the aggregate improvement, which is nice, but I really wanted a cleaner ablation disentangling these components. The current ablations are not quite enough for me.

I also found the evaluation a bit slippery. The “average 45.7% improvement” in the abstract sounds impressive, but averages over benchmark tasks can hide a lot. Looking at the per-task results in Table 2, the gains seem uneven, and on some tasks the margins are not huge. Maybe I’m missing something, but I would have liked confidence intervals or at least multiple seeds reported more systematically. In my experience with offline RL / imitation from heterogeneous robot data, variance can be brutal, especially when future-conditioning is involved.

The real-robot section is probably the most exciting part, but also the least rigorously presented. The qualitative results in Figure 5 are encouraging, yes, but the paper leans heavily on videos. I get why — robotics papers do this all the time — but for a top venue I wanted more quantitative evaluation, even if approximate. How many trials? What counts as success exactly? The appendix may contain some of this, but it should not be buried.

I’m also not entirely sure the comparison set is fully fair. The related work and baselines on p. 2–3 discuss prior play-data methods, but there is not enough discussion of whether they were tuned comparably for the same visual/state inputs and data regime.

Overall, I think this is a solid paper with an important problem setup and some genuinely promising results, especially on real hardware. But I’m a bit wary of the novelty claim and the somewhat under-analyzed empirical story. Lean accept / borderline for me.

146.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, but also somewhat frustrating. The core idea — learn causal graph induction end-to-end as a supervised mapping from datasets to graphs — is not crazy at all, and in fact probably overdue given how much of the recent literature still wraps neural components around essentially classical search/scoring pipelines. I appreciated that the authors are explicit about treating inference as a black box rather than pretending to derive a new identifiability result. That honesty is refreshing.

That said, after reading the paper twice, I’m still not fully convinced by the empirical story. The main claim in the abstract is “state-of-the-art performance on naturalistic graphs for low sample complexity,” but the evidence for this felt a bit selective. Table 1 and Table 2 look good for the proposed model, yes, especially in the small-sample regime, but I wanted a clearer breakdown of where the gains are actually coming from: the architecture, the synthetic training distribution, or simply the fact that the model has seen a huge number of supervised graph/data pairs. In my experience, these learned inference papers often hide a lot of problem-specific prior in the simulator. The discussion around train-test shift on page 6 is promising, but still limited; the shifts considered seem fairly mild.

I also found the treatment of interventions a bit confusing. Around Eq. (3) / the surrounding text on page 4, the representation of interventional data is introduced, but it is not entirely clear to me whether the model is learning intervention targets as part of the input encoding or assuming they are known and cleanly specified. This matters a lot. Figure 2 helps with the architecture, but I wish the data format were illustrated more concretely. Maybe I’m missing something, but the setup seems easier than many realistic causal discovery settings.

Another issue is that the paper leans heavily on synthetic supervision while saying relatively little about why generalization should hold beyond the graph families used in training. The naturalistic graph benchmark is a good step, but not enough. I would have liked at least one stronger stress test with misspecified functional forms or noisy/partial interventions. Right now the paper feels a bit too “benchmark-optimized.”

Still, I think there is a real contribution here. The results are strong enough that I wouldn’t dismiss the work, and I suspect people will build on it. But I’m not yet persuaded this is a robust causal induction method rather than a very effective amortized solver for the authors’ simulator. Weak accept / borderline for me.

148.review_text:
I found the paper interesting, and I do think the basic motivation is timely: a lot of recent SNN work talks about “energy efficiency” in a fairly hand-wavy way, while models have quietly become larger and denser. So I appreciate a paper that actually tries to optimize for energy rather than only top-1 accuracy. The idea of combining unstructured weight pruning with neuron pruning is also, at least at a high level, sensible.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m not fully convinced by the technical novelty or by the experimental evidence as currently presented. The main claim seems to be that this is the first use of unstructured neuron pruning for deep SNNs, but the distinction between “neuron pruning” and simply inducing activity sparsity / removing inactive units is not made sharply enough. On p.3 around the method overview, and later near Eq. (6)–(8), I kept wanting a cleaner formalization of what exactly is being pruned, when, and according to what criterion. Is the neuron pruning based on average firing rate, membrane potential statistics, saliency, or some proxy for SOP reduction? I might be missing it, but the exposition is muddy.

The energy story is also weaker than the abstract suggests. Table 2 reports SOP reductions and then translates these into “91x energy efficiency,” but this is not the same as measured energy on hardware. In my experience reviewing SNN papers, this distinction matters a lot. If the claim is about neuromorphic deployment, I would really like at least some hardware-backed estimate, or at minimum a more careful discussion of the assumptions behind the SOP-to-energy conversion. Figure 4 is visually nice, but it mostly reinforces the sparsity narrative rather than validating actual system-level gains.

I also thought the empirical section was too narrow. CIFAR-10 alone is not enough here, especially for a pruning paper. Where is CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet, or at least DVS-CIFAR10? And the baselines in Table 1 felt selective. There should be comparison to stronger pruning/sparsification methods adapted to SNNs, not just generic dense baselines. Without that, the result “0.63% remaining connections with 2.19% accuracy drop” sounds impressive but is hard to calibrate.

One more thing: the paper repeatedly emphasizes “unstructured” sparsity as if neuromorphic hardware will automatically exploit it. Sometimes yes, sometimes absolutely not. This is a pretty big practical caveat and it’s underplayed.

Overall I think there is a promising idea here, but the paper currently overclaims. I’m leaning borderline negative unless the authors can clarify the pruning mechanism and strengthen the evaluation substantially.

147.review_text:
I found this paper interesting, and honestly a bit more thought-provoking than the average “yet another faster diffusion variant” submission. The core idea—using the deterministic probability-flow correspondence to define line matching between source and target samples—is neat, and the empirical results are strong enough that I took it seriously. Table 1 and Table 2 are hard to ignore: the reported FID/NFE tradeoff is excellent, especially the 1–2 step regime. If these numbers hold under standard evaluation, this is a very competitive result.

That said, I had to work to understand what exactly is new versus repackaged from flow matching / rectified flow style ideas. On a first pass, the paper felt like “straight trajectories are good” plus “use a better pairing induced by an ODE model,” which is reasonable, but the novelty over existing straightening-based methods was not always crisply articulated. I think the key distinction is in the induced pairing, but this point is buried a bit. The discussion around the singularity/degeneracy in the denoising objective (around Eq. 4–6, if I’m reading correctly) is intriguing, but I’m not entirely sure the paper fully nails down when this pathology occurs in practice versus in a stylized analysis. I would have liked a cleaner statement of assumptions there.

I also found Figure 2 helpful, while Figure 3 was less convincing than the authors probably intend. The trajectories do look straighter, yes, but straightness alone is not the goal, and I wanted a stronger causal story connecting that metric to actual sample quality and one-step robustness. The ablations on reconstruction/adversarial losses are useful, though a little too “kitchen sink” for my taste: once those are added, it becomes harder to isolate what LMM itself is buying you.

The OT curse-of-dimensionality section near the end is probably true in spirit, and I appreciated that the authors addressed it, but I’m slightly skeptical about how much practical guidance it gives. It reads a bit like a theorem included to preempt obvious alternatives rather than something central to the method.

Overall I lean positive. The paper has real ideas and very strong results, even if the exposition is uneven and some claims are a touch over-sold. I think this will be influential if cleaned up. My main request would be to sharpen the positioning relative to flow matching / rectified flow papers and make the singularity argument less hand-wavy.

149.review_text:
I like the goal of this paper quite a bit. Public datasets for ML-for-EDA are badly needed, and in my experience this is one of the main reasons the literature keeps recycling toy settings. So I was positively inclined going in. CircuitNet 2.0 does seem materially more realistic than a lot of prior benchmark efforts, especially with full commercial flows and 14nm FinFET rather than some sanitized academic proxy. The scale claim (>10,000 samples) is also meaningful.

That said, after reading this twice, I’m still not fully convinced the paper gives enough detail for the community to really understand what is new relative to CircuitNet 1.0 and what exactly is being released. The high-level message is clear, but the specifics feel slippery. On p.3, where the dataset composition is described, I wanted a much more explicit breakdown by design family, variation source, and split construction. Figure 2 gives the pipeline, but not the actual statistical diversity. Table 1 helps a bit, though I found it oddly hard to infer whether the 10,000 samples correspond to fundamentally different netlists / floorplans / constraints, or many perturbations of a smaller base set. That distinction matters a lot for ML evaluation.

I also think the “realistic tasks” section is underdeveloped. The paper says these tasks verify the dataset’s potential, but that is a weak standard. Potential is not the same as utility. If I understood correctly, the baselines on timing/power/routability are mostly there to show feasibility, but the experimental section (around p.6–7) felt more like a dataset demo than a rigorous benchmark. I’m not necessarily asking for state-of-the-art modeling, but I do expect stronger protocols: cross-design generalization, leakage checks, and some ablation on which modalities are actually informative. Otherwise, it’s too easy for future papers to overfit to artifacts in the flow.

Another issue: reproducibility. I might be wrong, but I could not tell whether all raw intermediate representations are being released, or only processed tensors/images. This is a big deal. On p.5 the wording around commercial EDA outputs is a bit vague, and I worry that licensing constraints may limit practical usability. If so, the paper should be more upfront.

Still, I think the contribution is real. The community needs infrastructure papers like this, and the authors have clearly put in a lot of effort. My bottom line is positive but not enthusiastic: good idea, probably useful dataset, but the paper needs sharper documentation and more careful benchmarking before I’d call it a definitive resource.

150.review_text:
I found the paper interesting and, honestly, more ambitious than most “large-scale MARL” submissions I’ve seen recently. The central idea—replacing explicit pairwise interactions with a graph-attention-weighted mean field—is sensible, and the scale in the experiments (especially the >3000 agents claim in the abstract and later sections) is definitely attention-grabbing. In my experience, a lot of papers in this area quietly stop at 50–100 agents, so I appreciate that the authors are at least trying to push beyond that.

That said, I’m not fully convinced by the theoretical story as written. The paper says it “mathematically prove[s] the correctness of this conversion,” but the argument around the mean-field reduction (I think this is around Eq. (4)–(7), page 4) felt much more like a set of assumptions under which the approximation is reasonable, rather than a strong correctness result. Maybe I’m being too picky, but “correctness” is a very strong word here. After reading that section twice, I still wasn’t sure what exact object is being proven equivalent to what. Is it policy optimality? Value approximation consistency? Or just that a weighted average can represent aggregate interaction under some symmetry/exchangeability assumption? Those are very different claims.

I also had some confusion about the role of the attention module. Figure 2 is helpful at a high level, but the actual inputs to the graph attention and whether the graph is learned or induced from proximity/state similarity were not completely clear to me on first pass. The method seems to rely on local neighborhoods, but then the comparison to quadratic interaction modeling is slightly slippery: if neighbors are already pruned by domain structure, the savings are not only from mean-fieldization. This matters for the efficiency claim in Table 2 / Table 3.

The empirical section is decent and fairly broad. I liked that the paper includes both synthetic/manual and “real-world” scenarios. Still, the baselines felt a bit uneven. Some are old, and I’m not entirely sure they were tuned comparably for the large-agent setting. The 9.4x computational efficiency claim is nice, but wall-clock comparisons in MARL are notoriously fragile unless hardware, implementation details, and parallelism are very explicitly controlled. I may have missed it, but I wanted more detail there.

Overall, I think there is a real idea here, and the paper is stronger than average in ambition and practical relevance. But the theory is overstated, and parts of the experimental comparison need tightening.
